## Context
Web research subagents have collected vessel specifications for two datasets:

### HLV Vessels (#1798)
30+ heavy-lift vessels from: Heerema (Thialf, Sleipnir, etc.), Allseas (Pioneering Spirit), Boskalis, Van Oord, Jan De Nul, McDermott (DB series), DEME, and more.

### Pipelay Vessels (#1799)
Pipelay barges and vessels from: Allseas (Solitaire, Pioneering Spirit, Lorelay), McDermott (DB30, DB50, DB101), and others. Data includes tensioner capacities, lay methods, S-lay/J-lay/reel-lay capabilities, water depth ranges.

## Deliverable
Write two JSON files to `digitalmodel/assets/data/`:

1. `vessel_specs_hlv.json` — Array of HLV specs (crane capacity, deck space, DP class, bollard pull, etc.)
2. `vessel_specs_pipelay.json` — Array of pipelay vessel specs (lay method, tensioner capacity, max pipe diameter, water depth range, etc.)

These will be consumed directly by GTM demo scripts (#1800 series).

## Format for HLV
```json
[{"vessel_name": "Thialf", "owner": "Heerema", "crane_capacity_tonnes": 14000, "dp_class": "DP-3", ...}]
```

## Labels
gtm, cat:engineering
title: TASK: Write HLV and pipelay vessel spec JSON files from research data

## Automated compliance monitoring -- cron + alerts

### Current State
- compliance-dashboard.sh works and reports compliance metrics
- Must be run manually to check status
- No automated alerting when compliance drops
- No dashboard visualization

### What Needs to Happen

**Phase 1: Daily Compliance Cron**
Add to crontab:
```
0 6 * * * cd /mnt/local-analysis/workspace-hub && bash scripts/enforcement/compliance-dashboard.sh >> logs/compliance/daily.log 2>&1 || gh issue comment 2012 --body-file logs/compliance/compliance-$(date +\%Y\%m\%d).json
```

**Phase 2: Alert Thresholds**
- Compliance < 50%: Create GitHub issue with details
- Compliance < 30%: Add urgent label, flag for immediate action
- Compliance > 80%: Log as pass, no action needed

**Phase 3: Weekly Compliance Report**
Generate summary report every Monday:
- Week-over-week trend
- Top unreviewed commits

## Publish methodology docs to aceengineer.com for client acquisition

### Background
We have 6 methodology documents in docs/methodology/ but they only live in the repo. Clients can't see them. Publishing them to aceengineer.com turns technical capability into marketing content.

### Available Content (ready to publish)

| Document | Size | Client Value |
|----------|------|-------------|
| compound-engineering.md | 9.7KB | Shows systematic engineering approach |
| enforcement-over-instruction.md | 4KB | Demonstrates quality discipline |
| orchestrator-worker.md | 4.7KB | Shows modern AI-augmented workflow |
| multi-agent-parity.md | ~4KB | Demonstrates tool maturity |
| compliance-dashboard.md | 3.6KB | Shows monitoring/rigor |
| knowledge-to-website-pipeline.md | 4.6KB | Internal (not for publication) |

### Proposed Pages on aceengineer.com

1. **/methodology/compound-engineering** -- The 5-lesson framework
   - Target audience: engineering managers evaluating consultants
   - CTA: "Our approach to every project -- ask us about it"

2. **/methodology/enforcement** -- Why we enforce, not just recommend
   - Target audience: quality assurance teams
   - CTA: "See our review process in action"

3. **/methodology/orchestrator-worker** -- How we separate planning from execution
   - Target audience: technical directors
   - CTA: "Our AI-augmented workflow delivers better results"

4. **/blog/ai-engineering-methodology** -- Blog post version of above

### Implementation Options

**Option A: Static Markdown -> HTML (Recommended)**
- Convert .md to styled HTML
- Use aceengineer.com existing template/theme

- Pros: Fast, version controlled, easy to update

**Option B: Blog Post Series**

- More narrative, less technical
- Better for SEO

- [ ] Branded with ACE Engineer styling

A Python script that:

}
```

body:
## Enable review gate strict mode and add GitHub Actions CI enforcement

### Current State
- require-review-on-push.sh exists but REVIEW_GATE_STRICT defaults to 0 (advisory)
- Pre-push hook calls review gate but only warns
- No GitHub Actions workflow for PR-level enforcement
- Compliance at 20% (improving from 4%)

### What Needs to Happen

**Phase 1: Enable Local Review Gate Strict Mode**
After plan gate is working (#2025), escalate:
```bash
# In .git/hooks/enforcement-env:
export REVIEW_GATE_STRICT=1
```
This will block pushes without cross-review evidence.

**Phase 2: GitHub Actions CI Workflow**
Create `.github/workflows/enforcement-gate.yml`:
```yaml
name: Enforcement Gate
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  check-plan-approval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check Plan Approval
        run: bash scripts/enforcement/require-plan-approval.sh --strict
      - name: Check Review Evidence
        run: bash scripts/enforcement/require-review-on-push.sh HEAD $(git merge-base HEAD origin/main)
  compliance-dashboard:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run Compliance Check
        run: bash scripts/enforcement/compliance-dashboard.sh
```

**Phase 3: Progressive Rollout**
| Week | What | Risk |
|------|------|------|
| 1 | Plan gate strict (local only) | Low -- blocks obvious violations |
| 2 | Review gate strict (local only) | Medium -- may block legitimate work |
| 3 | CI workflow (PR level) | Medium -- external visibility |
| 4 | Full enforcement (all gates) | Low -- everything tested |

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] REVIEW_GATE_STRICT=1 in enforcement-env
- [ ] Push without review evidence is blocked
- [ ] GitHub Actions workflow created and passing
- [ ] PRs without plan approval are blocked by CI
- [ ] Compliance dashboard runs as CI step
- [ ] Bypass logging captures all override attempts

### Related
- #2025 -- Plan gate strict mode and hook wiring
- #1876 -- Enforcement infrastructure (parent)
- #2017 -- Agent bypass resistance (metrics)
- #2012 -- Review backlog audit
title: feat: review gate strict mode + GitHub Actions CI enforcement workflow

body:
## Parent: #2017 — Email-as-Queue Workflow

## Objective

Build the thread state tracking system that powers the "email as queue" model.

## Architecture

### Gmail Labels (visual + API-friendly)

Label hierarchy:
```
wh-email/extracted        — Data pulled, awaiting reply or action
wh-email/awaiting-reply   — You responded, waiting for other party
wh-email/completed        — Topic resolved, marked for deletion
wh-email/noise            — Spam/newsletter, safe to delete immediately
```

### Local State Log (`~/.hermes/email-state.yaml`)

```yaml
# Authoritative state tracker, backed up by Gmail labels
threads:
  - thread_id: "..."
    account: "ace"
    subject: "..."
    sender: "domain"
    state: "awaiting-reply"
    extracted_to: "repo/path/"
    extracted_date: "2026-04-07"
    last_activity: "2026-04-07"
    reply_count: 3
    completed_date: null
    eligible_for_deletion: null
```

### State Machine

```
inbox → extracted (data pulled)
extracted → awaiting-reply (you replied)
awaiting-reply → inbox (they replied, re-activated)
awaiting-reply → completed (topic resolved)
extracted → completed (resolved, no reply needed)
completed → [DELETE after 7 days]
noise → [DELETE immediately]
```

### Grace Period Logic

- `completed` threads: 7-day grace period
- At each cron run, scan for `completed` threads eligible for deletion
- If new unread message arrives during grace period: revert to `inbox`
- Deletion is logged before execution

## Implementation Tasks

1. [ ] Create Gmail labels via API (one-time setup)
2. [ ] Build state log reader/writer
3. [ ] Build state transition functions
4. [ ] Grace period checker (runs in daily cron)
5. [ ] Re-activation checker (scans for new replies on completed threads)
6. [ ] State report command: show all threads, counts by state
7. [ ] Migration: scan existing inbox and label all current emails

## Depends On
- #2017 — workflow design
- #2024 — extraction pipeline (labels threads as extracted)
title: build: Email state tracking system — Gmail labels + local state log + grace period deletion

## Parent: #2017 — Email-as-Queue Workflow

## Objective

Create per-domain extraction templates that define what structured data to pull from each email type, and how to map it to repo locations.

## Template Registry

Create `scripts/email/templates/` with YAML templates per domain:

```yaml
# scripts/email/templates/cre-listings.yaml
domain: sandsig.com|marcusmillichap.com|partnersrealestate.com
account: ace
extraction:
  from_subject:
    cap_rate: "\\d+\\.?\\d*\\s*%?\\s*CAP"
    price: "\\$\\d+(?:,\\d+)*(?:\\.\\d+)?[KMB]?"
    building_sf: "[\\d,]+\\s*SF"
    lease_years: "(\\d+)\\s*(?:Years?|Yr)(?:\\s+Remaining)?"
    state: "\\|\\s*([A-Z]{2})\\s*\\|"
  from_body:
    property_name: null  # need entity to pull from body
    address: null
    tenant_name: null
    broker: null
    noi: null
dest_repo: assethold
dest_path: data/cre-listings/
format: yaml

# scripts/email/templates/client-email.yaml
domain: dorisgroup.com|frontierdeepwater.com|engineeredcustomsolutions.com
account: ace
extraction:
  from_headers:
    sender_name: null  # parse From header
    sender_company: null  # extract domain
  from_subject:
    project_name: null
    deliverable_type: null
  from_body:
    action_items: null
    deadlines: null
dest_repo: aceengineer-admin
dest_path: data/client-{domain}/
format: yaml
```

## Template Types (5)

1. **CRE listings** — Sands IG, Marcus Millichap, LoopNet, Ten-X
   - Structured property data: name, tenant, price, cap rate, SF, state, lease
   - Target: `assethold/data/cre-listings/`

2. **Client/commercial** — Doris, Frontier, ECS, McDermott, etc.
   - Project communications, deliverables, deadlines
   - Target: `aceengineer-admin/data/client-{name}/`

3. **Tenant/property** — Family Dollar, insurance, title, HOA, vendors
   - Maintenance issues, insurance claims, resolutions
   - Target: `sabithaandkrishnaestates/data/{category}/`

4. **Tax/financial** — TurboTax, 1099s, financial statements
   - Tax years, amounts, forms, deadlines
   - Target: `achantas-data/data/tax/`

5. **Invoice/payment** — Vendor invoices, payment confirmations
   - Invoice numbers, amounts, due dates
   - Target: `aceengineer-admin/data/finance/`

## Implementation

1. [ ] Create `scripts/email/templates/` directory
2. [ ] Write template YAML for each domain group (5 templates minimum)
3. [ ] Build template parser that applies regex patterns to headers/body
4. [ ] Integrate with gmail-extract-and-act pipeline
5. [ ] Add validation: template must define at least 3 extraction fields
6. [ ] Test each template against real emails from that domain

## Priority Order

Start with CRE listings (Sands IG) — highest volume, most structured data, easiest to extract.

## Depends On
- #2017 — workflow design
- #2024 — extraction pipeline structure
title: build: Per-domain email extraction templates — CRE, client, tenant, tax, invoice

body:
## Parent: #2017 — Email-as-Queue Workflow

## Objective

Rewrite `scripts/email/gmail-archive-extract.py` to follow the new queue model: extract structured data, commit to repos, track thread state, and delete emails when topics complete. The current script archives entire raw email bodies to repos, which is the wrong approach.

## Current Script Analysis

`gmail-archive-extract.py` (19KB, ~500 lines) currently:
- Fetches full email (headers + body + attachments)
- Saves raw markdown dump to `/mnt/ace/<repo>/docs/email/`
- Optional: deletes from inbox after archiving

## New Script Requirements

### What to BUILD

1. **Structured extraction** — Parse subject/body/attachments into structured data (YAML/JSON) based on domain-specific templates:
   - CRE listings: property_name, tenant, price, cap_rate, address, state, lease_years, building_sf
   - Client emails: sender, company, project, deliverable_type, deadline, action_required
   - Tenant comms: store_number, issue_type, resolution, date_reported
   - Invoices: invoice_number, vendor, amount, due_date, status

2. **Thread state tracking** — Gmail labels (`wh-email/extracted`, `wh-email/awaiting-reply`, `wh-email/completed`) + local state file at `~/.hermes/email-state.yaml`

3. **Smart deletion** — 7-day grace period for completed threads, immediate for noise, NEVER for awaiting-reply

4. **Re-activation** — Check for new replies on "completed"/"awaiting-reply" threads, re-label as inbox

5. **Legal scan** — Run legal-sanity-scan.sh before every commit

### What to REMOVE

- Raw email body archiving to repos
- Full markdown dumps of email threads
- The `--delete` flag (deletion is now state-driven, not extraction-driven)

### Migration Plan

```
Phase 1: Build the new gmail-extract-and-act.py script
Phase 2: Test against a small batch (e.g., Sands IG CRE listings)
Phase 3: Compare extraction quality between old and new approach
Phase 4: Replace gmail-archive-extract.py with new script
Phase 5: Backfill — re-process previously archived emails to structured format
```

## Script Interface (proposed)

```bash
# Extract CRE data from Sands IG
uv run scripts/email/gmail-extract-and-act.py --account ace --domain sandsig.com --max 200

# Check for completed threads ready for deletion
uv run scripts/email/gmail-extract-and-act.py --account ace --action delete-completed --grace-days 7

# Dry run — show what would happen
uv run scripts/email/gmail-extract-and-act.py --account personal --action triage --dry-run

# State report — show all tracked threads and their status
uv run scripts/email/gmail-extract-and-act.py --state-report

# Re-activate stale awaiting-reply threads
uv run scripts/email/gmail-extract-and-act.py --account ace --action check-replies
```

## Deliverables
1. `scripts/email/gmail-extract-and-act.py` — new extraction script
2. `~/.hermes/email-state.yaml` — thread state tracker
3. Per-domain extraction templates in `scripts/email/templates/`
4. State machine diagram and documentation
5. Tests for extraction logic

## Depends On
- #2017 — workflow design decisions
- Existing: `email-routing.yaml`, OAuth setup, legal-sanity-scan.sh
title: build: gmail-extract-and-act pipeline — rewrite gmail-archive-extract.py for extract-first, delete-later queue model

body:
## Mission: Document compound engineering methodology for external consumption

> "Put all the random thoughts as issues and it will straighten the twisted brain."

### The Methodology That Emerged

This is NOT another "agentic workflow" document. This is the actual operational methodology that works in production, not theory.

### Core Components (Document These)

1. **Orchestrator/Worker Separation**
   - Single agent with context overload vs orchestrator delegating to isolated workers
   - Context relief enables parallel work with focused intent
   - Real operational example: 3-agent cross-review running for 1 year

2. **Enforcement Over Instruction**
   - Text-based rules (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md) get bypassed over time
   - Need technical gates (pre-commit hooks, CI checks) to enforce compliance
   - Current compliance: 4% -- proves the point

3. **Compound Learning Loop**
   - Skills improve from actual work, not upfront design
   - Post-task auto-issue generation for patterns worth capturing
   - Knowledge base grows from real engineering work, not artificial curation

4. **Multi-Agent Parity**
   - All agents read from same .claude/skills/ directory
   - Hermes secret sauce: skills -> repo hub -> symlink -> memory consolidation
   - No agent silos, shared context, immediate propagation

5. **3-Agent Cross-Review**
   - Plan review AND artifact review
   - Independent reviewers catch different failure modes
   - Works with Claude, Codex, Gemini -- any combination

### Output Artifacts
- [ ] `docs/methodology/compound-engineering.md` -- the methodology document
- [ ] `docs/methodology/enforcement-over-instruction.md` -- why gates beat text
- [ ] `docs/methodology/multi-agent-parity.md` -- skill sharing architecture
- [ ] `docs/methodology/orchestrator-worker.md` -- context isolation pattern
- [ ] `docs/methodology/compliance-dashboard.md` -- the monitoring approach
- [ ] README.md update linking to methodology docs

### Who This Is For
- ACE Engineer clients evaluating the firm (proof of engineering discipline)
- Other engineers building similar setups
- Future self documenting why certain decisions were made

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 methodology documents written and linked
- [ ] Includes real metrics (4% compliance, 691 skills, 1281 contacts)
- [ ] Clear enough for a new engineer to replicate
- [ ] Published on aceengineer.com (or linked from it)

### Related
- #2016 (client conversion pipeline -- uses this as marketing material)
- #1760 (self-improvement commands -- implementation details)
- #2018 (agent bypass resistance -- enforcement details)
- #1962 (Tier-1 repo refactoring -- engineering examples)
title: feat: document compound engineering methodology -- 5 operational lessons from production

body:
## Mission: Clean orchestrator/worker separation with context isolation

### What Works
Orchestrator delegates to isolated workers with fresh context. This provides real context relief -- the orchestrator maintains state and coordinates, workers execute with focused intent.

This has been running for 1 year across Claude, Gemini, and Codex before the term "adversarial" was applied to it.

### What Needs Improvement
Even with separation, agents "rush through and bypass." The architecture is sound but the enforcement is weak:

1. Workers don't wait for orchestrator plan approval before acting
2. Workers skip artifact review when they think results are "obvious"
3. Orchestrator trusts worker outputs without verification
4. No mechanism for workers to update orchestrator knowledge on discoveries

### Required Improvements

**Phase 1: Plan Gate Enforcement**
- Workers blocked from implementation until orchestrator plan is approved
- PreToolUse hook checks for approval marker
- Clear error message: "Plan not approved for this task"

**Phase 2: Artifact Verification**
- Workers produce artifacts, orchestrator verifies against spec
- If verification fails, worker gets specific feedback, not generic rejection
- Verified artifacts get review markers for future reference

**Phase 3: Knowledge Propagation**
- When a worker discovers something important, it updates the skill/knowledge base
- Orchestrator learns from worker outcomes
- Future tasks benefit from past worker learnings

**Phase 4: Fresh Repo Bootstrap**
- New repos get orchestrator + worker setup in minutes
- Skills propagate automatically
- No manual context sharing between agents

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Workers cannot act without approved plan
- [ ] Artifacts are verified by orchestrator before acceptance
- [ ] Worker learnings auto-update shared knowledge
- [ ] Fresh repo setup under 10 minutes
- [ ] Works across Claude Code, Codex, Gemini as workers

### Related
- #1760 (self-improvement commands - includes context capture)
- #1775 (CC best-practice adoption)
- engineering-issue-workflow skill
- SUBAGENT_CONTEXT_ISOLATION.md convention
title: feat: orchestrator/worker context enforcement -- plan gates, artifact verification, knowledge propagation

body:
## Mission: Agents must follow the workflow without exceptions

### Current State
- Review compliance at 4% (#2012)
- Text-based instructions in CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md get bypassed over time
- Agents (all of them) act like humans who want to get shit done and skip the hard parts
- Plan gate and commit gate testing in progress (#1876)

### Core Insight
> "Even hermes is not confident LLM text will make it adhere to it -- overtime agents just bypass"

The problem is fundamental: LLMs optimize for task completion, not process adherence. Telling an agent to follow rules is like telling a developer to write tests -- it happens sometimes, not always.

### Required: Infrastructure Enforcement

Text instructions MUST be backed by technical enforcement:

1. **Pre-commit hook** -- blocks commits without plan approval marker
2. **CI gate** -- rejects PRs without cross-review evidence
3. **Agent prefill** -- injects workflow constraints into session context
4. **Compliance dashboard** -- tracks and alerts on violations
5. **Auto-rollback** -- reverts commits that bypass gates

### Why This Matters
Without enforcement mode, every single artifact produced is potentially lower quality. The 3-agent review system only works if agents actually use it.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pre-commit hook blocks 100% of non-compliant commits
- [ ] CI pipeline rejects PRs without review sign-off
- [ ] Compliance rate > 80% (from current 4%)
- [ ] Zero engineering commits without plan review for 30+ days
- [ ] All agents tested: Hermes, Claude Code, Codex, Gemini

### Notes
- #1876 covers the implementation details
- #2012 tracks the compliance audit backlog
- This is the PRIORITY for any serious engineering work

### Related
- #1876 (Enforce engineering workflow via Hermes prefill + Claude Code hooks)
- #2012 (Review backlog audit - 4% compliance)
- docs/standards/HARD-STOP-POLICY.md
- docs/standards/AI_REVIEW_ROUTING_POLICY.md
title: feat: agent bypass resistance -- enforce workflow with technical gates, not text instructions

body:
## Context

Current email system is designed around **archiving everything** — routing 36,100 emails across 3 accounts into git repos by sender domain. This creates massive repo noise and does not match how email should actually be managed.

## New Principle: Email is a QUEUE, not an ARCHIVE

```
INBOUND → TRIAGE → EXTRACT DATA → ACT → DELETE
                                      ↓
                          Topic completed? → DELETE email
                          Awaiting reply?  → KEEP (live)
                          New reply arrives → RE-ACTIVATE
```

### Core Rules

1. **Do NOT save all emails.** Extract only the actionable data/information needed
2. **Delete the email when the topic is completed** — the extracted data lives in the repo, not the raw email
3. **Keep email alive when awaiting response** — thread stays in inbox until resolved
4. **Re-activate on reply** — when client/other party responds, the topic is live again
5. **Learn and improve over time** — the extraction patterns, routing rules, and triage logic should get smarter

### What Gets Extracted (examples)

| Email Type | What to Extract | Where It Goes |
|---|---|---|
| Client RFP/SOW | Scope, timeline, budget, contacts | aceengineer-admin/prospects/ |
| Invoice/payment | Amount, date, vendor, invoice # | aceengineer-admin/finance/ |
| Tenant communication | Issue, resolution, date | sabithaandkrishnaestates/ |
| CRE listing (Sands IG) | Property, price, cap rate, tenant | assethold/data/ |
| Tax document | Form type, amounts, tax year | achantas-data/tax/ |
| Recruiter outreach | Company, role, rate, contact | aceengineer-admin/recruiting/ |
| Technical deliverable | Report link, client, project | aceengineer-admin/projects/ |

### What Gets Deleted Immediately (no extraction needed)

- Marketing/newsletters after unsubscribe
- Social media notifications
- Promotional emails
- Duplicate forwards (ace↔personal)
- Expired event invitations

## Design Decisions Needed

1. **Thread state tracking**: How to know a topic is "completed" vs "awaiting reply"?
   - Option A: Gmail labels (e.g., `extracted`, `awaiting-reply`, `completed`)
   - Option B: Local state file tracking thread IDs and status
   - Option C: GitHub issue per active thread (heavyweight)

2. **Extraction format**: Structured YAML? Markdown? CSV rows?
   - Needs to be machine-readable for future analysis
   - But also human-scannable

3. **Deletion safety**: How to prevent accidental deletion of live threads?
   - Require explicit "mark as completed" before delete
   - Grace period (mark for deletion, delete after 7 days if no new reply)
   - Never auto-delete — always require user approval

4. **Learning loop**: How does the system get smarter?
   - Track extraction patterns that work well
   - Flag emails the system cannot classify
   - User corrections feed back into routing rules
   - Periodic review of deleted-vs-kept decisions

## Relationship to Existing Issues

This issue SUPERSEDES the archive-everything approach in:
- #1987 — Email cleanup pipeline (needs rewrite to extract-and-delete, not archive-and-delete)
- #1963 — Multi-account Gmail management (infra done, but triage workflow needs this new direction)
- Parts of #1968, #1969, #1971 — per-account triage workflows need to follow queue model

These issues remain valid but need updating:
- #1986 — Communication style profiles (still needed for drafting replies)
- #1991 — Sands IG flooding (extraction target: structured CRE data)

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Workflow Design (this issue)
- [ ] Decide thread state tracking approach
- [ ] Define extraction templates per email type
- [ ] Define deletion policy and safety rules
- [ ] Update email-routing.yaml to reflect extract-not-archive model
- [ ] Document the workflow in docs/email/WORKFLOW.md

### Phase 2: Build Extract-and-Delete Pipeline
- [ ] Modify gmail-archive-extract.py → gmail-extract-and-delete.py
- [ ] Implement extraction templates (structured data out, not raw email)
- [ ] Add thread state tracking (label-based or local)
- [ ] Add "mark completed" + grace period deletion

### Phase 3: Skill Consolidation
- [ ] Merge overlapping email skills into fewer, workflow-aligned skills
- [ ] Update gmail-triage skill to follow queue model
- [ ] Retire archive-everything skills

### Phase 4: Learning Loop
- [ ] Track what gets extracted vs deleted
- [ ] Flag unclassifiable emails for user review
- [ ] Periodic routing rule refinement

## Agent Assignment
- **Claude Code**: Workflow design, skill consolidation, pipeline rewrite
- **Hermes**: Cron execution, daily triage runs
- **Codex**: Extraction template patterns, test coverage
- **Gemini**: Research email workflow best practices, template design

## Skill Evolution
This workflow will be captured as a living skill that improves over time. Initial version is a starting point — corrections and new patterns get fed back in continuously.
title: design: Email-as-Queue workflow — extract data, delete when done, re-activate on reply

## Summary

Build tooling to discover and maintain cross-links between the 3 domain wikis (marine-engineering, maritime-law, naval-architecture).

## Problem

Wikis are currently siloed. Real engineering topics span domains:
- Deepwater Horizon → engineering (engineering) + law (OPA 90)  
- Mooring failures → marine-eng + naval-arch stability analysis
- Classification society rules → naval-arch + maritime-law liability

## Proposed: Cross-Wiki Link Index

```
knowledge/wikis/cross-links.md
```

| Source Wiki | Entity/Concept | Target Wiki | Target Page | Link Type |
|-------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| maritime-law | Deepwater Horizon | marine-engineering | lng-carrier-mooring | related-case |
| maritime-law | Amoco Cadiz | naval-architecture | ship-structures | hull-failure |

## Tooling

`llm-wiki cross-links --source wiki --target wiki` command:
1. Scan source wiki for mentions of entities in target wiki
2. Scan target wiki for mentions of source wiki entities
3. Report candidate links
4. Optionally create cross-wiki reference pages

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] cross-lines.md generated with at least 10 discovered links
- [ ] `llm-wiki cross-links` CLI command
- [ ] Existing wiki pages updated with cross-references

body:
## Context

Tier-1 Repo Audit (#1962) identified **10+ orphan modules** in worldenergydata (files with 0 imports).

## Orphan Detection Method

The audit used grep-based import counting — a weak heuristic that misses dynamic imports, __import__(), and importlib usage. So the true orphan count may be lower.

## Work

1. Run AST-based orphan detection (proper import analysis)
2. For each confirmed orphan: check if it's a CLI entry point (not imported but executed)
3. Archive truly unused modules or mark with `# CLI entry point` header
4. Update documentation accordingly

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] AST-based orphan scan complete
- [ ] All orphans categorized (archive / CLI / dynamic import / keep)
- [ ] Unused code archived with clear git history
title: audit(worldenergydata): 14000+ orphan modules — triage for archival vs active use (#1962 Phase 3)

body:
## Context

Tier-1 Repo Audit (#1962) found critical infrastructure issues in worldenergydata:

### Test Collection Timeout
- The test suite times out during collection (>30s)
- Cannot even enumerate tests, let alone run them
- Likely caused by heavy imports or expensive fixture setup

### 14,145 Bytecode Files
- Extremely deep dependency tree
- 11.71s compile time — major code smell
- May indicate excessive dependencies in pyproject.toml
- Consider lazy imports or splitting dev dependencies

## Investigation Steps

1. `uv run python -c "import time; start=time.time(); import worldenergydata; print(f'import took {time.time()-start:.2f}s')"`
2. Audit pyproject.toml — check for unnecessary dev dependencies being installed
3. Profile imports: `python -X importtime -m worldenergydata 2>&1 | tail -50`
4. Identify which 14K .pyc files belong to which packages

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Test collection completes within 30s
- [ ] All 431 tests pass with 0 errors
- [ ] Bytecode file count explained or reduced
- [ ] pyproject.toml cleaned if needed
title: audit(digitalmodel): worldenergydata test collection timeout + 14K bytecode files — investigate and resolve

Expertise: offshore riser/pipeline/mooring design, FEED/pre-FEED, OrcaFlex/AQWA/ANSYS, digital twins/automation. Former BP Macondo emergency response engineering manager. > 2026-04-06 02:49:51 PM (UTC)

## Required Actions

2. [ ] Identify which secret was exposed (likely the Gmail OAuth client credentials from the earlier push attempt)
3. [ ] Rotate the exposed secret if needed
4. [ ] Verify GitHub push protection blocked the commit (it did — the amended commit without secrets was what landed)
5. [ ] Enable Secret Scanning on workspace-hub repo: https://github.com/vamseeachanta/workspace-hub/settings/security_analysis
6. [ ] Add pre-commit hook to scan for secrets before commit

## Context
During the Gmail OAuth setup (#1964), the client_id and client_secret were initially hardcoded in gmail-digest.py. GitHub push protection blocked the push. The commit was amended to use env vars + local config file (~/.gmail-mcp/oauth-env.json). However, GitGuardian may have detected the secret in the local git history.

## Agent Assignment

## Safety Rules
- NEVER delete without user approval for first run per category

- Keep all sent emails (communication history)

body:
## Parent: #1963 — Multi-account Gmail Management

## Objective
Analyze sent email history for each account and codify communication style into reusable templates/profiles that agents can use when drafting replies.

## Analysis of Sent Email Patterns (from ~30 sent per account)

### ACE (vamsee.achanta@aceengineer.com) — PROFESSIONAL ENGINEERING
**Observed patterns:**
- Greeting: First name only ("Howard,", "Chau,", "Justin,")
- Closing: "Thank you," or "Thank you and Best regards," followed by "Vamsee"
- Tone: Technical, direct, concise — shares GitHub report links inline
- Content: Engineering deliverables, calculation references, analysis results
- Frequency: Multiple rapid exchanges in technical threads
- Signature: No P.E. suffix in casual threads, uses it in formal contexts
- Quirk: Shares GitHub hosted HTML reports as deliverables

**Derived style profile:**
```
greeting: "{FirstName},"
closing: "Thank you,\nVamsee"
tone: professional-technical
formality: medium (formal with new contacts, relaxed with ongoing)
length: concise — 2-5 sentences typical
attachments: prefers inline links over attachments
signature: "Vamsee Achanta, P.E." (formal), "Vamsee" (casual)
```

### PERSONAL (achantav@gmail.com) — CASUAL/ABBREVIATED
**Observed patterns:**
- Greeting: First name or none
- Closing: "Vamsee" or none (terse messages)
- Tone: Very brief, informal, often just forwards or one-liners
- Content: Self-forwards (ace<->personal), family, job discussions
- Many emails are just file forwards to self (ace account) with no body
- Professional spillover: recruiter responses, SOW discussions

**Derived style profile:**
```
greeting: "{FirstName}," or omitted
closing: "Vamsee" or omitted
tone: casual-terse
formality: low
length: ultra-short — 1-2 sentences or just "FYI"
typical_patterns: "Thank you for the update. will keep churning on my side :)"
```

### SKESTATES (skestatesinc@gmail.com) — BUSINESS FORMAL/LANDLORD
**Observed patterns:**
- Greeting: First name ("Kevin,", "Angela,", "Ian,", "Connie,")
- Closing: "Thank you very much," or "With best regards," + "Vamsee"
- Tone: Business formal but warm — acknowledges good work, shows appreciation
- Content: Property management, lease, 1099 disputes, maintenance follow-ups
- Subject line convention: "FD30150 | {Topic}" — always includes store number
- Follow-up pattern: persistent but polite ("Following up on...", "Please kindly let me know the status")
- References "the owners" when talking about SKEstates decisions

**Derived style profile:**
```
greeting: "{FirstName},"
closing: "Thank you very much,\nVamsee"
tone: business-formal-warm
formality: high
length: medium — 3-6 sentences
subject_prefix: "FD30150 | "
identity: "the owners" or "SKEstates Inc"
follow_up_style: "Following up on the {topic} plan and progress for the property."
appreciation: "deeply thank you", "made their week", "Keep up the good work"
```

## Tasks
1. [ ] Create `config/email/ace-style.yaml` — tone, greeting, closing, signature rules
2. [ ] Create `config/email/personal-style.yaml`
3. [ ] Create `config/email/skestates-style.yaml`
4. [ ] Update `gmail-touchbase` skill with per-account templates derived from real style
5. [ ] Create `scripts/email/style-analyzer.py` — re-run periodically to update style from recent sent
6. [ ] Integrate style profiles into digest script for draft-response suggestions

## Agent Assignment
- **Claude Code**: Style YAML creation, skill updates
- **Gemini**: Validate tone classification against email samples
title: feat: Establish per-account email communication style profiles from sent history

## Account: vamsee.achanta@aceengineer.com (alias: ace)

## Purpose
Engineering consulting inbox — highest business value. RFPs, client comms, invoices, GTM prospects.

## Triage Rules (ace-specific)

### VIP Senders (always surface)
- Active clients: @ril.com, @dorisgroup.com, @mcdermott.com
- GTM prospects (cross-ref aceengineer-strategy/ pipeline)
- aceengineer.com internal

### Classification
| Category | Signal | Action |
|---|---|---|
| URGENT | VIP sender, urgent/asap/deadline, invoice/payment | Immediate notify |
| ACTIONABLE | Question, RFP, meeting request, scope of work | Draft response |
| FYI | CCd, industry news, project updates | Batch read |
| NEWSLETTER | SPE, OTC, LinkedIn, industry digests | Keep but deprioritize |
| SPAM | Unknown sender, no contact match | Flag for review |

### Unsubscribe Policy: CONSERVATIVE
- Keep: SPE, OTC, ASME, offshore industry newsletters
- Unsubscribe: vendor marketing, recruiter spam from staffing agencies
- Ask user: LinkedIn notifications, conference promos

body:
## Overview

Enable unified multi-account Gmail management from the CLI/agent layer. Three accounts, each with distinct purpose, contact base, and handling rules.

## Accounts

| Account | Purpose | Contacts Repo | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| `vamsee.achanta@aceengineer.com` | Engineering consulting, GTM, client comms | `aceengineer-admin` (1,306 contacts) | Business/Professional |
| `achantav@gmail.com` | Personal, career networking, subscriptions | `aceengineer-admin` (1,157 contacts) | Personal |
| `skestatesinc@gmail.com` | Real estate LLC, tenant/vendor comms | `sabithaandkrishnaestates` | Business/RE |

## Architecture

### Layer 1: Authentication & Transport
- [ ] Install himalaya CLI (for IMAP/SMTP — lightweight, no OAuth dance)
- [ ] Configure 3 Gmail accounts with App Passwords in `~/.config/himalaya/config.toml`
- [ ] Verify Google Workspace OAuth setup for accounts needing Calendar/Drive/Sheets (aceengineer only)
- [ ] Create `scripts/email/gmail-multi-account.sh` — wrapper that routes `--account` flag

### Layer 2: Contact Management
- [ ] Normalize `aceengineer-admin/admin/contacts/aceengineer_contacts.csv` — clean duplicates, parse names, categorize (client/vendor/recruiter/newsletter)
- [ ] Normalize `aceengineer-admin/admin/contacts/achantav_contacts.csv` — same cleanup
- [ ] Create `sabithaandkrishnaestates/admin/contacts/skestates_contacts.csv` from existing key_contacts.md + fd_corporate_contact_maintenance.md
- [ ] Build `scripts/email/contact-classifier.py` — categorize contacts as: VIP, client, vendor, recruiter, newsletter, spam, unknown
- [ ] Tag contacts with `touchbase` flag (people to maintain relationship with)
- [ ] Tag contacts with `unsubscribe` flag (newsletters/marketing to purge)

### Layer 3: Email Triage Skill
- [ ] Create skill `email/gmail-triage` — multi-account inbox scan, categorize, and recommend actions
  - Scan each inbox for unread
  - Classify: urgent/actionable/FYI/newsletter/spam
  - Cross-reference sender against contacts DB
  - Flag unknown senders for contact-add or unsubscribe
  - Generate daily digest

### Layer 4: Action Handlers
- [ ] Create skill `email/gmail-unsubscribe` — identify and execute unsubscribe from marketing/newsletters
  - Scan for `List-Unsubscribe` headers
  - Batch unsubscribe with confirmation
  - Move to trash after unsubscribe
- [ ] Create skill `email/gmail-touchbase` — periodic outreach to tagged contacts
  - Pull contacts tagged `touchbase`
  - Check last interaction date
  - Draft personalized check-in emails
  - Queue for user approval before sending
- [ ] Create skill `email/gmail-responder` — draft replies for actionable emails
  - Match email context to account domain
  - aceengineer: professional engineering tone
  - achantav: casual/personal tone  
  - skestates: business/formal landlord tone

### Layer 5: Cron Automation
- [ ] Daily morning inbox scan (all 3 accounts) — cron job delivering digest to Telegram/CLI
- [ ] Weekly touchbase reminder — contacts due for outreach
- [ ] Monthly unsubscribe sweep — identify new newsletter subscriptions

## Account-Specific Rules

### vamsee.achanta@aceengineer.com
- Priority: client emails, RFPs, invoice responses
- Auto-label: GTM prospects, active clients, vendors
- Touchbase: engineering contacts, potential clients from GTM pipeline
- Link to: `aceengineer-strategy/` prospect data

### achantav@gmail.com  
- Priority: personal finance, family, career
- Aggressive unsubscribe: marketing, social media notifications
- Touchbase: close professional network, alumni
- Filter: separate personal from professional spillover

### skestatesinc@gmail.com
- Priority: tenant communications (Family Dollar), tax/legal
- Auto-label: tenant, insurance, tax, maintenance
- Touchbase: property management contacts, tax advisors
- Link to: `sabithaandkrishnaestates/` deal files

## Dependencies
- himalaya CLI installed on ace-linux-1
- Gmail App Passwords generated for all 3 accounts (2FA must be enabled)
- Contact CSVs cleaned and normalized

## Deliverables
1. Working 3-account email access from CLI
2. Cleaned/categorized contact databases
3. `gmail-triage` skill with daily digest
4. `gmail-unsubscribe` skill
5. `gmail-touchbase` skill  
6. Cron jobs for automated monitoring

## Agent Assignment
- **Hermes (ace-linux-1)**: Primary email operator — cron jobs, daily digest, inbox scans
- **Claude Code (CLI)**: Skill creation, contact normalization scripts, code review
- **Codex**: Contact classifier ML/heuristics, unsubscribe pattern detection
- **Gemini**: Research best practices for email automation, touchbase templates

---

## Execution Plan & Issue Dependency Graph

```
PHASE 1 — Infrastructure (Week 1)
  #1964  Install gmail-mcp-multiauth + himalaya        [BLOCKING — everything depends on this]
         Requires: GCP project, OAuth setup, App Passwords
         User action: browser OAuth flow for 3 accounts

PHASE 2 — Contact Normalization (Week 1-2, parallel with Phase 1 partial)
  #1965  Normalize ace contacts (1,306 rows)            [Independent]
  #1966  Normalize personal contacts (1,140 rows)       [Independent]
  #1967  Build skestates contacts (~23 entries)          [Independent]
  #1970  Cross-account dedup (132 overlaps)              [Depends: #1965, #1966]

PHASE 3 — Account-Specific Triage (Week 2-3, after Phase 1+2)
  #1971  ace triage workflow                             [Depends: #1964, #1965]
  #1968  personal triage workflow                        [Depends: #1964, #1966]
  #1969  skestates triage workflow                       [Depends: #1964, #1967]
```

## Child Issues

body:
## Summary

Systematic refactoring of the workspace-hub repo ecosystem, starting with tier-1 repositories. The goal is to reduce architectural debt, consolidate duplicate patterns, enforce consistent structure, and improve agent navigability across all tier-1 repos.

## Tier-1 Repos (Priority Order)

| # | Repo | Purpose | Maturity | Depends On |
|---|------|---------|----------|------------|
| 1 | `digitalmodel/` | Engineering digital twin (asset integrity, CP, GIS, hydro) | beta | assetutilities |
| 2 | `assetutilities/` | Shared engineering utilities (config, units, calculations) | stable | — |
| 3 | `assethold/` | Asset portfolio financial analysis | beta | assetutilities |
| 4 | `worldenergydata/` | Global energy market data (BSEE, EIA, drilling) | beta | assetutilities |
| 5 | `CAD-DEVELOPMENTS/` | CAD solvers, models, database, utilities | beta | — |
| 6 | `aceengineer-website/` | Consulting website | — | — |
| 7 | `aceengineer-strategy/` | GTM, business strategy | — | — |
| 8 | `sabithaandkrishnaestates/` | SKEstates Inc. business ops | — | — |

## Refactoring Scope

### Phase 1: Audit (analysis-only, no code changes)
- [ ] Run `repo-architecture-analysis` on each tier-1 repo
- [ ] Identify cross-repo duplicate code (especially assetutilities consumers)
- [ ] Map dependency graph (imports, shared configs, data flows)
- [ ] Catalog test coverage per repo (current state)
- [ ] Identify orphaned/dead code, unused modules, oversized files (>400 lines)
- [ ] Score each repo against the Control-Plane Contract (AGENTS.md, provider adapters)
- [ ] Identify skill duplicates across repos (134 known from #1720 audit)

### Phase 2: Plan (per-repo refactor plans)
- [ ] Generate refactor plan per tier-1 repo with estimated effort
- [ ] Prioritize by: business value × technical debt severity × dependency impact
- [ ] Identify breaking changes and migration paths
- [ ] Define test coverage targets (tier-1 = 85% per pytest.tier1.ini)

### Phase 3: Execute (TDD, incremental, per-repo)
- [ ] Execute refactors repo-by-repo following dependency order (assetutilities first)
- [ ] TDD mandatory — tests before implementation
- [ ] Each repo refactor as a separate sub-issue for tracking

## Execution Strategy: Claude Code Plan Mode (Cloud)

### Claude Code `--permission-mode plan` ("Ultraplan")

Claude Code v2.1.92 supports `--permission-mode plan` which restricts the agent to **read-only analysis and planning** — no file writes, no shell mutations. This is ideal for Phase 1 (Audit) and Phase 2 (Plan).

**Proposed execution:**

```bash
# Phase 1: Audit each tier-1 repo in plan mode (safe, read-only)
claude --permission-mode plan \
  --print \
  --model claude-opus-4-6 \
  --max-budget-usd 5 \
  -p "Audit the repository architecture of digitalmodel/. Analyze: module structure, dependency graph, test coverage, dead code, file sizes >400 lines, AGENTS.md compliance. Output a structured JSON report."

# Phase 2: Generate refactor plan
claude --permission-mode plan \
  --print \
  --model claude-opus-4-6 \
  --max-budget-usd 5 \
  -p "Given the audit report, generate a prioritized refactor plan for digitalmodel/. Include: breaking changes, migration steps, test targets, estimated effort per module."
```

### Cloud Execution Considerations

| Aspect | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| **Claude Code CLI** | Installed (v2.1.92) | Available on ace-linux-1 |
| **Plan mode** | Supported | `--permission-mode plan` — read-only, safe for audit |
| **API-key mode** | Supported | `--bare` + `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` for headless/cloud runs |
| **Print mode** | Supported | `-p` / `--print` for non-interactive batch execution |
| **Budget cap** | Supported | `--max-budget-usd` to control spend per run |
| **Cloud sandbox** | Possible | Can run via Hermes `delegate_task` with `acp_command='claude'` |
| **Max subscription** | Claude Max $200/mo | Rate-limited but sufficient for plan-mode audit |

### Recommended Approach
1. **Phase 1 + 2 (Audit + Plan)**: Run Claude Code in plan mode via `delegate_task(acp_command='claude')` — subagents get isolated context, read-only, budget-capped
2. **Phase 3 (Execute)**: Requires interactive Claude Code sessions with full permissions — NOT suited for autonomous cloud execution without user oversight

## Adversarial Review Requirements

Per [AI Review Routing Policy](docs/standards/AI_REVIEW_ROUTING_POLICY.md):

- **3-agent adversarial review at BOTH stages** (plan + code/artifact)
- Claude: orchestrator + reviewer
- Codex: implementation worker + adversarial reviewer
- Gemini: architecture reviewer + adversarial reviewer
- Max 3 review iterations per deliverable
- All MAJOR findings must be resolved before completion

### Review Checkpoints
1. **After Phase 1 (Audit)**: All 3 agents review the audit findings
2. **After Phase 2 (Plan)**: All 3 agents review each repo's refactor plan
3. **After Phase 3 (Execute)**: All 3 agents review code changes per repo

## Related Issues
- #1567 — Feature: Continuous Repo Architecture Intelligence
- #1545 — Agentic feature progression for repo ecosystem productivity under GSD
- #1603 — Multi-repo architecture scan — expand scanner to all tier-1 repos
- #1720 — Cross-agent session corpus audit (134 duplicate skills identified)
- #1734 — chore: add domain skills to assethold (tier-1 with 0 skills)
- #1661 — Architecture scanner: dependency cycle detection and layering violations
- #113 — ROADMAP: Repo ecosystem 3-6 month horizon

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Audit report generated for each tier-1 repo
- [ ] Refactor plan per repo reviewed and approved by all 3 agents
- [ ] Phase 3 sub-issues created with clear scope per repo
- [ ] Test coverage meets tier-1 target (85%) after refactoring
- [ ] Zero cross-repo duplicate skills remaining
- [ ] All repos score 100% on Control-Plane Contract compliance
- [ ] Dependency graph documented and validated

labels.name: agent:claude
labels.description: Heavy coding, architecture, orchestration, complex TDD

labels.name: agent:codex
labels.description: Bounded implementation, test writing, review, refactoring

title: FEATURE: Tier-1 Repo Ecosystem Refactoring — audit, plan, execute with Claude Code plan mode

body:
## Context

The conference indexing lightweight catalog (#1608) completed Phase A — 27,735 files across 30 collections cataloged with metadata only (no full text extraction).

The full text extraction via `extract-document.py` was blocked by a DocumentManifest schema bug in pdf.py (early-return path missing version/tool/domain args). This bug is now fixed (commit: 75985abd).

## Scope

Run `extract-document.py` on all 27,735 conference files to produce full extraction manifests with sections, tables, and figure refs.

### Collections by Size
| Collection | Files | Size | Priority |
|------------|-------|------|----------|
| OMAE | 10,130 | 7.4 GB | P1 (largest, highest value) |
| OTC | 5,725 | 6.9 GB | P1 |
| ISOPE | 4,183 | 2.9 GB | P1 |
| DOT | 3,636 | 1.3 GB | P2 |
| UK Conference Folder | 2,431 | 1.7 GB | P2 |
| 25 smaller collections | 1,630 | 2.0 GB | P3 |

## Approach

1. Create a batch wrapper script similar to `index-conferences-lightweight.py` but calling `extract-document.py`
2. Process in collection-sized chunks with checkpointing
3. Output manifests to `data/doc-intelligence/manifests/conferences/<collection>/`
4. Monitor for extraction failures (scanned PDFs may need OCR pipeline)

## Dependencies
- [x] DocumentManifest schema bug fixed (75985abd)
- [x] pdfplumber added to dependencies
- [ ] OCR pipeline wired in (for scanned conference PDFs)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 27,735 files processed with extraction manifests
- [ ] Error rate < 10% documented
- [ ] Manifests indexed into searchable format
- [ ] Conference summary report with extraction stats

labels.description: Bounded implementation, test writing, review, refactoring

# Excel-to-Code Conversion Pipeline — 100 workbooks via ws014 Claude-in-Excel

> **Execution Machine**: ws014 (Windows, Claude in Excel add-in)
> **Round Trip**: Workbooks here -> analyze in Claude-in-Excel -> convert to Python -> PR back to digitalmodel/assetutilities repos

## Scope

100 engineering workbooks staged in `engineering_workbooks/` for systematic conversion to Python code. Each converted workbook needs:
1. Read sheets, extract formulas and calculation logic
2. Write Python module with openpyxl for I/O, type hints, unit tests
3. Verify outputs match original spreadsheet
4. PR back to appropriate repo (`digitalmodel/` or `assetutilities/`)

## Batches

| Batch | Issue | Domain | Workbooks | Est Tokens |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | #1935 | Ballymore Jumper + Mudmat | 10 | ~4.5M |
| 2 | #1936 | FDAS Riser Engineering | 20+ | ~8.5M |
| 3 | #1937 | Installation References | 18 | ~5.0M |
| 4 | #1938 | SCR + Wall Thickness Design | 18 | ~4.5M |
| 5 | #1939 | Talos Venice Umbilical | 31 | ~4.0M |
| 6 | #1940 | Shell Perdido + VMCast + Production Eng | 71 | ~11M |
| 7 | TBD | SRP/Dynacard + Casing | ~20 | ~4.0M |

## Tracking

- **Registry**: [EXCEL-CONVERSION-REGISTRY.md](docs/document-intelligence/EXCEL-CONVERSION-REGISTRY.md)
- **Priority list**: [EXCEL-CONVERSION-PRIORITY.md](docs/document-intelligence/EXCEL-CONVERSION-PRIORITY.md)

## Workflow (ws014)

1. Pull client_projects repo on ws014: `gh repo clone vamseeachanta/client_projects`
2. Open workbook from `engineering_workbooks/` folder with Claude Excel add-in
3. Analyze sheet structure, formulas, cross-refs — understand calculation logic
4. Convert to Python: openpyxl I/O, type hints, pytest coverage
5. Open PR back to `digitalmodel/` or `assetutilities/` repo with code
6. Cross-review: another Claude/Codex instance reviews
7. Mark checkbox below when batch is complete

## Progress

- [ ] #1935 Batch 1: Ballymore Jumper + Mudmat (10 workbooks) — ~4.5M tokens
- [ ] #1936 Batch 2: FDAS Riser Engineering (20+ workbooks) — ~8.5M tokens
- [ ] #1937 Batch 3: Installation References (18 workbooks) — ~5.0M tokens
- [ ] #1938 Batch 4: SCR Design DNV F201 + Wall Thickness (18 workbooks) — ~4.5M tokens
- [ ] #1939 Batch 5: Talos Venice Umbilical (31 workbooks) — ~4.0M tokens
- [ ] #1940 Batch 6: Shell Perdido + VMCast + Production Eng Library (71 workbooks) — ~11M tokens
- [ ] TBD Batch 7: SRP/Dynacard + Casing (~20 workbooks) — ~4.0M tokens
title: FEATURE: Excel-to-Code Conversion Pipeline — 100 workbooks via ws014 Claude-in-Excel

body:
# Excel Workbook Conversion: Engineering Calculations to Python

## Objective

Systematically convert productive engineering Excel workbooks to Python code in our repo ecosystem. This enables:

- **Scalable, testable calculations** — TDD-based engineering code with `pytest`
- **Version-controlled logic** — No more "which version of the spreadsheet is correct"
- **Reusable library** — Production engineering, structural, and hydro calculations in `digitalmodel/` and `assetutilities/`
- **GTM enablement** — Converted calculations become demo capabilities (FDAS, freespan, etc.)

## Strategy

1. **Transfer** productive workbooks to private `client_projects` repo on target machine via private repo
2. **Analyze** with Claude in Excel to extract calculation logic, formulas, and assumptions
3. **Convert** to Python code with proper typing, openpyxl for I/O, and pytest coverage
4. **Verify** results match original spreadsheet outputs (cross-check)
5. **Deploy** to `digitalmodel/` or `assetutilities/` repos

## Phase A: High-Priority Workbooks (38 total)

### Tier 1: Deepwater Installation/Lift Engineering (Priority: P0)
| # | File | Domain | Sheets | Status |
|---|------|--------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `Jumper_Input_Ballymore_Manifold-PLET V2.xlsx` | Jumper lift engineering | 7 sheets (GA, Bare pipe, Bouyancy, Strake, Rigging, Crane Configuration, Weight Check) | INVENTORY |
| 2 | `614-CAL-2217-01 (SideLift Plate Buckling).xlsm` | Plate buckling analysis | TBD | INVENTORY |
| 3 | `614-CAL-2215-01 (SEWOL ANSYS Buckling Template).xlsm` | ANSYS buckling template | TBD | INVENTORY |

### Tier 2: Riser/Intervention Engineering (Priority: P1)
| # | File | Domain | Status |
|---|------|--------|--------|
| 4 | `InterventionRiser.xlsm` (Rev1/Rev2 variants) | Intervention riser Abaqus model | INVENTORY |
| 5 | `drillingriser.xlsm` (+ HWCG variant) | Drilling riser Abaqus model | INVENTORY |
| 6 | `Riser Sizing and Top Tension*.xlsx` | Riser sizing/TT calc | INVENTORY |
| 7 | `20160730 Riser Stroke-Stretch v WSirius Heave.xlsx` | Riser stroke/stretch | INVENTORY |
| 8 | Telescopic Joint, Tree, Stem Joint models (12+ files) | Riser assembly models | INVENTORY |

### Tier 3: Umbilical/SCR Installation (Priority: P1)
| # | Source | Domain | Status |
|---|--------|--------|--------|
| 9 | `saipem/yellowtail/code/` | Umbilical dynamic analysis/post-processing | INVENTORY |
| 10 | `saipem/general/engg/dynamic_umb_go_by/` | Catenary wave installation | INVENTORY |
| 11 | Multiple Static Results workbooks | FPSO offset/static analysis | INVENTORY |

### Tier 4: Production Engineering Calculations (Priority: P0)
| # | File | Domain | Status |
|---|------|--------|--------|
| 12 | ESPdesign-SI/US Field Units.xls | ESP design | INVENTORY |
| 13 | GasLiftValveDesign-SI/US.xls | Gas lift valve design | INVENTORY |
| 14 | Cullender-SmithBHP, Guo-GhalamborBHP | BHP calculation methods | INVENTORY |
| 15 | Brill-Beggs-Z, Hall-Yarborough-Z, etc. | PVT/fluid properties | INVENTORY |
| 16 | BottomHoleNodalGas, Multilateral Gas/Oil | Nodal analysis | INVENTORY |
| 17 | Centrifugal/Reciprocating Compressor Power | Compression calcs | INVENTORY |
| 18 | SuckerRodPumping + dynacard workbooks | SRP/dynamometer card | INVENTORY |
| 19 | Hydraulics Input Sheet v2.xlsm | Well hydraulics | INVENTORY |

### Tier 5: Structural/Mechanical (Priority: P2)
| # | Domain | Count | Status |
|---|--------|-------|--------|
| 20 | Corrosion/materials | 2 workbooks | INVENTORY |
| 21 | FPSO/barge characteristics | 3 workbooks | INVENTORY |
| 22 | Padeye sizing/structural lifts | 4 workbooks | INVENTORY |
| 23 | SCR pipe design | 3 workbooks | INVENTORY |

### Tier 6: Hydrodynamics/Mooring (Priority: P2)
| # | Domain | Count | Status |
|---|--------|-------|--------|
| 24 | Passing ship analysis | 6 workbooks (.xlsm) | INVENTORY |
| 25 | Vessel stability analysis | 10+ workbooks | INVENTORY |
| 26 | RAO data processing/curves | 3 workbooks | INVENTORY |
| 27 | Mooring static analysis | 4 workbooks | INVENTORY |
| 28 | AQWA damping/setup | 2 workbooks (.xlsm) | INVENTORY |

## First Conversion Target

**Workbook**: `Jumper_Input_Ballymore_Manifold-PLET V2.xlsx`
**Location**: `/mnt/ace/rock-oil-field/s7/ballymore/Jumper_Manifold to PLET/`
**Sheets** (7 total):
1. **GA** — General Arrangement geometry (85 formulas, pipe segments, bend arc lengths)
2. **Bare pipe** — Pipe physical properties (OD, WT, ID, linear weight, insulation density)
3. **Bouyancy** — Buoyancy module characterization (displaced volume, hydrodynamic OD)
4. **Strake** — VIV suppression strake properties
5. **Rigging** — Lift rigging design (sling stiffness, spreader bar geometry, crane trigonometry)
6. **Crane Configuration** — Crane capacity/Utilization Check
7. **Weight Check** — Master weight tally with COG calculations, cross-sheet references

**Size**: 2.3 MB | **Last modified**: 2025-01-31

This is already analyzed and documented. Ready for code conversion when tokens/credits permit.

## Phase B: Internal Reference Workbooks

4 internal workbooks (O&G knowledge base, pipeline training, J-lay analysis, ICE emissions) — keep in `client_projects` repo.

## Phase C: Exclude from Conversion (Admin/Data Only)

- Timesheets (Saipem, ACMA) — TRANSFER ONLY
- Invoices/business expenses — TRANSFER ONLY
- Personal financial data — TRANSFER ONLY
- BSEE production/well raw data — KEEP AS DATA FILES

## Tracking Document

Full registry with 50 entries at: `docs/document-intelligence/EXCEL-CONVERSION-REGISTRY.md`

## Labels

`cat:engineering`, `cat:data-pipeline`, `priority:medium`, `domain:document-intelligence`

## Scope
Build Demo 1: DNV-RP-F105 freespan/VIV screening for pipelines and rigid jumpers.

## Ready to Build
- Input data files exist: pipelines.json, rigid_jumpers.json, freespan_scenarios.json
- Python modules exist: digitalmodel/subsea/pipeline/free_span/ (9 modules)
  - span_natural_frequency.py, span_onset_screening.py, span_viv_response.py
  - span_fatigue_damage.py, span_allowable_length.py, wave_velocity.py
  - weibull_current.py, _bilinear_sn.py, models.py
- report_template.py (GTMReportBuilder) ready
- calc_values.py (shared helpers) ready

## Parameter Matrix
- Pipelines: 8", 12", 16" x span 10-80m (8 steps) x 5 currents x 4 gap ratios = ~480 cases
- Jumpers: 5 lengths x span 5-40m x 5 currents = ~200 cases
- Total: ~680 cases

## Charts (5)
1. Natural frequency vs span length (pipe size parametric)
2. VIV onset screening (inline + crossflow threshold)
3. Max allowable span heatmap (pipe size x current velocity)
4. Pass/fail matrix (pipe x span x current x gap ratio)
5. Jumper vs pipeline comparison

## Output
- output/demo_01_freespan_report.html
- results/demo_01_freespan_results.json (--from-cache ready)

## Build Order
Demo 3 -> Demo 5 -> Demo 1 -> Demo 4

body:
## Scope
Install xlrd library and run deep scan of all 3,879 .xls binary workbooks discovered in Phase 1 (#1910).

## Context
Phase 1 scanned 4,125 Excel files. 3,879 are legacy BIFF binary (.xls) — 94% of the total.
xlrd is not installed in the workspace-hub environment, so we could only detect binary header magic bytes.

## Action Items
1. Install xlrd: `uv add xlrd` in workspace-hub
2. Update scanner script to use xlrd for .xls files
3. Re-run scan on all 3,879 .xls files (estimated ~2-3 min at 60 files/s)
4. Merge new results with existing excel-inventory.jsonl
5. Update report

## Expected Output
- Full sheet names, row counts, header samples for all 3,879 .xls files
- Formula detection
- Macro detection (OLE2 compound doc VBA project detection)

## Parent
#1909

## Related
- #1910 — Phase 1: Excel file discovery (DONE ✅)
- #1905 — Rigid jumper workbook (consumer)
title: PHASE 2: Deep scan 3,879 .xls workbooks with xlrd library

body:
## Problem

After marine taxonomy V2 classifier (#1653), 10,530 marine documents remain unclassified. These are:

1. **DNV/Classification standards** (~6K) — filenames like `DNV_OS_304_Risk_Based_Verification.pdf`
2. **OTC Conference papers** (~3K) — filenames like `OTC-7325-MS.pdf`
3. **Generic/Admin** (~1.5K) — quality manuals, guidelines, wave energy reports

These require reading PDF content (title page, abstract) to classify into subdomains.

## Classification Approach

**Phase 1: DNV Standard Number Mapping**
- Many DNV OS/RP/CN codes are not yet in the standard mapping
- Research and add DNV standard-to-subdomain mappings:
  - `DNV OS E301` → mooring
  - `DNV OS J101` → wind/vessels
  - `DNV RP C205` → hydrodynamics
  - `DNV CN 30.4` → structural
  - etc.

**Phase 2: PDF Title/Abstract Extraction**
- Use pdfplumber/pdfminer to extract first 2 pages of digital PDFs
- Parse title page for classification keywords
- Match title/abstract text against marine subdomain keywords (from #1653 taxonomy)

**Phase 3: OTC Paper Classification**
- Extract paper title from first page
- Map OTC paper topics to subdomains
- Many OTC papers reference multiple subdomains — use multi-label approach

## Expected Outcome

- Reduce unclassified marine docs from 10,530 to < 2,000
- Overall marine classification rate (ex-CAD): 90%+
- Each doc tagged with subdomain + confidence score

## Dependencies

- #1653 (marine taxonomy V2) — keyword lists and taxonomy structure
- #1643 (OCR parser) — ONLY if scanned PDFs need processing (punted)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Expanded DNV standard mapping (100+ additional codes)
- [ ] PDF title/abstract extraction pipeline
- [ ] All 10,530 docs processed
- [ ] < 2,000 remain truly unclassifiable
- [ ] Updated `marine-subdomain-tags.yaml` with content-based tags
title: Classify remaining 10K marine docs via PDF content extraction and title parsing

## Summary

Current summarization: 61.9% (639,585 of 1,033,933 docs)
Target: 90%+ summarization
Remaining: ~394K documents to summarize

## Plan (from #1769 — plan document exists)

See `docs/document-intelligence/phase-b-summarization-plan.md` for detailed plan.

### Batch Strategy

| Batch | Scope | Docs | Est. Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Marine + CAD | 100K | ~22 hours |
| 2 | Pipeline + Materials | 100K | ~22 hours |
| 3 | Structural + Other | 100K | ~22 hours |
| 4 | Remainder | 94K | ~20 hours |

**Total: ~86 hours (~88 hours including QA time)**

### Key Design Decisions

1. **Skip image-based/scanned PDFs** — OCR punted per user decision
2. **Text-extractable only** — pdfminer/pdfplumber for digital PDFs
3. **Quality checks after each batch** — spot-check 50-100 summaries
4. **Overnight batch execution** — run batches across separate terminals
5. **Subdomain-aware** — use marine taxonomy tags (#1653) to enrich summaries

### Compute Cost Estimate

- Average 0.8s per document
- ~$0.50-$2.00 per 1K docs depending on model choice
- Total estimated: $200-$800 for full corpus (depending on model tier)

### Architecture Needed

```python
scripts/doc-intel/phase-b-summarizer.py
├── Input: data/document-index/index.jsonl (unsummarized entries)
├── Processing: Batch by domain, 100K per batch
├── Extract text: pdfplumber for PDFs, python-docx for DOCX, etc.
├── Summarize: via API call (Gemini preferred for cost)
└── Output: Update index.jsonl with summary field
```

## Dependencies

- #1653 (marine taxonomy v2) — provides subdomain context for marine docs
- #1643 (OCR parser) — SKIPPED/PUnted, skip image-based docs
- #1294 (TDD fixtures) — downstream, uses summarization results

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Batch 1: 100K docs summarized, quality score > 80%
- [ ] All 4 batches completed: 394K docs summarized
- [ ] Overall summarization rate: 90%+
- [ ] Quality report generated after each batch
- [ ] Resume capability: interrupted batches can continue

body:
## Problem

The marine taxonomy classification (#1653) revealed that 195,687 of 217,117 marine documents (90.1%) are DWG/DXF CAD drawings. These cannot be classified by filename/path keywords and require CAD metadata extraction.

## Scope

- **195,687 files** across DWG and DXF formats
- Located in `/mnt/ace/` under project and standards directories
- Currently tagged as `cad_drawing` in `data/document-index/marine-subdomain-tags.yaml`

## What CAD metadata can provide

| Metadata | Classification Value |
|---|---|
| Layer names | Subdomain hint (mooring, riser, subsea, etc.) |
| Block references | Equipment/component identification |
| Title blocks | Drawing title, discipline, project code |
| XREF paths | System relationships and scope |
| File paths | Project context (current marine taxonomy already uses this) |

## Suggested approach

1. **Phase 1: Proof of concept — 100 files**
   - Use `ezdxf` library for DXF files
   - Use `ODAFileConverter` or `libredwg` for DWG files
   - Extract title blocks, layer names, block references
   - Map to marine subdomain taxonomy keywords

2. **Phase 2: Classification logic**
   - Map layer names → subdomain keywords
   - Map block patterns → engineering context
   - Multi-tag support (drawings often span multiple subdomains)

3. **Phase 3: Batch processing**
   - Process all 195K files
   - Update `marine-subdomain-tags.yaml` with CAD-extracted subdomains
   - Generate classification report

## Tools to evaluate

| Tool | Format | License | Linux Support |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ezdxf` | DXF | MIT (Python) | Yes |
| `libredwg` | DWG | GPL 3 | Yes |
| `ODA File Converter` | DWG/DXF | Free (proprietary) | Yes |
| `Teigha` | DWG | Commercial | Yes |

## Dependencies

- Depends on: #1653 (marine taxonomy v2 — completed)
- Enables: #1769 (Phase B summarization — CAD docs will have subdomain tags)
- Related: digitalmodel CAD module development

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Prototype: Extract metadata from 10+ sample DWG/DXF files
- [ ] Classification: 50%+ of CAD drawings assigned to at least one subdomain
- [ ] Pipeline: Script processes 100+ files/hour
- [ ] Report: Updated taxonomy YAML with CAD-extracted subdomains
title: Build CAD metadata extraction pipeline for 195K marine DWG/DXF files

body:
## Summary

Audit GPU/CPU specs on all non-primary machines to determine which can run Ollama with useful models (>2B params on GPU), then install and configure on the best candidates.

## Dependency
- Blocked by workspace-hub#1921 (ace-linux-1 feasibility assessment must complete first)

## Why This Matters

ace-linux-1 has a GTX 750 Ti with only 2 GB VRAM -- it can only run 0.6B-1.2B models on GPU. The strategic value of Ollama comes from running 4B+ models on machines with better GPUs for real local LLM work.

## GPU Audit Results (Known)

| Machine | GPU | VRAM | CUDA Cap | Verdict |
|---------|-----|------|----------|---------|
| **ace-linux-1** (dev-primary) | GTX 750 Ti | 2 GB | 5.0 | Tiny models only (0.6B-1.2B). Good for embeddings. |
| **ace-linux-2** (dev-secondary) | NVIDIA T400 | 4 GB | 7.5 | Can run 3B-4B models. Decent for light inference. |
| **licensed-win-1** | **Possibly RTX 3060 12GB** | TBD | TBD | If confirmed: PRIMARY Ollama runner. 8B-14B models. |
| **licensed-win-2** | Possibly RTX 3060 12GB | TBD | TBD | If confirmed: SECONDARY Ollama runner. |

## Priority Order for Installation

1. **RTX 3060 12GB machine (whichever Windows machine has it) -- PRIORITY #1**
   - Can run 8B-14B models on GPU at good speed
   - qwen3.5:14b (~14GB Q4) would need quantization or CPU offload
   - qwen3.5:8b (~5GB Q4) fits comfortably
   - qwen3-coder:7b (~5GB) for code work
   - devstral:small-2 (~14B, MoE) for reasoning
   - Multiple 3B-4B models simultaneously

2. **ace-linux-2 (T400 4GB) -- PRIORITY #2**
   - qwen3.5:4b (~3GB Q4) fits
   - llama3.2:3b (~2GB) fits
   - nomic-embed-text for embeddings pipeline
   - Good for concurrent embedding work

3. **licensed-win-2 -- PRIORITY #3**
   - Needs audit first
   - If RTX 3060 12GB, same potential as #1

## Phase 1: GPU Audit

- [ ] licensed-win-1: Confirm if RTX 3060 12GB (the machine you mentioned has it). Run `nvidia-smi` in PowerShell
- [ ] licensed-win-2: Run `nvidia-smi` in PowerShell to confirm GPU specs
- [ ] Document results in `docs/ops/ollama-machine-audit.md`

## Phase 2: Install on Primary Candidate (RTX 3060 12GB machine)

### Windows Install
- [ ] Download from https://ollama.com/download
- [ ] Run: `winget install Ollama.Ollama` or install manually
- [ ] Verify: `ollama --version`
- [ ] Add to PATH and configure auto-start
- [ ] Pull models: `ollama pull qwen3.5:8b`, `ollama pull nomic-embed-text`, `ollama pull qwen3-coder:7b`
- [ ] Test API: `curl http://localhost:11434/api/generate -d "{\"model\":\"qwen3.5:8b\",\"prompt\":\"hello\"}"`

## Phase 3: Install on ace-linux-2 (T400 4GB)

- [ ] `curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh`
- [ ] Install systemd service for auto-start
- [ ] Pull models: `ollama pull qwen3.5:4b`, `ollama pull nomic-embed-text`
- [ ] Test inference and embedding

## Phase 4: Ecosystem Integration

- [ ] Design shared embedding pipeline: each machine generates embeddings locally, syncs index to workspace-hub
- [ ] Configure Ollama API (port 11434) as local fallback for AI tools
- [ ] Document `ollama-integration.md` in docs/ops/
- [ ] Add to scheduled tasks: nightly model pull + health check

## Model Selection Matrix

| VRAM Available | Recommended Models | Use Case |
|----------------|--------------------|----------|
| 2 GB (ace-linux-1) | qwen3.5:0.6b, nomic-embed-text | Embeddings, basic tasks |
| 4 GB (ace-linux-2) | qwen3.5:4b, llama3.2:3b | Summarization, light reasoning |
| 12 GB (RTX 3060) | qwen3.5:8b, qwen3-coder:7b, devstral:small-2, phi4-mini:3.8b | Code review, reasoning, doc extraction |

## Acceptance Criteria

1. GPU audit complete for all 3 non-primary machines
2. Ollama installed on RTX 3060 machine with at least 8B model running on GPU
3. Ollama installed on ace-linux-2 with 3B-4B model running on GPU
4. Integration plan written with specific next steps
5. Embedding pipeline design completed

## Target Repos
workspace-hub

## References
- workspace-hub#1921 (parent feasibility issue)
- https://ollama.com/download
- https://docs.ollama.com/installation
- docs/ops/scheduled-tasks.md
title: infra: Ollama ecosystem rollout -- GPU audit (ace-linux-2 has T400 4GB, win machines need RTX 3060 12GB audit)

body:
## Summary

Investigate, assess, and (where hardware allows) install [Ollama](https://ollama.com/) in our machine ecosystem to enable offline/fallback local LLM inference, embedding generation for doc-intelligence, and cost-effective batch processing.

## Why

| Benefit | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Zero-cost inference | No subscription burn for local runs (batch doc extraction, embedding, pre-screening) |
| Privacy | Client data never leaves machine (matches DATA_RESIDENCE_POLICY.md) |
| Continuity | Fallback when Claude/Codex/Gemini are throttled or offline |
| Embedding pipeline | Run `nomic-embed-text` or similar for local semantic search across workspace-hub docs |

## Machine Inventory & GPU Assessment

| Machine | GPU | VRAM | CPU | RAM | Free Disk | Verdict |
|---------|-----|------|-----|-----|-----------|---------|
| **ace-linux-1** (dev-primary) | GTX 750 Ti | 2 GB (Compute Cap 5.0) | Xeon E5-2630 v3 (32T) | 32 GB (~22 free) | 123 GB | Can run 0.6B-1.2B models on GPU. Larger models need CPU fallback (slow but functional). |
| **ace-linux-2** (dev-secondary) | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD | Needs audit. If GPU >= 4 GB, becomes primary Ollama runner. |
| **licensed-win-1** | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD | Needs audit. Windows-native install supported. |
| **licensed-win-2** | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD | Needs audit. Windows-native install supported. |

## ace-linux-1 Capability

### GPU-eligible models (fits 2 GB VRAM)
| Model | Q4 Size | Notes |
|-------|---------|-------|
| qwen3.5:0.6b | ~600 MB | Fast, decent for basic tasks |
| lfm2.5-thinking:1.2b | ~1.2 GB | Thinking model, marginal fit |
| qwen3.5:1.7b | ~1.2 GB | Marginal fit, may swap |

### CPU fallback models (uses system RAM)
| Model | Q4 Size | Expected Speed (32-core Xeon) |
|-------|---------|-------------------------------|
| qwen3.5:4b | ~3 GB | Slow but usable |
| phi4-mini:3.8b | ~2.5 GB | Moderate |
| llama3.2:3b | ~2 GB | Moderate |
| nomic-embed-text | ~274 MB | Fast, ideal for embeddings |

## Phases

### Phase 1: Install & Verify on ace-linux-1
- [ ] `curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh`
- [ ] Verify: `ollama --version`
- [ ] Pull small model: `ollama pull qwen3.5:0.6b`
- [ ] Test inference: `ollama run qwen3.5:0.6b "Hello, world"`
- [ ] Pull embedding model: `ollama pull nomic-embed-text`
- [ ] Verify embedding API: `curl http://localhost:11434/api/embed`

### Phase 2: Integration Planning
- [ ] Design embedding pipeline for workspace-hub doc intelligence (replaces or complements existing semantic search)
- [ ] Design offline fallback workflow (detect Claude/Codex/Gemini throttle, route to Ollama)
- [ ] Document config: systemd service, data dirs, model storage location

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Ollama installed and running on ace-linux-1 as a systemd service
2. At least one chat model and one embedding model verified working
3. Hardware assessment completed for all 4 machines in ecosystem
4. Phase 2 integration plan written to `docs/ops/ollama-integration.md`
5. Machine-by-machine GPU audit results documented

## Target Repos
workspace-hub (ops docs), all machines (installation)

## References
- https://ollama.com
- https://docs.ollama.com
- docs/ops/scheduled-tasks.md (machine roles)
- docs/BUSINESS_BRAIN.md (machine inventory)
title: infra: Ollama local LLM runtime -- feasibility assessment and ace-linux-1 install

body:
## Context

Bridge script syncs: Hermes → .claude/memory/ → all machines via git pull.

Missing: Windows Claude sessions generate auto-memory corrections (feedback files, working-style updates, project context) that stay local to Windows. These enrichments never reach the repo or Hermes machine.

## Problem

Unidirectional sync:
- Linux Hermes → repo ✅
- Windows → repo ❌

User corrections made on Windows (e.g. 'use python not uv run', project context updates) are lost when Windows is reformatted or user switches machines.

## Task

Design and implement bidirectional sync:
1. Windows-side: post-session script that copies new/changed .claude/memory/ topic files to repo
2. Or: git-tracked Claude memory directory that both machines write to
3. Merge strategy: when both machines edit same topic file, preserve both changes
4. Test on licensed-win-1

## Acceptance Criteria

- Windows corrections appear in .claude/memory/topics/ after git push
- No merge conflicts when both machines update different topic files
- Health-check detects when Windows last synced
title: memory: Claude Code auto-memory sync from Windows back to repo

body:
## Context

Bridge script pushes to .claude/memory/ on every run. If something goes wrong (bad memory state, bridge bug, corrupted output), there is no rollback.

## Problem

No backup history of .claude/memory/ state between bridge runs.

Currently relies entirely on git history, which requires understanding git to find previous good state.

## Task

1. Add to bridge script: before writing, backup current .claude/memory/ to .claude/memory-backups/${TIMESTAMP}/
2. Retain only last 7 backups (delete older)
3. Create restore script: scripts/memory/restore-memory-backup.sh <timestamp_or_latest>
4. Test: corrupt memory, restore, verify integrity

## Acceptance Criteria

- Backup created before each bridge run
- Restore script works with timestamp or 'latest' keyword
- Only 7 most recent backups retained
- Restore preserves git history consistency
title: memory: backup and rollback mechanism for .claude/memory/

## Scope
Locate, catalog, and prepare the rigid jumper OrcaFlex model and associated Excel workbook for GTM Demo 5 and general reuse.

## Confirmed Reference Models (per user)

The two rigid jumper reference models are:
1. **manifold_to_plet** — 1996m water depth, 10.75" rigid pipe
2. **plet_to_plem** — same config, different connection endpoints

### Model Details (from SZ.yml inspection)

**Software**: OrcaFlex 11.6b, exported from ACMA-ANSYS05

**Line Types** (key ones):
| Name | OD (m) | ID (m) | Type | Notes |
|------|--------|--------|------|-------|
| 10.75"Jumper_wCoat | 0.27305 | 0.18212 | Homogeneous pipe | E=212 GPa, SMYS=360 MPa, coating 76.2mm |

## Task
Configure Telegram bot integration for the Hermes gateway running as a systemd service on ace-linux-1.

## Current State
- Hermes gateway installed and running as system service (v0.7.0)
- `GATEWAY_ALLOW_ALL_USERS=true` already set in `~/.hermes/.env`
- Systemd service properly loads `EnvironmentFile=/home/vamsee/.hermes/.env`
- No messaging platforms configured yet — gateway logs: "No messaging platforms enabled"

## Required Config (add to `~/.hermes/.env`)
```
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=<bot-token-from-botfather>
TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS=<user-chat-id>
```

## Steps
1. Create bot via @BotFather on Telegram → get token
2. Start a chat with the bot, send any message
3. Get chat ID via `curl https://api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/getUpdates`
4. Add both vars to `~/.hermes/.env`
5. `hermes gateway restart --system`
6. Verify gateway logs show Telegram polling started
7. Send test message to verify end-to-end

## Acceptance
- Gateway logs show Telegram bot connected
- Bot responds to a test command (e.g., /status)
- Message delivery to origin works
title: Configure Telegram messaging platform for Hermes gateway on ace-linux-1

Parent: #1839, related to #1512, #1515

Create a nightly cron job that scans for engineering commits without review evidence and auto-creates GitHub issues for remediation.

## What it does

Each day at 06:00:
- Scan all commits from the previous day
- Filter commits with feat/fix/refactor prefixes touching engineering paths
- Cross-reference with scripts/review/results/ for review artifacts
- For each unreviewed engineering commit:
  - Flag in daily report
  - If no review exists after 48 hours, create GitHub issue: "Unreviewed engineering commit: hash -- requires retroactive review"
  - Track unreviewed streak: number of consecutive days without any review

This is the enforcement complement to the engineering-issue-workflow skill. The skill tells agents what to do. The audit catches when they don't do it.

## Deliverables

- [ ] scripts/cron/unreviewed-commit-audit.sh
- [ ] Schedule in config/scheduled-tasks/schedule-tasks.yaml: daily 06:00
- [ ] Output: notes/unreviewed-commits-daily.md
- [ ] Auto-create GitHub issues after 48h unreviewed
- [ ] Escalation: if >5 unreviewed commits in a week, create P1 issue

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Cron runs daily, scans all engineering commits
- [ ] Audit report generated with pass/fail per issue
- [ ] GitHub issues auto-created for overdue unreviewed commits
- [ ] Weekly summary: total reviewed vs unreviewed, compliance rate
- [ ] Escalation triggers at >5 unreviewed per week
title: Cross-review enforcement: nightly audit cron for unreviewed engineering commits
