The current tool-call ceiling hook (.claude/hooks/tool-call-ceiling.sh) warns at 400 calls and blocks at 500. Historical data shows that sessions with >200 calls are almost always problematic:
- 50% of recent sessions exceed 50 tool calls
- 27% exceed 100 tool calls
- Past runaway sessions hit 6,000+ calls before being stopped
- 6.1M total wasted tool calls across 3 WRK items

## Deliverables

- [ ] Update tool-call-ceiling.sh: warn at 160, block at 200
- [ ] Add error-loop breaker: if same error message repeats 3 times, hard stop
- [ ] Add time ceiling: auto-pause after 45 minutes of continuous work
- [ ] Present progress summary to user at ceiling, wait for confirmation to continue
- [ ] Log ceiling events to logs/session-ceiling-events.jsonl
- [ ] Add --override flag for user to extend ceiling for specific sessions

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Warning emitted at 160 tool calls
- [ ] Hard stop at 200 tool calls (not advisory, actual block)
- [ ] Error-loop detection triggers after 3 same-error repeats
- [ ] Time ceiling triggers after 45 minutes
- [ ] User can override with explicit confirmation
- [ ] All ceiling events logged
- [ ] Zero sessions exceed 500 calls in first month
title: Tool-call ceiling: reduce from 500 to 200 with auto-pause and error-loop breaker

body:
Parent: #1839, related to #1515, #1537, #1538

The review enforcement scripts default to WARNING mode, meaning they print a warning but allow the commit/push to proceed. This has resulted in 542 commits since Mar 24 with only 1 review artifact.

## Scripts affected

- scripts/enforcement/require-review-on-push.sh: defaults to WARNING, need REVIEW_GATE_STRICT=1
- .git/hooks/pre-push.sh: review gate is advisory only
- scripts/ai/review_routing_gate.py: routes reviews but does not block

## Current bypasses

- SKIP_REVIEW_GATE=1: bypasses the review gate entirely (logged)
- GIT_PRE_PUSH_SKIP=1: soft bypass of pre-push hook (logged)
- User overrides: agents discover and use these env vars

## Deliverables

- [ ] Set REVIEW_GATE_STRICT=1 as default in CI/gate
- [ ] Remove or require explicit override for SKIP_REVIEW_GATE
- [ ] Add PreToolUse hook to check for plan approval before implementation
- [ ] Add daily cron audit to detect unreviewed engineering commits
- [ ] Create .planning/plan-approved/ directory for approval markers

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] REVIEW_GATE_STRICT=1 by default (not opt-in)
- [ ] Push blocked if no review evidence for engineering commits
- [ ] Bypass requires explicit user confirmation, not just env var
- [ ] Daily audit cron creates issues for unreviewed work
- [ ] Bypass log (logs/hooks/review-gate-bypass.jsonl) has entries showing intentional user-approved bypasses
title: Enable cross-review strict mode -- gate defaults to WARNING not blocking

## Current state

data/document-index/index.jsonl has 647,054 records. ALL show `content_type: unknown` and `summary_done: false`. The metadata was wiped or regenerated at some point. 0% of records have usable metadata.

However: resource-intelligence-maturity.yaml claims 61.9% summary coverage (639,585 summaries across 1,033,933 records). This refers to a DIFFERENT dataset -- the older canonical index, not the current index.jsonl.

## Impact

Agents cannot search for document metadata in index.jsonl -- all searches return unknown. Resource intelligence step (Step 2 of engineering-issue-workflow) is blocked for document lookups.

## Root cause hypothesis

index.jsonl was regenerated from scratch (e.g., dedup or re-crawl) without carrying over metadata from the prior index. The summary data lives somewhere else -- not in the index records themselves.

## Deliverables

- [ ] Diagnose where the summary metadata actually lives
- [ ] Restore or rebuild content_type and summary_done fields
- [ ] Verify index.jsonl + conference-index-batch.jsonl together cover all 1M+ records
- [ ] Add validation test: reject index files with 0% content_type coverage
- [ ] Add index regeneration test: backup metadata before reindex

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] content_type populated for >=90% of records
- [ ] summary_done populated for >=60% of records (matches maturity.yaml claim)
- [ ] No new index regeneration without metadata backup
- [ ] Validation test passes: rejects indexes with 0% coverage
title: Fix document index metadata broken -- 647K records showing unknown content_type

body:
Parent: #1839

Created from engineering-issue-workflow skill v1.1.0 deployment.

Option 1 (skill-based workflow) is the baseline. Layer enforcement if adherence stays below 30% after 2 weeks.

## Option 2: Hermes prefill

Set `~/.hermes/config.yaml`: `prefill_messages_file: 'docs/standards/engineering-workflow-prefill.md'`

The prefill file contains a system message that checks the issue being discussed. If it matches engineering-critical labels, injects the workflow instructions before the agent's first turn.

**Scope:** Hermes only. Requires `hermes chat` invocation.

**Deliverables:**
- [ ] `~/.hermes/config.yaml` with prefill_messages_file
- [ ] `docs/standards/engineering-workflow-prefill.md`
- [ ] Test: verify Hermes session injects workflow on engineering issue

## Option 3: Claude Code hooks

### SessionStart hook
- Add to `.claude/settings.json` SessionStart
- Detects if engineering-critical GitHub issue is referenced
- Injects workflow into session context before first turn

### PreToolUse hook
- When agent tries Write/Edit/Agent AND no plan approval exists, block with message
- Checks for plan approval marker: `.planning/plan-approved/${ISSUE_NUM}.md`
- If marker found: allow implementation
- If not found: block and tell agent what to do

**Deliverables:**
- [ ] `.claude/hooks/engineering-workflow-start.sh`
- [ ] `.claude/hooks/engineering-workflow-gate.sh`
- [ ] `.claude/settings.json` update
- [ ] `.planning/plan-approved/` directory structure

## Escalation trigger

If after 2 weeks:
- Engineering commits still ship without plan review
- Cross-review rate stays below 30%
- User reports agents skipping the workflow

Then implement Option 2 and Option 3.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Hermes prefill tested and working
- [ ] Claude Code hooks tested and working
- [ ] Plan approval marker system tested
- [ ] PreToolUse hook blocks implementation before approval
- [ ] Zero engineering commits without plan review after deployment
title: Enforce engineering workflow via Hermes prefill + Claude Code hooks (Option 2+3 of engineering-issue-workflow)

## Scope
Build Demo 5: Deepwater rigid jumper installation analysis comparing 2 vessel sizes.

## Parameter Matrix
- **Vessels**: Large CSV (5000te crane) vs Medium CSV (2500te crane) -- SAME as Demo 3
- **Jumpers**: 20m, 40m, 60m, 80m, 100m length (all 8" X65)
- **Water depths**: 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000m
- **Total**: 2 x 5 x 6 = 60 cases

## Engineering Basis
- DNV-RP-H103 (2011) Sec 4 -- splash zone, lowering
- DNV-ST-N001 (2021) -- marine operations
- Same installation phases as Demo 3 (mudmat) but with:
  - Pipe body stress during lift (bending at lift points)
  - Longer lowering times (deeper water + heavier cable)
  - Cable weight in water becomes significant at depth
  - Slamming on cylindrical body (different C_s than flat mudmat)

## Charts (5)
1. **Go/No-Go matrix**: vessel x jumper length x depth (hero chart)
2. **Crane utilisation vs water depth** (per vessel, parametric jumper)
3. **Lowering time vs water depth** (practical operations planning)
4. **Pipe stress during lift** (bending utilisation vs jumper length)
5. **Cable weight contribution** (pct of total hook load vs depth)

## Input Data
- `data/csv_hlv_vessels.json` (existing)
- `data/rigid_jumpers.json` (existing)

## Output
- `output/demo_05_jumper_installation_report.html`
- `results/demo_05_jumper_installation_results.json`
- Updates to comparison matrices

## Dependencies
- #1798 for real vessel data (can proceed with generic data)
- shares calculation code with Demo 3 -- same installation phases

## Build Order
Demo 3 -> Demo 5 -> Demo 1 -> Demo 4

## Reuse from Demo 3
Demo 5 shares 80 pct of Demo 3's phase calculators (liftoff, splash zone, lowering).
Only new: pipe bending during lift, cylindrical slamming, lowering time.

body:
## Scope
Build Demo 4: Shallow water S-lay pipeline installation analysis comparing 2 vessel types.

## Parameter Matrix
- **Vessels**: Large PLV (600te tensioner, 140m stinger) vs Shallow Water Barge (250te tensioner, 80m stinger)
- **Pipe sizes**: 8", 12", 16", 20", 24" OD (X65)
- **Water depths**: 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30m
- **Total**: 2 x 5 x 6 = 60 cases

## Engineering Basis
- DNV-ST-F101 (2021) Sec 5 -- Installation
- API RP 1111 (2015) -- installation stress checks
- S-lay catenary mechanics:
  - Overbend: pipe curvature at stinger departure
  - Sagbend: pipe curvature near touchdown
  - Top tension: horizontal + vertical components
  - Stinger tip departure angle
- Shallow water challenges:
  - Low catenary parameter -> tight sagbend radius
  - Large pipes need high tension -> tensioner limit
  - Very shallow (<10m) may need special stinger config

## Charts (5)
1. **Go/No-Go matrix**: vessel x pipe size x depth (hero chart)
2. **Sagbend stress utilisation heatmap**: pipe x depth (per vessel)
3. **Required tension vs water depth** (per pipe size)
4. **Stinger angle requirement vs water depth**
5. **Vessel head-to-head**: max pipe size installable at each depth

## Input Data
- `data/pipelay_vessels.json` (existing, swap real data later)
- `data/pipelines.json` (existing)

## Output
- `output/demo_04_shallow_pipelay_report.html`
- `results/demo_04_shallow_pipelay_results.json`
- Updates to comparison matrices

## Dependencies
- Catenary solver: marine_ops.marine_analysis.catenary.solver exists but needs verification

## Build Order
Demo 3 -> Demo 5 -> Demo 1 -> Demo 4

body:
## Scope
Build Demo 3: Deepwater mudmat installation analysis comparing 2 vessel sizes across water depths, mudmat sizes, and sea states.

## Design Decisions (confirmed by user)
- Splash zone: simplified screening method (OrcaFlex will govern for final results)
- Cable weight: will be sourced from existing vessel catalogs / models
- Go/No-Go thresholds: per DNV-ST-N001 utilisation factors (not arbitrary ratios)
- Hero chart: two side-by-side heatmaps (one per vessel)
- Seabed: assume soft clay (50 kPa bearing), add sensitivity chart for varying soil
- DAF: applied where needed; OrcaFlex simulation governs actual results
- Reference sea state: Hs sweep (not fixed at single value)

## Parameter Matrix
- **Vessels**: Large CSV (5000te crane) vs Medium CSV (2500te crane)
- **Water depths**: 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000m
- **Mudmats**: 50te (6x6m), 100te (8x10m), 200te (12x14m)
- **Hs sweep**: 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0m (5 sea states)
- **Total**: 2 x 6 x 3 x 5 = **180 cases**

## Engineering Basis
- DNV-RP-H103 (2011) -- Modelling and Analysis of Marine Operations
  - Sec 4.3.4: Slamming force at water entry
  - Sec 4.3.5: Varying buoyancy in splash zone
  - Sec 4.4: Lowering through water column
- DNV-ST-N001 (2021) -- Marine Operations and Marine Warranty
- Existing modules: `marine_ops.installation.models` (Structure, Vessel, CraneCurve, InstallationCriteria)
- Existing modules: `marine_ops.installation.vessel_screening` (screen_vessels)
- Existing modules: `marine_ops.installation.splash_zone` (slamming_force, varying_buoyancy)
- DNV utilisation factors from document intelligence reports (reference for thresholds)

## Installation Phases (per case)
1. **Lift-off**: hook load = mass_air x g x DAF_lift; check vs crane SWL
2. **In-air**: tilt check from CoG offset + sling geometry
3. **Splash zone** (CRITICAL): slamming, varying buoyancy, drag; max hook load
4. **Lowering**: cable tension = W_sub + W_cable_sub(depth); snap risk check
5. **Landing**: bearing pressure vs seabed allowable

## Charts (5)
1. **Go/No-Go heatmap** (HERO): side-by-side (Large CSV | Medium CSV), depth x mudmat, colour-coded by go/no-go/marginal
2. **Crane utilisation vs water depth**: per mudmat, both vessels overlaid
3. **DAF vs water depth**: splash zone + lowering phases
4. **Max Hs limit vs structure weight**: operability envelope per vessel
5. **Seabed bearing sensitivity**: go/no-go count vs bearing pressure (5-200 kPa)

## Sensitivity Chart (bonus #6)
- X-axis: Seabed bearing pressure (5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 kPa)
- Y-axis: Number of cases GO / total cases
- Lines: per vessel
- Shows how soil assumptions drive operability

## Input Data
- `data/csv_hlv_vessels.json` (existing)
- `data/mudmat_structures.json` (existing)

## Output
- `output/demo_03_mudmat_installation_report.html`
- `results/demo_03_mudmat_installation_results.json` (ALL data cached, --from-cache ready per #1832)
- Updates to `results/vessel_comparison_matrix.json`
- Updates to `results/structure_comparison_matrix.json`

## Script Structure
```
demo_03_deepwater_mudmat_installation.py
- Data loaders (JSON -> Python objects)
- Phase calculators (5 functions: liftoff, in_air, splash, lowering, landing)
- Parametric runner (180 cases)
- Chart builders (5 + 1 sensitivity)
- Report assembly (GTMReportBuilder)
- Results export (JSON)
- --from-cache support
```

## Dependencies
- #1798 for real vessel data (can proceed with generic data now)
- report_template.py (done)
- Requires DNV utilisation factor review from doc-intelligence reports

## Parent
#1800

## Part of build order
Demo 3 -> Demo 5 -> Demo 1 -> Demo 4

body:
## Scope
Build Demo 1: DNV-RP-F105 freespan/VIV screening for pipelines and rigid jumpers.

## Parameter Matrix
- **Pipelines**: 8", 12", 16" OD x span lengths 10m to 80m (8 steps) x 5 current velocities x 4 gap ratios = ~480 cases
- **Jumpers**: 5 jumper lengths (20-100m) x span lengths 5-40m x 5 currents = ~200 cases
- **Total**: ~680 cases

## Engineering Basis
- DNV-RP-F105 (2006) Free Spanning Pipelines
- Natural frequency calculation (Sec 3.3, Eq 3.6)
- Inline and crossflow VIV onset screening (Table 3-2)
- Reduced velocity screening: V_R = U / (f_n x D)
- Static deflection at midspan
- Soil stiffness effect on boundary conditions

## Charts (5)
1. **Natural frequency vs span length** (parametric pipe size lines)
2. **VIV onset screening** (inline + crossflow threshold overlay)
3. **Max allowable span heatmap** (pipe size x current velocity)
4. **Pass/fail matrix** (pipe x span x current x gap ratio)
5. **Jumper vs pipeline comparison** (same span range, different stiffness)

## Input Data
- `data/pipelines.json` (pipe properties)
- `data/rigid_jumpers.json` (jumper properties)
- `data/freespan_scenarios.json` (currents, spans, soils, gap ratios)

## Output
- `output/demo_01_freespan_report.html`
- `results/demo_01_freespan_results.json` (ALL data cached, --from-cache ready per #1832)

## New Module Needed
- `src/digitalmodel/structural/analysis/freespan_viv.py` (DNV-RP-F105 calcs) -- OR self-contained calcs in demo script
- NOTE: `subsea/pipeline/free_span/` already exists with 9 modules:
  - natural_frequency, onset_screening, viv_response, fatigue_damage,
    allowable_length, wave_velocity, weibull_current, bilinear_sn, models

## Dependencies
- None (no vessel data needed)
- report_template.py (done)
- Input databases (done)

## Build Order
Demo 3 -> Demo 5 -> Demo 1 -> Demo 4

#1800

## Problem

Hermes has `deepseek` in its auth registry, model list, and provider config, but the CLI `--provider` argparse choices in `main.py` do not include it. We patched this locally (line ~4038) but it should be fixed upstream.

## Details

File: `hermes_cli/main.py`
Location: `chat_parser.add_argument("--provider", choices=[...])`

Current (missing deepseek):
```python
choices=["auto", "openrouter", "nous", "openai-codex", "copilot-acp", "copilot", "anthropic", "huggingface", "zai", "kimi-coding", "minimax", "minimax-cn", "kilocode"]
```

Should be:
```python
choices=["auto", "openrouter", "nous", "openai-codex", "copilot-acp", "copilot", "anthropic", "huggingface", "zai", "kimi-coding", "minimax", "minimax-cn", "kilocode", "deepseek"]
```

Or better: dynamically build choices from `PROVIDER_REGISTRY.keys()` so new providers are automatically included.

## Action
- Open PR or issue on github.com/openclaw/openclaw
- Until merged, maintain local patch per #1867

## References
- #1867 (provider maintenance)
- #1838 (parent)

## Problem

Three free-tier providers remain unconfigured. Each takes 5 minutes to set up and adds resilience + free model access.

## Providers to Add

### 1. HuggingFace (free inference)
- Sign up: huggingface.co/settings/tokens
- Env var: HF_TOKEN
- Models: Qwen3.5-397B, DeepSeek-V3.2, Kimi-K2.5, MiMo-V2-Flash
- Good for: research subagents, code review

### 2. OpenRouter (free tier + aggregator)
- Sign up: openrouter.ai
- Env var: OPENROUTER_API_KEY
- Models: 100+ models, many free (Gemma, Llama, Mistral, Qwen)
- Good for: universal fallback, access to any model

### 3. Z.AI / GLM (free tier)
- Sign up: open.bigmodel.cn
- Env var: ZAI_API_KEY
- Models: GLM-5, GLM-5-turbo
- Good for: alternative reasoning model, Chinese language tasks

## For Each Provider

```bash
# 1. Sign up and get API key
# 2. Add to .env:
echo "HF_TOKEN=your-key" >> ~/.hermes/.env
echo "OPENROUTER_API_KEY=your-key" >> ~/.hermes/.env
echo "ZAI_API_KEY=your-key" >> ~/.hermes/.env

# 3. Add aliases to ~/.bash_aliases:
alias h-hf='hermes chat --provider huggingface -m Qwen/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B'
alias h-router='hermes chat --provider openrouter -m qwen/qwen3.6-plus:free'
alias h-glm='hermes chat --provider zai -m glm-5'

# 4. Verify: hermes auth list
```

- [ ] OPENROUTER_API_KEY configured and tested
- [ ] ZAI_API_KEY configured and tested

body:
## Problem

Several Hermes customizations we applied today are fragile — they will break on Hermes updates.

## Items That Need Maintenance

### 1. DeepSeek CLI Provider Patch (breaks on update)
We patched `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/main.py` to add `deepseek` to the `--provider` argparse choices. This is a Hermes bug — deepseek is in the auth registry and model list but missing from CLI choices.

**File:** `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/hermes_cli/main.py` line ~4038
**Patch:** Added `"deepseek"` to the choices list

After any `hermes update`, re-apply:
```python
# In chat_parser.add_argument("--provider", choices=[...])
# Add "deepseek" to the list
```

Or upstream a PR to openclaw/openclaw to fix this permanently.

### 2. Shell Aliases (~/.bash_aliases)
These survive updates but need to stay in sync with model names:
```
h, h-opus, h-sonnet, h-gpt, h-o3
h-copilot, h-copilot-gemini, h-copilot-gpt
h-deepseek, h-deepseek-reason
h-which
```
If model names change (e.g. opus-4-7), aliases need updating.

### 3. Config Settings (~/.hermes/config.yaml)
These may get reset or reformatted on update:
- `fallback_providers` chain (copilot → openai-codex)
- `credential_pool_strategies` (round_robin, fallback)
- `smart_model_routing` enabled with copilot/gemini-2.5-pro
- `delegation` model/provider (copilot)
- Config backup at: `~/.hermes/config.yaml.bak.20260404`

### 4. GH_TOKEN in .env
The `gh auth token` value may expire or rotate. If Copilot provider stops working, refresh:
```bash
echo "GH_TOKEN=$(gh auth token)" > /tmp/gh_update
grep -v GH_TOKEN ~/.hermes/.env > /tmp/env_clean
cat /tmp/env_clean /tmp/gh_update > ~/.hermes/.env
```

## Post-Update Checklist

After any `hermes update`:
- [ ] Verify `hermes chat --provider deepseek` still works (re-patch if not)
- [ ] Verify `hermes auth list` shows all 4 providers
- [ ] Verify fallback chain: `grep fallback ~/.hermes/config.yaml`
- [ ] Verify delegation: `grep -A3 delegation ~/.hermes/config.yaml`
- [ ] Test one alias from each provider: h-opus, h-copilot, h-deepseek, h-gpt

## References
- #1838 (parent — AI credit utilization governance)
- #1856 (Hermes quick model switching)
- DeepSeek patch: main.py line ~4038, argparse choices list

body:
## Problem

Our workflow lacks enforced hard stops where the user must review and approve before the agent continues. Evidence from session trend data is damning:

- **6.1M wasted tool calls** across 3 runaway WRK items (no session abort mechanism)
- **4,740 high-churn sessions** (100+ tool calls) with no ceiling
- **3% TDD pairing** vs "TDD mandatory" policy — the gate is aspirational, not enforced
- **12-day consecutive** smoke test failures went unnoticed
- **Review gate defaults to warning mode** — bypass trivial with `SKIP_REVIEW_GATE=1`
- **session-start-routine skill lost** during GSD migration cleanup
- **comprehensive-learning skill orphaned** from skills tree (runs via cron but invisible to skill discovery)

The old 20-stage WRK pipeline was removed (4% completion rate). The replacement 6-stage GSD workflow has quality gates but no session-level governance or mandatory user checkpoints.

## Current State

**What exists (stages/workflow):**
- 6-stage workflow: GitHub Issue → Triage → GSD Plan → Implement → Cross-Review → Close
- GSD framework with 56 workflow files (.claude/get-shit-done/workflows/)
- AGENTS.md hard gates: (1) Plan before acting, (2) TDD mandatory
- Trust Architecture doc (docs/governance/TRUST-ARCHITECTURE.md) — A/B/C action classification
- Pre-push review gate (warning mode by default)
- Self-learning loop: session signals → 3AM analysis → 5AM reflection
- Nightly comprehensive-learning pipeline (scripts/cron/)

**What exists (skills/docs):**
- agent-usage-optimizer skill (sub-skills all archived)
- AI_REVIEW_ROUTING_POLICY.md (3-provider adversarial review)
- work-queue-workflow.md (6-stage reference)
- 11 session handoff docs in docs/handoffs/

**What is missing:**
- No session hard-stop / tool-call ceiling — runaway sessions burn credits unchecked
- No mandatory user review checkpoint between Plan and Implement stages
- No session-start-routine skill (lost during migration)
- No session-corpus-audit capability (never built)
- No inter-session continuity gate (sessions dont validate prior state before starting new work)
- TDD gate not enforced via pre-commit hook
- comprehensive-learning disconnected from skill tree
- cross-review-policy, dev-workflow, codex-review, gemini-review skills referenced but never created

## Proposal: Hermes-Orchestrated Workflow with Hard Stops

### Phase 1: Session Lifecycle Gates

**Mandatory User Hard Stops (cannot be bypassed):**

```
SESSION START
  ├─ [GATE 1: Session Init] Auto — load context, check prior state, validate env
  │
  ├─ [GATE 2: Work Selection] Auto — triage issues, present options
  │
  ├─ [HARD STOP 1: Plan Approval] ◆ USER MUST APPROVE plan before any implementation
  │
  ├─ [GATE 3: TDD Red] Auto — tests written, all failing (enforced, not advisory)
  │
  ├─ [GATE 4: Implementation] Auto — make tests pass
  │
  ├─ [GATE 5: Cross-Review] Auto — route to Codex+Gemini per routing policy
  │
  ├─ [HARD STOP 2: Review Verdict] ◆ USER REVIEWS cross-review results, approves/rejects
  │
  ├─ [GATE 6: Commit+Push] Auto — commit, push, close issue
  │
  └─ [HARD STOP 3: Session Close] ◆ USER CONFIRMS session summary, next priorities
SESSION END
```

3 hard stops per session. Everything else is automated by Hermes.

### Phase 2: Runaway Protection

- **Tool-call ceiling**: Auto-pause at 200 tool calls, require user confirmation to continue
- **Time ceiling**: Auto-pause after 45 minutes of continuous work, present progress summary
- **Error loop breaker**: If same error repeats 3x, hard stop — do not retry
- **Credit guard**: Pause if estimated session cost exceeds threshold (ties to #1838)

### Phase 3: Restore Lost Infrastructure

- Rebuild `session-start-routine` skill with: pre-flight checks, context loading, prior-state validation, tool readiness
- Create `session-corpus-audit` skill: analyze session quality trends, identify high-churn patterns, report waste
- Promote `comprehensive-learning` into skills tree (currently cron-only, invisible)
- Create `cross-review-policy` skill (policy exists as doc, not as actionable skill)
- Create `dev-workflow` skill encapsulating the full Issue→Close lifecycle

### Phase 4: Hermes as Workflow Orchestrator

Hermes (not Claude Code directly) orchestrates the session lifecycle:
- Hermes manages gate transitions and hard-stop enforcement
- Hermes dispatches to Claude/Codex/Gemini per routing matrix (#1838)
- Hermes tracks session metrics (tool calls, time, credits, gate pass/fail)
- Hermes generates session report at close (ties to gsd-session-report)
- Inter-session continuity: Hermes checks last session state before starting new work

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] 3 mandatory user hard stops implemented and non-bypassable
- [ ] Tool-call ceiling (200) with auto-pause operational
- [ ] TDD gate enforced via pre-commit hook (not advisory)

title: Workflow hard-stops and session governance — Hermes-orchestrated lifecycle with enforced gates

body:
## Problem

We pay for 4 AI subscriptions monthly (~$260/mo total):
- Claude Max: $200 (heavy use — near quota ceiling)
- OpenAI/Codex x2: $20 each (moderate use — review/coding tasks)
- Google Gemini Pro: $19.99 (severely underutilized — 162MB session data sitting idle)

Claude absorbs nearly all work. Gemini credits go to waste. No systematic routing ensures the right provider handles the right task type ("horses for courses").

## Current State

**What exists:**
- `agent-usage-optimizer` skill — reads `~/.cache/agent-quota.json` but sub-skills all archived
- `model-selection` skill — Claude-only tier matrix (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku), no cross-provider routing
- `usage-optimization` skill — script-first patterns, but Claude-centric
- `AI_AGENT_USAGE_OPTIMIZATION_PLAN.md` — comprehensive doc but not operationalized
- `AI_REVIEW_ROUTING_POLICY.md` — 3-provider review policy (Claude/Codex/Gemini)
- Related: #1803 (context-budget audit), #1804 (MCP token-optimizer eval), #1802 (batch-at-stop)

**What is missing:**
- No cross-provider credit/quota dashboard
- No automated routing that considers remaining credits per provider
- Gemini has no task-type assignment beyond occasional review duty
- No credit exhaustion alerts or rate-limit handling
- agent-usage-optimizer sub-skills all archived — effectively dead

## Proposal

### 1. Horses-for-Courses Task Routing Matrix

Map task types to optimal providers based on strengths + available credits:

| Task Type | Primary | Fallback | Rationale |
|-----------|---------|----------|-----------|
| Code implementation | Claude | Codex | Deepest context, best code quality |
| Code review (adversarial) | Codex + Gemini | Claude | Independent eyes, cheaper |
| Research & literature | Gemini | Claude | 1M+ context window, web grounding |
| Document analysis (large) | Gemini | Claude | Long-context advantage |
| Data analysis / viz | Gemini | Claude | Good at structured data |
| Plan review (adversarial) | All three | — | Policy already mandates this |
| Quick lookups / triage | Gemini | Codex | Preserve Claude quota for heavy work |
| Overnight batch prompts | Split evenly | — | Distribute load across all 3 |

### 2. Credit Utilization Tracking

- Script: `scripts/ai/credit-utilization-tracker.sh` — daily snapshot of usage per provider
- Dashboard: weekly summary in `notes/ai-credit-utilization-weekly.md`
- Alert: warn when any provider is <20% utilized at week midpoint

### 3. Operationalize agent-usage-optimizer

- Restore or rewrite archived sub-skills with cross-provider awareness
- Update model-selection skill to include Gemini and Codex routing
- Add Gemini task-type capabilities to the routing logic

### 4. Gemini-Specific Activation

- Route all research/literature tasks to Gemini by default
- Use Gemini for large-document ingestion (1M context advantage)
- Assign Gemini as primary reviewer for non-code artifacts (plans, docs)
- Use Gemini for overnight batch prompt #2 or #3 (currently all Claude)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Task routing matrix implemented in agent-usage-optimizer skill
- [ ] Credit utilization tracker script running weekly
- [ ] Gemini handles >=25% of total AI tasks (up from ~5%)
- [ ] model-selection skill updated with cross-provider routing
- [ ] Overnight batch prompts distribute across all 3 providers
- [ ] No provider at <15% weekly utilization (excluding rate limits)

## References

- agent-usage-optimizer skill (sub-skills archived)
- model-selection skill (.claude/skills/ai/optimization/model-selection/)
- usage-optimization skill (.claude/skills/ai/optimization/usage-optimization/)
- docs/modules/ai/AI_AGENT_USAGE_OPTIMIZATION_PLAN.md
- docs/standards/AI_REVIEW_ROUTING_POLICY.md
- #1803 #1804 #1802 #1782

body:
## Purpose
Build a parametric pipeline CAPEX cost model for field development trade-off studies.

## Cost Categories
1. **Linepipe steel**: weight/m × unit cost (USD/te, varies by grade/size)
2. **Anti-corrosion coating**: FBE, 3LPP, 3LPE (cost/m by OD)
3. **Concrete weight coating**: CWC thickness-dependent (cost/m)
4. **Weld + NDT**: per joint, varies by WT and grade
5. **Installation**: vessel day rate × duration (from lay rate and length)
6. **Survey & positioning**: fixed + per-km
7. **Shore approach**: HDD/trenching/pull-in (lump sum estimates)
8. **Tie-in & commissioning**: per-end estimates

## Key Outputs
- Total installed cost (CAPEX) per candidate pipe size
- Cost breakdown by category
- Sensitivity: cost vs pipe size, cost vs WT, cost vs vessel
- Cost/km metric for comparison

## Data Sources
- Industry-average unit costs (parametric, not project-specific)
- Vessel day rates from public market data (Westwood, Rystad)
- Steel price indices (Metal Bulletin, CRU)

## Location
`src/digitalmodel/economics/pipeline_cost_model.py`

## Parent
#1834 (Field Development Skill)
title: MODULE: Pipeline CAPEX cost model (steel + coating + installation)

## Purpose
Implement shore approach analysis for subsea pipeline landfalls.

## Scope
- **HDD (Horizontal Directional Drilling)**: pull force calculation, drill path geometry, pipe stress during pull-in
- **Open-cut trenching**: trench stability, pipe lowering, backfill
- **Surface pull-in**: winch force, roller friction, beach section stress
- **Floatation/sinking**: buoyancy control during tow and flooding, CWC sizing

## Key Outputs
- Required pull force [kN] for HDD/pull-in
- Pipe stress during pull (bending + tension + friction)
- Floatation stability: submerged weight profile along route
- Shore approach method recommendation based on conditions

## Code References
- DNV-RP-F110 (2019) -- Global Buckling of Submarine Pipelines
- DNV-RP-F109 (2021) -- On-Bottom Stability
- API RP 1102 -- Pipeline Crossings Under Railroads/Highways (for HDD)
- ASCE Manual of Practice 108 -- Pipeline Design for Installation by HDD

body:
## Purpose
Implement on-bottom stability analysis per DNV-RP-F109 for subsea pipelines.

## Scope
- Lateral stability under waves + current (absolute stability method)
- Generalized lateral stability method (DNV-RP-F109 Sec 3.3)
- Required submerged weight for stability
- Concrete weight coating (CWC) sizing to achieve target submerged weight
- Pipe-soil interaction (sand, clay) friction factors

## Key Outputs
- Required submerged weight [N/m]
- Required CWC thickness [mm]
- Lateral displacement under design conditions [m]
- Stability check: PASS/FAIL

## Inputs
- Pipe OD, WT, coating
- Current velocity profile (near-bottom)
- Wave orbital velocity (from Hs, Tp, water depth)
- Seabed soil type and properties
- Allowable lateral displacement

## Code Reference
- DNV-RP-F109 (2021) -- On-Bottom Stability Design of Submarine Pipelines

## Location
`src/digitalmodel/structural/analysis/on_bottom_stability.py`

## Parent
#1834 (Field Development Skill)
title: MODULE: On-bottom stability analysis (DNV-RP-F109)

body:
## Vision
Build a field development pipeline engineering skill that connects the full decision chain from reservoir to operations. For a given field, the engineer must choose between close pipe sizes (e.g. 10" vs 12" vs 14") where the decision depends on the interplay of:

### The Decision Chain
```
Reservoir Data
  → Flow Assurance (min ID for throughput, slugging, wax, hydrates)
    → Candidate Pipe Sizes (e.g. 10", 12", 14")
      → Wall Thickness per Code (DNV/API/PD8010 -- narrows WT range)
        → Installation Feasibility
          ├── Deepwater: J-lay or S-lay? Vessel tensioner capacity?
          ├── Shallow water: S-lay barge? Stinger angle limits?
          ├── Shore approach: HDD? Trenching? Pull-in?
          └── Floatation/sinking: submerged weight, on-bottom stability
            → Vessel Selection (which vessels can do it?)
              → Operability (weather windows, seasonal constraints)
                → Cost Comparison (steel + vessel + schedule)
                  → RECOMMENDED PIPE SIZE
```

### What This Skill Should Do
For a given field development scenario, run the FULL cascade:

1. **Pipe Size Screening**
   - Input: throughput requirement, fluid properties, pipeline length, water depth profile
   - Output: candidate pipe sizes that satisfy flow assurance constraints
   - Note: flow assurance is typically done by others; we receive the candidates

2. **Wall Thickness Sizing** (Demo 2 capability)
   - For each candidate size: min WT per code
   - Lifecycle analysis: installation, hydrotest, operation, shutdown
   - Output: WT selection per pipe size

3. **Installation Design** (Demos 3, 4, 5 capabilities)
   - For each size + WT combo: can it be installed?
   - S-lay: sagbend/overbend stress, tension, stinger angle
   - J-lay: departure angle, tension, stability
   - Shore approach: horizontal pull force, buoyancy, trenching
   - Floatation analysis: submerged weight, concrete weight coating needs
   - On-bottom stability: current/wave loading, lateral stability

4. **Vessel Selection**
   - For each installable combo: which vessels from fleet?
   - Tensioner utilisation, crane capacity (for structures)
   - Operability windows (Hs limits from metocean data)

5. **Cost Comparison**
   - Steel cost: weight/m × length × unit cost
   - Coating cost: CWC vs FBE
   - Vessel cost: day rate × installation duration
   - Total installed cost per candidate pipe size

6. **Recommendation Matrix**
   - Side-by-side comparison of all candidates
   - Technical feasibility + cost + schedule
   - Clear recommendation with justification

### Example Output
```
FIELD: Block A Deepwater Export Pipeline
LENGTH: 85 km, WATER DEPTH: 800-1200m

CANDIDATE    WT(DNV)  WT(API)  INSTALL   VESSEL      COST
10" X65     15.1mm   14.2mm   S-lay OK  Vessel A    $42M
12" X65     17.5mm   16.3mm   S-lay OK  Vessel A/B  $51M  ← RECOMMENDED
14" X65     19.8mm   18.7mm   S-lay OK  Vessel B    $63M

Recommendation: 12" provides adequate flow capacity with
the best cost/vessel optionality balance. Both Vessel A and B
can install, giving schedule flexibility.
```

### Existing Capabilities to Leverage
- Wall thickness: `digitalmodel.structural.analysis.wall_thickness` (3 codes)
- Installation catenary: `digitalmodel.marine_ops.marine_analysis.catenary`
- Vessel models: `digitalmodel.marine_ops.installation.models`
- Vessel screening: `digitalmodel.marine_ops.installation.vessel_screening`
- GTM Demo infrastructure: `examples/demos/gtm/` (report template, databases)

### New Modules Needed
- [ ] Flow assurance interface (receive candidate sizes from external tools)
- [ ] Shore approach analysis (HDD pull force, trenching stability)
- [ ] Floatation/sinking analysis (buoyancy, CWC sizing)
- [ ] On-bottom stability (DNV-RP-F109)
- [ ] Pipeline cost model (steel + coating + installation + CAPEX)
- [ ] Field development comparison engine (ties it all together)
- [ ] Interactive field development report template

### GTM Value
This is the KILLER demo for marine installation contractors:
*"Give us your field parameters at 5 PM. By 8 AM we tell you which
pipe size to buy, which vessel to charter, and what it will cost.
With sensitivity analysis on all three."*

### Related Issues
- #1800 GTM Demo master
- #1831 Demo 2 caching
- #1832 --from-cache pattern for all demos

### Priority
High -- this ties all 5 individual demos into a coherent field development story.
title: SKILL: Field Development Pipeline Engineering -- pipe sizing through installation to operations

body:
## Review Findings from Demo 2

### Finding 1: Pressure insensitivity for 16" and 20" pipes
At 16" and 20" with mid-range WT at 500m depth, propagation buckling governs across ALL pressure levels (10-25 MPa). This is technically correct (propagation buckling is D/t-dependent, not pressure-dependent) but may confuse non-specialist viewers who expect different pressures to show different utilisations.

**Suggestion**: Add a tooltip/annotation explaining: *"Propagation buckling is geometry-dependent (D/t ratio), not pressure-dependent. The same WT is needed regardless of operating pressure."*

### Finding 2: 16" and 20" ALL FAIL -- good for demo narrative
All 24 cases for 16" and 20" fail because mid-range catalog WT is too thin for 500m. This is actually a FEATURE for the demo narrative: *"Overnight screening caught that your catalog stock is undersized for this water depth."* 

**Suggestion**: Add a callout box in the report: *"⚠ 16" and 20" pipes require wall thickness above standard catalog mid-range for 500m water depth. See Chart 2 for minimum required values."*

### Finding 3: Lifecycle chart (Chart 1) clarity
The hero chart shows phase utilisation but the dropdown selector only changes pipe size. Could be enhanced to also filter by code, or show a comparison mode.

**Suggestion**: Add radio buttons: "Compare codes" (current) vs "Compare pipe sizes" (transpose the grouping).

### Priority
Low -- these are polish items. Demo is functional and defensible as-is.

## Parent
#1800
title: GTM Demo 2: Review findings -- pressure insensitivity at large D/t, lifecycle phase chart clarity

body:
## Pattern
Every GTM demo script (demos 1-5) should follow this pattern:

### First run (full calculation):
```
uv run demo_XX.py
```
- Runs all engineering calculations
- Saves ALL intermediate data to results/demo_XX_results.json
- Generates HTML report

### Subsequent runs (from cache):
```
uv run demo_XX.py --from-cache
```
- Loads results from JSON (no engineering module imports needed)
- Regenerates charts and HTML report only
- Useful for: chart styling iteration, branding updates, template changes

### Force recalculation:
```
uv run demo_XX.py --force
```
- Ignores cache, recalculates everything
- Useful when input databases change

## JSON Results Schema
Each demo's results JSON should contain:
- `metadata`: run parameters, timestamps, version
- `cases`: full parametric sweep results (one object per case)
- `derived`: pre-computed chart data (min WT, phase results, comparison data)
- `summary`: table-ready summary data

## Benefits
- Token savings: no agent compute for chart iterations
- Speed: <0.5s from cache vs 2-10s full calculation
- Reproducibility: results JSON is the single source of truth
- Portability: can regenerate reports on machines without engineering modules

## Applies To
- [x] Demo 2: Wall Thickness (#1831)
- [ ] Demo 1: Freespan
- [ ] Demo 3: Mudmat Installation
- [ ] Demo 4: Shallow Water Pipelay
- [ ] Demo 5: Jumper Installation

## Parent
#1800
title: GTM: All demo scripts must support --from-cache flag for chart-only regeneration

body:
## Problem
Demo 2 script saves the 72-case parametric sweep results to JSON, but recalculates several expensive operations every run:

1. **Lifecycle phase analysis** (Chart 1) -- runs PhaseAnalysisRunner for 6 pipes x 3 codes = 18 phase analyses. NOT saved.
2. **Min WT binary search** (Chart 2) -- runs ~15 iterations per pipe/code combo = 18 x 15 = 270 analyzer calls. NOT saved.
3. **Weight penalty calculations** (Chart 4) -- derived from min WT, so also NOT saved.
4. **Heatmap utilisation values** (Chart 3) -- additional single-point analyses. NOT saved.

## Fix
Extend the JSON results file to include ALL intermediate data:

```json
{
  "metadata": { ... },
  "parametric_sweep": [ ... 72 cases ... ],
  "lifecycle_phases": {
    "6in": {
      "DNV-ST-F101": { "Installation": {...}, "Hydrotest": {...}, ... },
      "API RP 1111": { ... },
      "PD 8010-2": { ... }
    },
    ...
  },
  "min_wall_thickness": {
    "6in": { "DNV-ST-F101": 8.51, "API RP 1111": 7.62, "PD 8010-2": 8.57 },
    ...
  },
  "weight_penalty": {
    "6in": { "delta_wt_mm": 0.95, "delta_kg_m": 3.5, "delta_pct": 12.4 },
    ...
  },
  "summary": [ ... 18 rows ... ]
}
```

Add a `--from-cache` flag so the script can regenerate charts from saved JSON without rerunning any analysis:
```
uv run demo_02_wall_thickness_multicode.py --from-cache
```

## Impact
- Saves tokens when iterating on chart styling
- Saves compute time (2.5s -> <0.5s from cache)
- Enables chart-only regeneration without engineering module imports
- Results are fully reproducible from JSON alone

## Parent
#1800
title: GTM Demo 2: Cache ALL computed data to JSON (avoid recalculation)

## Purpose
Record animated GIF screencasts of each GTM demo showing the FULL WORKFLOW:
1. User types a natural language prompt in CLI (e.g. 'run wall thickness comparison for 12in X65 at 500m')
2. Agent processes the request, navigates digitalmodel repo code
3. Agent runs the parametric analysis (show case counter ticking)
4. HTML report is generated and opened in browser
5. Interactive charts with hover/zoom demonstrated

This shows the END-TO-END value: prompt -> agent -> code -> deliverable.

These GIFs will be used for:
- GitHub repo README (instant visual proof of capability)
- aceengineer.com / digitalmodel website
- LinkedIn posts and cold outreach emails (embedded or linked)

## Deliverables
| Demo | GIF File | Content |
|------|----------|---------|
| 1 | gtm/media/demo_01_freespan.gif | CLI prompt -> agent runs freespan sweep -> HTML with VIV heatmap |
| 2 | gtm/media/demo_02_wall_thickness.gif | CLI prompt -> agent runs multi-code WT -> HTML with lifecycle utilisation chart |
| 3 | gtm/media/demo_03_mudmat_installation.gif | CLI prompt -> agent runs vessel x mudmat matrix -> HTML with go/no-go heatmap |
| 4 | gtm/media/demo_04_shallow_pipelay.gif | CLI prompt -> agent runs pipelay sweep -> HTML with installation envelope |
| 5 | gtm/media/demo_05_jumper_installation.gif | CLI prompt -> agent runs jumper matrix -> HTML with crane utilisation |
| All | gtm/media/demo_comparison_matrix.gif | Quick scroll through vessel and structure comparison matrices |

## GIF Story Arc (each demo)
```
Scene 1 (3s):  User prompt in CLI terminal
               e.g. 'Analyze wall thickness for 6in-20in pipes, compare DNV/API/PD8010'
Scene 2 (5s):  Agent thinking + navigating digitalmodel codebase
               Show: loading modules, reading pipe database, setting up sweep
Scene 3 (10s): Parametric analysis running
```

body:
## Context

From the security guide in affaan-m/everything-claude-code. Key stat: 36% of 3,984 public skills had prompt injection vectors (Snyk ToxicSkills study).

## Scope

### 1. Skill Supply Chain Scanning
Treat skills as supply chain artifacts. Scan for: hidden Unicode/bidi characters, base64 payloads, suspicious tool calls. Gate: new skills must pass scan before activation.

### 2. Memory Poisoning Mitigation
After untrusted/external code, rotate or audit memory. Flag memory entries added during untrusted sessions.

### 3. Process-Group Kill
When killing agent processes, kill the entire process group (not just parent). Prevents orphaned child processes.

### 4. Heartbeat Dead-Man Switches
For unattended/overnight agent loops, require periodic heartbeat. Auto-terminate if missed for N minutes.

## Minimum Security Bar Checklist
- [ ] Separate agent identities from personal accounts
- [ ] Short-lived scoped credentials for agent sessions
- [ ] Deny outbound network by default for untrusted work
- [ ] Log all tool calls and approval decisions
- [ ] Process-group kill + heartbeat dead-man switches
- [ ] Scan skills/hooks as supply chain artifacts

## Reference
- https://github.com/affaan-m/everything-claude-code/blob/main/the-security-guide.md
- CVEs: CVE-2025-59536, CVE-2026-21852

## Context

From review of affaan-m/everything-claude-code (50K stars, MIT).

## Problem

With 691 skills, multiple MCP servers, AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and memory injected into context, we have no visibility into how much of the 200K context window is consumed by system overhead. Key data: context drops from 200K to ~70K with too many MCP tools loaded.

## Proposed Solution

Create a /context-budget command that audits:
1. CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md token count
2. Memory injection size
3. Active MCP tool descriptions
4. Loaded skills token overhead
5. System prompt overhead
6. Remaining tokens for conversation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Audit script counts tokens for all injected components
- [ ] Recommendations when overhead exceeds 30% of context
- [ ] MCP tool count warning (>80 tools = degraded performance)
- [ ] Can run as slash command or standalone script

## Reference
- https://github.com/affaan-m/everything-claude-code (skills/context-budget)
- MAX_THINKING_TOKENS 31999->10000 = ~70% hidden cost reduction

body:
## Context

From review of affaan-m/everything-claude-code (50K stars, MIT).

## Problem

Running format/lint/typecheck after every file edit wastes tokens and time. The batch-at-Stop pattern accumulates edits during a response and runs format+typecheck ONCE at the Stop hook.

## Proposed Solution

1. PostToolUse accumulator hook: record all edited files during a response
2. Stop hook: batch-run formatter + type checker on accumulated file list
3. Environment variable toggle: ECC_BATCH_FORMAT=1

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Accumulator records edited files per response
- [ ] Stop hook runs ruff format + ruff check on Python files
- [ ] Measurable token reduction vs per-edit approach
- [ ] Toggle via environment variable

## Reference
- https://github.com/affaan-m/everything-claude-code (hooks/hooks.json stop:format-typecheck)

body:
## Context

The MATLAB code includes two corrections to VIV fatigue damage that are
not yet in the Python implementation:

### 1. Strake Coverage Correction
For helical strakes that suppress VIV:
    correction = ((100/strake_coverage) × (1 - strake_efficiency/100))^flag
    flag = 1 for straked, 0 for bare

MATLAB defaults: strake_coverage=0%, strake_efficiency=0% (bare pipe).

### 2. Stress Concentration Factor (SCF)
The MATLAB code applies per-keypoint SCF values to the stress range:
    S_effective = S × SCF × gamma_s
    gamma_s = 1.3 (stress range safety factor)

Currently `span_viv_response.py` does not apply SCF or gamma_s.

### 3. Base Thickness Correction
    If wall_thickness > base_thickness:
        correction *= (wall_thickness / base_thickness)^exponent
    MATLAB defaults: base_thickness=22mm, exponent=0.25

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Add strake_coverage and strake_efficiency to PipeSpanInput
- [ ] Add stress_concentration_factor (SCF) to PipeSpanInput
- [ ] Add gamma_s (stress range safety factor) to PipeSpanInput
- [ ] Apply corrections in span_fatigue_damage.py or span_viv_response.py
- [ ] Tests with and without strakes/SCF

## Related
- Parent: #1773 (closed)
- MATLAB: TwoHspanvivDamageAssessment.m CorrectionFactor calculation
title: feat(freespan): strake and SCF corrections for VIV fatigue damage

body:
## Context

DNV-RP-F105 and the MATLAB code include a trench depth correction factor
for the cross-flow onset velocity:

    delta_D = (1.25·trench_depth - seabed_proximity) / drag_diameter
    delta_D clamped to [0, 1]
    trench_onset = 1 + 0.5·delta_D

This multiplies the CF onset velocity (Vr_onset_CF), making onset less
likely for pipes in trenches (higher onset threshold).

Currently `span_onset_screening.py` does not include the trench correction.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Add trench_depth_m field to PipeSpanInput (default 0)
- [ ] Apply trench correction to CF onset in span_onset_screening.py
- [ ] Test: trench depth > 0 increases CF onset threshold
- [ ] Test: trench depth = 0 gives trench_onset = 1.0

## Related
- Parent: #1773 (closed)
- MATLAB: TwoHspanvivCrossflowScreen.m trenchOnset variable
title: feat(freespan): trench depth correction for CF onset velocity

body:
## Context

DNV-RP-F105 Section 4.3.6 defines two reduction factors that reduce the
effective VIV response:

### R1 — Turbulence intensity + flow angle (Sec 4.3.6)
    R1 = 1 - π²·(Ic - 0.03)·(π/2 - √2·θ_rel)
    Clamped to [0, 1]

### R2 — Turbulence intensity only (Sec 4.3.6)  
    R2 = 1 - (Ic - 0.03) / 0.17
    Clamped to [0, 1]

### RD — Wave spreading (Sec 3.4)
    RD = ∫ w·(sin(θ_rel - β))² dβ

These are implemented in the MATLAB code (`ObtainParams` function) but not
yet in the Python module. Currently `span_viv_response.py` does not apply
R1/R2 to the IL/CF amplitudes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Add turbulence_intensity field to PipeSpanInput
- [ ] Add relative_flow_angle field to PipeSpanInput  
- [ ] Compute R1, R2 in span_viv_response.py
- [ ] Apply R1 to IL amplitude, R2 to CF amplitude
- [ ] Wave spreading RD (optional, lower priority)
- [ ] Tests with default Ic=0.05, theta=90°

## Related
- Parent stream: #1773 (closed)
- MATLAB reference: TwoHspanvivGetInputs.m ObtainParams function
- Tests exist for the formulas: TestReductionFactors in test_free_span_matlab_regression.py
title: feat(freespan): turbulence intensity and wave spreading reduction factors

body:
## Context

`wave_velocity.py` (#1790, closed) computes Uw from Hs/Tp/depth but is not yet
wired into the `FreespanVIVFatigue` facade. Currently the user must call
`compute_wave_velocity()` manually and pass `wave_velocity_ms` to `PipeSpanInput`.

## Proposed Change

Add optional `Hs` and `Tp` fields to `PipeSpanInput`. When provided (and
`wave_velocity_ms` is not set), the facade auto-computes Uw via
`compute_wave_velocity()` before running the assessment.

```python
inp = PipeSpanInput(
    od_m=0.2731, wt_m=0.0127, span_length_m=34.0,
    Hs_m=5.0, Tp_s=8.0, water_depth_m=120.0,
    # wave_velocity_ms auto-computed from Hs/Tp
)
```

Also auto-compute KC and alpha from Uw/Uc/Tu.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Optional Hs_m, Tp_s, water_depth_m fields on PipeSpanInput
- [ ] Auto-compute Uw, Tu, KC, alpha in facade when Hs/Tp provided
- [ ] Explicit wave_velocity_ms overrides auto-computation
- [ ] Tests for auto vs manual Uw
- [ ] Backward compatible (existing code unchanged)

## Related
- Uses: wave_velocity.py (#1790, closed)
- Parent stream: #1773 (closed)
title: feat(freespan): wire wave_velocity into facade — auto-compute Uw from Hs/Tp

body:
## Context

A full audit of all 4 AI agents (Claude Code, Hermes, Codex, Gemini) revealed that **~6.7GB of machine-local state** has NO git protection. If ace-linux-1 dies, the entire institutional memory built over months of agent interaction is lost.

The learnings that matter most — user corrections, learned patterns, permission rules, cross-agent insights — total only ~400KB but are scattered across 4 different home directories with no unified backup or portability strategy.

This is the umbrella issue coordinating the fix across all agents.

## Architecture: Three Tiers of Portability

### Tier 1: Git-committed snapshots (survives `git clone`)
Small, high-value files snapshotted nightly into workspace-hub repo:
- Agent memory files (Hermes MEMORY.md/USER.md, Claude project memory, Codex rules)
- Distilled learning artifacts (patterns, corrections, insights, drift summaries)
- Exported session metadata (JSONL tool-call summaries, not full transcripts)

### Tier 2: Rsync backup (survives single-machine failure)
Large files backed up to ace-linux-2 daily:
- Full session transcripts (Claude 2.5GB, Codex 899MB, Gemini 162MB, Hermes 124MB)
- SQLite databases (Hermes state.db 53MB, Codex state_5.sqlite 59MB)

### Tier 3: Regenerable (no backup needed)
Caches, plugins, debug logs, telemetry — recreated on agent install.

## Child Issues

| # | Issue | Agent | What's tracked | Size | Status |
|---|-------|-------|---------------|------|--------|
| #1777 | Hermes memory snapshots | Hermes | MEMORY.md, USER.md | 3KB | NEW |
| #1778 | Un-gitignore hermes/cross-agent state | Hermes/Cross | hermes-insights.yaml, cross-agent-memory.yaml, session JSONL | 15KB+11MB | NEW |
| #1779 | Claude project memory snapshots | Claude | 39 feedback/project/reference memory files | 196KB | NEW |
| #1780 | Fix nightly pipeline git-add gap | Claude/All | corrections/, patterns/, cc-insights/, reflect-history/, learned-patterns.json, etc. | ~25MB | NEW |
| #1781 | Codex + Gemini state snapshots | Codex/Gemini | default.rules, history.jsonl, state.json, session export | ~350KB | NEW |
| #1583 | Hermes config parity | Hermes | config.yaml template, SOUL.md | existing | OPEN |
| #894 | Knowledge persistence architecture | All | WRK completion learnings, career knowledge | existing | OPEN |
| #194 | Codex session archive integration | Codex | Session JSONL export pipeline | existing | OPEN |
| #193 | Session scanner for historical learnings | Claude | Retroactive learning extraction | existing | OPEN |

## Implementation Order

1. **#1780** (fix nightly pipeline) — Lowest effort, highest impact. Just add `git add` + commit for files already allowed by .gitignore exceptions. ~25MB of corrections, patterns, insights immediately saved.

2. **#1778** (un-gitignore hermes state) — Add 7 gitignore exceptions + commit existing files. Hermes insights, cross-agent memory, drift summary, portfolio signals.

3. **#1779** (Claude project memory) — Snapshot 39 memory files (196KB). Most valuable per-byte: every `feedback_*.md` is a user correction.

4. **#1777** (Hermes memory) — Snapshot MEMORY.md + USER.md (3KB).

5. **#1781** (Codex + Gemini) — Snapshot rules, state, history. Build Codex session export pipeline.

6. **#1583** (config parity) — Already scoped, extends to ace-linux-2.

## Nightly Pipeline Changes (consolidated)

All snapshot + commit logic should be consolidated into ONE script called at the end of `comprehensive-learning-nightly.sh`:

```bash
scripts/cron/commit-learning-artifacts.sh
```

This script:
1. Snapshots agent memories (Hermes, Claude, Codex, Gemini) to `config/agents/*/`
2. `git add` all learning state directories
3. Runs `legal-sanity-scan.sh --diff-only` (MANDATORY before commit)
4. Commits and pushes if anything changed

## Legal Risk

Memory files and correction records can contain file paths, command strings, or project names that reference client work. ALL snapshot directories must be covered by `legal-sanity-scan.sh`. The commit script must fail-safe: if legal scan finds violations, skip the commit and log the issue.

## What a Fresh `git clone` Gets You After This

```
config/agents/
  hermes/memories/          # Hermes MEMORY.md + USER.md snapshots
  claude/memory-snapshots/  # 39 Claude project memory files
  codex/state-snapshots/    # default.rules + history.jsonl
  gemini/state-snapshots/   # state.json + projects.json

.claude/state/
  corrections/              # Error correction history
  patterns/                 # Learned behavioral patterns
  reflect-history/          # Self-reflection analysis
  cc-insights/              # Agent insights
  learned-patterns.json     # Distilled pattern database
  cc-user-insights.yaml     # User preference model
  hermes-insights.yaml      # Cross-agent Hermes export
  cross-agent-memory.yaml   # Unified memory index
  drift-summary.yaml        # Config/skill drift tracking
  portfolio-signals.yaml    # Repo health signals
  skill-eval-results/       # Skill evaluation scores

logs/orchestrator/hermes/   # Hermes session exports (JSONL)
logs/orchestrator/codex/    # Codex session exports (JSONL, future)
```

Total: ~40MB of institutional memory that survives machine loss.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All child issues closed
- [ ] `git clone` on a fresh machine + `sync-agent-configs.sh` restores all agent memories
- [ ] Nightly pipeline commits all learning artifacts without manual intervention
- [ ] Legal scan gates all committed learning data
- [ ] Session transcript rsync backup covers all 4 agents

body:
## Context

Review of [shanraisshan/claude-code-best-practice](https://github.com/shanraisshan/claude-code-best-practice) (133k+ stars, updated for CC v2.1.90) revealed significant gaps between native Claude Code capabilities and our ecosystem usage. This issue tracks an 8-week adoption cadence.

**Key finding:** `/powerup` and `/insights` are NATIVE CC commands (not custom). We have been reinventing wheels instead of leveraging built-in features. See #1760 comment for details.

**Source repo cloned to:** `/tmp/claude-code-best-practice` (reference)

---

## 8-Week Adoption Cadence

### Week 1: Native Command Literacy
**Goal:** Every developer runs /powerup once, team adopts /insights + /stats + /context

- [ ] Run `/powerup` on all machines (dev-primary, dev-secondary) — complete all 10 lessons
- [ ] Run `/insights` and review session analysis report; compare with our custom pipeline output
- [ ] Run `/stats` to establish baseline usage patterns
- [ ] Run `/context` at mid-session to understand context budget consumption
- [ ] Document findings in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-1-native-commands.md`
- [ ] Add `/context` at 50% as a session discipline rule to CLAUDE.md ("compact at max 50%")

### Week 2: settings.json Modernization
**Goal:** Leverage native CC settings we are ignoring

- [ ] Configure `statusLine` — show context %, model, rate limits, git branch
- [ ] Configure `spinnerVerbs` and `spinnerTipsOverride` with ecosystem-specific tips
- [ ] Set `outputStyle: "Explanatory"` for insight boxes during development
- [ ] Set `effortLevel` appropriately (opus=high for planning, sonnet=medium for code)
- [ ] Configure `plansDirectory` to route to our `.planning/` directory
- [ ] Configure `worktree.symlinkDirectories` for `node_modules`, `.cache`
- [ ] Add `$schema` for IDE validation: `"https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json"`
- [ ] Review and set `autoMode` rules for trusted operations
- [ ] Document in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-2-settings.md`

### Week 3: Hooks Infrastructure
**Goal:** Implement the 3 high-value hook patterns from best-practice repo

- [ ] **PreToolUse hook** — measure skill usage frequency (which skills fire, how often)
  - Log to `.claude/state/skill-usage.jsonl`
  - Surface in nightly comprehensive-learning pipeline
- [ ] **PostToolUse hook** — auto-format on Edit/Write (ruff format for .py, prettier for .md/.yaml)
  - Only trigger on file-modifying tools
  - Must be < 2 seconds per invocation
- [ ] **Stop hook** — verification nudge at end of turn
  - Prompt: "Did you verify the output? Did you run tests?"
  - Light touch — raw signal write, no blocking
- [ ] Wire hooks into `.claude/settings.json` (project-scoped)
- [ ] Test on dev-primary, verify no performance regression
- [ ] Document in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-3-hooks.md`

### Week 4: Skills Modernization (Batch 1 — Top 20)
**Goal:** Upgrade 20 most-used skills with native CC frontmatter fields

- [ ] Audit top 20 skills by usage (from Week 3 hook data, or git log frequency)
- [ ] Add native frontmatter fields to each:
  - `context: fork` for isolated execution where appropriate
  - `paths` for auto-discovery triggers (e.g., OrcaFlex skill fires on .dat/.yml files)
  - `model` and `effort` overrides (haiku for fast lookup, opus for planning)
  - `hooks` scoped to skill (e.g., /careful blocks destructive commands)
- [ ] Add `## Gotchas` section to each skill (failure points learned over time)
- [ ] Add `## Progressive Disclosure` structure (references/, scripts/, examples/ subdirs)
- [ ] Ensure skill `description` is written as trigger ("when should I fire?") not summary
- [ ] Document in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-4-skills-modernization.md`

### Week 5: Native Agent Definitions (.claude/agents/)
**Goal:** Create 5 core agents using native CC agent format

- [ ] **engineering-explorer** — model:haiku, tools:Read-only, for fast codebase search
  - Skills preloaded: repo-structure, ecosystem-terminology
- [ ] **orcaflex-specialist** — model:opus, skills:orcaflex+orcawave, color:green
  - Preloaded domain knowledge, isolation:worktree for parallel runs
- [ ] **code-reviewer** — model:opus, effort:high, skills:code-review+clean-code
  - Stop hook for review verdict enforcement
- [ ] **overnight-planner** — model:opus, effort:high, background:true
  - Skills: overnight-parallel-agent-prompts, cross-review-policy
  - initialPrompt with session-start context load
- [ ] **data-pipeline** — model:sonnet, skills:data-analysis+xlsx+pdf
  - paths: "*.csv,*.xlsx,*.pdf" for auto-activation
- [ ] Place in `.claude/agents/` directory (native CC format)
- [ ] Document in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-5-agents.md`

### Week 6: Command→Agent→Skill Orchestration
**Goal:** Build 3 orchestration commands following the pattern from best-practice repo

- [ ] **`/workspace-health`** — Command orchestrates:
  1. Spawns `engineering-explorer` agent to scan repo health
  2. Invokes `repo-capability-map` skill for analysis
  3. Invokes `interactive-report-generator` skill for HTML output
- [ ] **`/orcaflex-pipeline`** — Command orchestrates:
  1. Spawns `orcaflex-specialist` agent to parse .dat files
  2. Invokes `orcaflex-enrichment` skill for YAML conversion
  3. Invokes `plotly-visualization` skill for results
- [ ] **`/daily-brief`** — Command orchestrates:
  1. Runs native `/insights` for session analysis
  2. Spawns agent to check GitHub issues + PR status
  3. Invokes `today` skill for priority recommendations
- [ ] Place in `.claude/commands/` directory
- [ ] Document orchestration pattern in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-6-orchestration.md`

### Week 7: Self-Improvement Commands (#1760)
**Goal:** Wire the self-improvement commands from #1760

- [ ] `/improve` — lightweight ad-hoc mode (standalone, < 30s)
- [ ] `/compound` — wire compound-engineering skill to command entry
- [ ] `/reflect` — wire claude-reflect skill to command entry
- [ ] `/knowledge` — implement knowledge capture/retrieval command
- [ ] Update `config/workflow-tips/tips-catalog.yaml` with correct try_it paths
- [ ] Verify nightly comprehensive-learning pipeline still works unchanged
- [ ] Document in `docs/superpowers/reports/week-7-self-improvement.md`

### Week 8: Cross-Model Review + Squash Discipline
**Goal:** Enforce the workflow patterns from Boris Cherny tips

- [ ] Enforce squash merge on all PRs (configure GitHub repo settings)
- [ ] Set PR size target: p50 < 150 lines (add to CLAUDE.md as rule)
- [ ] Implement cross-model review: Claude plan → Codex review → Gemini audit
  - Wire to existing cross-review-policy skill
  - Add as mandatory gate in GSD workflow
- [ ] Add `/compact` at 50% context as a CLAUDE.md rule
- [ ] Add `/rename` discipline for important sessions
- [ ] Final report: `docs/superpowers/reports/week-8-workflow-discipline.md`

---

## Weekly Execution Pattern

Each week follows the same ritual:

```
Monday:    Read the week focus above. Plan specific files/configs to change.
Tue-Thu:   Implement changes. Use /powerup lessons if stuck on CC features.
Friday:    Write the weekly report in docs/superpowers/reports/week-N-*.md
           Run /insights + /stats to measure impact.
           Commit + push all changes.
```

## Success Metrics (measured at Week 8)

| Metric | Baseline (now) | Target |
|--------|---------------|--------|
| Native CC features actively used | ~3 (/plan, /compact, /model) | 15+ |
| Skills with native frontmatter (context, paths, effort) | 0 | 20+ |
| .claude/agents/ definitions | 0 | 5+ |
| Orchestration commands (Command→Agent→Skill) | 0 | 3+ |
| Hooks active (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop) | 0 | 3 |
| settings.json features leveraged | ~5 | 15+ |
| Self-improvement commands wired | 0 | 4+ (/improve, /compound, /reflect, /knowledge) |
| Average PR size (lines) | unmeasured | p50 < 150 |

## Related Issues

- #1760 — feat(self-improvement): operationalize /powerup, /insights, /improve, etc. (Week 7)
- #1749 — convert .claude/commands to SKILL.md
- #113 — repo ecosystem 3-6 month roadmap
- #894 — knowledge persistence architecture
- #202 — per-workstation analysis compilation

## Reference

- Source repo: https://github.com/shanraisshan/claude-code-best-practice
- Local clone: /tmp/claude-code-best-practice
- 69 tips cataloged, 64 native slash commands documented, 5 official skills, 5 official agents
- Key video: Boris Cherny on Pragmatic Engineer (Mar 4, 2026) — building Claude Code

title: feat(harness): weekly Claude Code best-practice adoption cadence — 8-week plan from shanraisshan/claude-code-best-practice

body:
## Problem

The workspace-hub ecosystem has a rich catalog of self-improvement slash commands documented in `config/workflow-tips/tips-catalog.yaml` and backed by skills (`comprehensive-learning`, `compound-engineering`, `claude-reflect`, etc.), but they are NOT operationally ready:

1. **No `/powerup` command exists** — there is no unified "level up this session/repo" entry point
2. **`/insights` exists as a concept** (tips-catalog eco-insights) but has no standalone invocation path — it is locked inside the nightly `comprehensive-learning` pipeline (Phase 1) with an "Iron Law" ban on mid-session use
3. **`/improve` same story** — Phase 4 of nightly pipeline only, no ad-hoc usage
4. **`/compound`** — skill exists but no `.claude/commands/` entry or wrapper script
5. **`/reflect`** — skill exists (`claude-reflect`) but scripts are placeholders, no `.claude/commands/` entry
6. **`/knowledge`** — referenced everywhere but no concrete implementation found

The net result: a user typing `/powerup`, `/insights`, `/improve` etc. gets... nothing actionable.

## Desired Outcome

A working suite of self-improvement commands that an engineer can invoke ad-hoc OR that run automatically in the nightly pipeline:

### Tier 1 — Must Have
- **`/powerup`** — New unified entry point. Runs a lightweight self-improvement sweep:
  - Quick session-insights extraction (last N sessions)
  - Surface top 3 actionable skill/memory/config improvements
  - Present as a checklist, apply with confirmation
  - Safe for mid-session use (< 30s, minimal token cost)
- **`/insights`** — Standalone lightweight mode that extracts patterns/anti-patterns from recent sessions WITHOUT the full nightly pipeline overhead
- **`/improve`** — Standalone mode to apply improvement candidates from `candidates/` directory

### Tier 2 — Should Have
- **`/compound`** — Wire the compound-engineering skill to a `.claude/commands/` entry so it is invocable
- **`/reflect`** — Wire the claude-reflect skill to a `.claude/commands/` entry; verify scripts actually run
- **`/knowledge`** — Implement the knowledge capture/retrieval command referenced in tips-catalog

### Tier 3 — Nice to Have
- **`/self-improve`** — Alias/aggregator that chains: insights -> improve -> compound-capture
- All commands emit structured YAML signals for the nightly pipeline to consume (no duplication of work)

## Implementation Notes

### Iron Law Reconciliation
The `comprehensive-learning` skill has an "Iron Law" that bans `/insights`, `/reflect`, `/improve` from running mid-session. This issue proposes:
- A **lightweight ad-hoc mode** for each command (fast, minimal context, signal-only)
- A **full mode** that runs in the nightly pipeline (current behavior)
- The Iron Law applies to full mode only

### File Locations
- Commands: `.claude/commands/` (or Hermes equivalent)
- Skills: already exist in skill library, need wiring
- Tips catalog: `config/workflow-tips/tips-catalog.yaml` (update try_it paths)
- Nightly script: `scripts/cron/comprehensive-learning-nightly.sh`

### Existing Skills to Wire
| Command | Backing Skill | Status |
|---------|--------------|--------|
| `/powerup` | NEW — to be created | Does not exist |
| `/insights` | comprehensive-learning Phase 1 | Exists, needs lightweight mode |
| `/improve` | comprehensive-learning Phase 4 | Exists, needs lightweight mode |
| `/compound` | compound-engineering | Exists, needs command entry |
| `/reflect` | claude-reflect | Exists, needs command entry + script verification |
| `/knowledge` | knowledge-manager | Exists as skill, no command entry |

### Related Issues
- #194 — Codex session archive into comprehensive-learning
- #202 — Per-workstation analysis compilation
- #47 — Windows comprehensive-learning fix
- #894 — Knowledge persistence architecture
- #1749 — Convert .claude/commands to SKILL.md
- #113 — Repo ecosystem 3-6 month roadmap

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `/powerup` command exists and produces actionable output in < 30 seconds
- [ ] `/insights` runs standalone in lightweight mode, producing session pattern summary
- [ ] `/improve` runs standalone, listing and applying improvement candidates
- [ ] `/compound`, `/reflect`, `/knowledge` are invocable via slash commands
- [ ] Nightly pipeline still works unchanged (full mode)
- [ ] tips-catalog.yaml updated with correct try_it paths
- [ ] Each command has a `--help` or usage banner
- [ ] Tested on dev-primary (Linux) — cross-platform is follow-up

body:
## Context
Follow-up from #1721. Manual conversion is done. Now automate it going forward.

## Problem
When new agent .md files are added to any repo, they don't automatically become Hermes skills. The gap grows silently.

## Proposed approach

### Option A: Git hook (post-commit)
Add to `scripts/_core/hooks/post-commit`:
- Detect new/modified .claude/agents/*.md files in commit
- Run `convert-agent-to-skill.py --dry-run` and log candidates
- Optionally auto-convert with `--auto` flag

### Option B: Cron job (weekly)
Add to nightly/weekly cron:
- Scan all external_dirs repos for agent files newer than their SKILL.md counterparts
- Generate conversion report
- Auto-convert with review queue

### Option C: CI workflow (GitHub Action)
- On push to any repo with .claude/agents/ changes
- Auto-create PR with SKILL.md conversions

## Recommendation
Option B (cron) fits the existing pattern (`scripts/cron/` already has skill maintenance jobs). Run weekly alongside the existing skill health checks.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Automated detection of new agent files
- [ ] Draft SKILL.md generation (manual review before commit)
- [ ] Integration with existing cron/maintenance pipeline

body:
## Context
Follow-up from #1721 (Terminal 4). Agent-to-skill conversion is done. Commands are the next layer.

## Scale
- workspace-hub root: ~135 commands
- digitalmodel: ~216 commands
- Other repos: 150+ each (mostly template commands from GSD)

Total: ~306 unique command .md files (after deduplicating templates).

## Work
1. Extend `scripts/skills/convert-agent-to-skill.py` with `--type command` mode
   - Commands have different frontmatter: `allowed-tools`, `user-input`, etc.
   - Map command names to skill names (e.g. `/project:init` -> `project-init`)
2. Audit template commands — classify as CONVERT or SKIP
   - Many are Claude-specific slash commands (e.g. `/agent:create`, `/flow:start`)
   - Some have general value (e.g. `/project:plan`, `/test:run`)
3. Convert top ~50 general-purpose commands
4. Deduplicate against existing Hermes skills

## Why priority:low
Commands tend to be short (trigger + 1-2 instructions) vs agents which have deep domain knowledge. The ROI of converting agents was much higher. Commands are nice-to-have for completeness.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Command conversion mode added to script
- [ ] Template commands audited
- [ ] Top 50 commands converted
- [ ] No name collisions with existing skills

body:
## Problem
Cross-review logs use 7+ different verdict strings, making automated agreement analysis unreliable.

## Observed Verdict Strings
Codex: APPROVE, MINOR, MAJOR, REVISION NEEDED, REVISE, changes-requested, Request changes, REQUEST_CHANGES, changes_requested, No findings, PENDING
Gemini: APPROVE, MINOR, MAJOR (plus unparsed entries)

## Impact
- Cannot compute reviewer agreement rates
- Cannot trend verdict quality over time
- Automated quality gates may not match correctly

## Action
1. Define canonical enum: {APPROVE, MINOR, MAJOR, REJECT}
2. Update `scripts/review/submit-to-codex.sh` to normalize verdicts
3. Update `scripts/review/submit-to-gemini.sh` to normalize verdicts
4. Add validation to log parsers

## Source
Phase E: `analysis/cross-agent-audit-20260402/phase-e-agent-routing.md` Section 4

body:
## Objective

Analyze the full session log corpus across all 4 AI agents to identify:
1. **Skill gaps** — recurring manual workflows with no skill coverage
2. **Stale/dead skills** — skills never invoked across any agent
3. **Cross-repo work patterns** — which repos get worked on together (inform skill grouping)
4. **Correction hotspots** — files/modules that repeatedly need fixing (inform test/skill priorities)
5. **Agent strengths** — what each agent does well (inform routing policy)
6. **Memory duplication** — facts independently learned by multiple agents (promote to shared state)

## Data corpus

| Agent | Source | Records | Date Range | Size |
|-------|--------|---------|------------|------|
| Claude Code | `logs/orchestrator/claude/*.jsonl` | 153,884 lines | Mar 2–25, 2026 | 36 MB |
| Claude Code | `.claude/state/sessions-archive/` | native sessions | varies | — |
| Codex | `logs/orchestrator/codex/` | 410 files | Mar 4–11, 2026 | 1.7 MB |
| Codex | `~/.codex/sessions/` | 853 native sessions | varies | — |
| Gemini | `logs/orchestrator/gemini/` | 219 files | varies | 854 KB |
| Hermes | `logs/orchestrator/hermes/*.jsonl` | 13,554 lines | Apr 1–2, 2026 | 4 MB |
| Hermes | `~/.hermes/sessions/*.json` | 238 native sessions | Apr 1–2, 2026 | 71 MB |
| **Derived** | `.claude/state/corrections/*.jsonl` | 8,965 corrections | rolling | — |
| **Derived** | `.claude/state/patterns/` | 177 pattern files | rolling | — |
| **Derived** | `.claude/state/session-signals/*.jsonl` | 390 signal files | rolling | — |
| **Derived** | `.claude/state/skill-scores.yaml` | 547 skills scored | latest | — |

## Per-repo .claude/ ecosystem (not just workspace-hub)

24 repos have `.claude/` directories. Key repos with rich AI content:

| Repo | Skills | Commands | Docs | AGENTS.md | CLAUDE.md | Work-Queue |
|------|--------|----------|------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| workspace-hub | 568 | 14 | 30 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| CAD-DEVELOPMENTS | 218 | 21 | 8 | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| digitalmodel | — | 30 | 20 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| worldenergydata | 21 | 26 | 8 | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| achantas-data | 13 | 20 | — | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| assetutilities | 3 | 20 | 4 | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| + 18 more repos | — | ~20 each | varies | ✓ | ✓ | — |

## Already known from existing signals

- **Repos by tool calls**: workspace-hub (147K), digitalmodel (3.9K), assetutilities (1.1K), worldenergydata (427)
- **Tool distribution**: Bash dominates (84K Claude + 6.7K Hermes), Read (28K+2K), Edit/Write (13K each)
- **skill-scores.yaml**: 547 skills tracked, many scored `tier: dead` — never invoked
- **Correction type**: 8,965 corrections logged but all typed as generic "correction" — type taxonomy needed
- **Learning reports**: 23 reports, latest shows context pollution risk flagged 3x

## Analysis tasks

### Phase A: Cross-agent tool & file frequency (baseline)
- For each agent: top 50 files read, written, edited
- For each agent: tool call patterns (sequences of 3+ that repeat)
- Cross-agent: same file worked by multiple agents (collaboration or duplication?)
- Output: `analysis/cross-agent-baseline-YYYYMMDD.md`

### Phase B: Skill gap detection
- Extract all multi-step manual workflows (≥5 tool calls without a skill invocation)
- Cluster by similarity (file patterns, command patterns)
- Cross-reference with existing 691 skills — which workflows have no skill?
- Output: `analysis/skill-gap-candidates.md` → feeds Phase 7 candidates

### Phase C: Dead skill audit
- Cross-reference skill-scores.yaml (547 scored) with full 691 skill library
- For skills with `tier: dead` — check if the underlying domain is still active (files modified recently)
- Classify: truly-dead (archive), dormant-but-needed (keep), wrong-trigger (fix)
- Output: `analysis/skill-health-report.md`

### Phase D: Correction hotspot analysis
- Parse 8,965 corrections for file paths, modules, patterns
- Identify files corrected ≥5 times → test coverage gap or skill gap
- Identify modules corrected across agents → systemic issue
- Output: `analysis/correction-hotspots.md`

### Phase E: Agent routing intelligence
- Per agent: what domains/repos/file-types does it handle best?
- Per agent: average tool calls per task type
- Per agent: correction rate (corrections / tool calls)
- Feed into `config/agents/routing-config.yaml` and `provider-capabilities.yaml`
- Output: `analysis/agent-routing-intelligence.md`

### Phase F: Memory deduplication
- Parse Hermes MEMORY.md + USER.md (§-separated)
- Parse Claude memory topic files (`~/.claude/projects/*/memory/`)
- Parse Codex rules (`~/.codex/rules/`)
- Identify facts stated in ≥2 agents → promote to shared config or AGENTS.md
- Output: `analysis/memory-dedup-candidates.md`

### Phase G: Per-repo ecosystem audit
- For each of 24 repos with .claude/: does AGENTS.md match actual repo content?
- Are commands stale? Do docs reflect current architecture?
- Which repos have skills that should be promoted to workspace-hub?
- Output: `analysis/per-repo-ecosystem-audit.md`

## Execution approach

This is a **read-only analysis** — no files are modified except under `analysis/`.
Safe for overnight batch execution across agents.

Suggest 3-terminal overnight batch:
- **T1 (Claude)**: Phases A+B (heaviest — 153K lines)
- **T2 (Hermes)**: Phases C+D+F (skill audit, corrections, memory)
- **T3 (Codex)**: Phases E+G (routing intel, per-repo audit)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Phase A baseline report generated
- [ ] Phase B: ≥5 skill gap candidates identified
- [ ] Phase C: dead skills classified (archive/dormant/fix)
- [ ] Phase D: top 10 correction hotspot files identified
- [ ] Phase E: per-agent routing recommendations
- [ ] Phase F: ≥3 shared memory facts identified for promotion
- [ ] Phase G: per-repo audit for 24 repos

## Related
- #1719 — Hermes learning pipeline integration (enables Hermes session data)
- #1712 — Hermes external_dirs (enables multi-repo skill access)
- #1668 — Harness infrastructure (closed)
