body:
## Summary

AGENTS.md states:
> Tasks tracked as GitHub issues — no local work-queue

But the repo still has:
- `.claude/work-queue/pending/WRK-5015.md` — an active work item
- `.planning/architecture/agent-vision.md` references `.claude/work-queue/` as part of the workflow
- `.planning/architecture/work-queue-codex-parity.md` — full doc about work-queue routing
- `docs/assessments/document-intelligence-audit.md` references work-queue output
- `docs/reports/skill-promotion-audit.md` references work-queue scripts

This sends conflicting signals to agents. An agent reading AGENTS.md will avoid the work-queue; an agent reading architecture docs will try to use it.

## Options

### Option A: Remove work-queue (enforce AGENTS.md policy)
- Archive `.claude/work-queue/` contents
- Remove or mark architecture docs as superseded
- Ensure WRK-5015 is tracked as a GitHub issue instead

### Option B: Update AGENTS.md (acknowledge work-queue as legacy)
- Change AGENTS.md to: "Tasks tracked as GitHub issues; `.claude/work-queue/` is legacy and being wound down"
- Set a sunset date for work-queue removal

### Option C: Keep both (hybrid model)
- Update AGENTS.md to document when each is used
- Not recommended — increases agent confusion

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] No contradiction between AGENTS.md and active planning/architecture docs
- [ ] Agents get consistent guidance on where to track work
- [ ] If work-queue is removed: WRK-5015 migrated to GH issue

## References

- AGENTS.md line 10: "Tasks tracked as GitHub issues — no local work-queue"
- `.claude/work-queue/pending/WRK-5015.md` (active item)
- Discovered in: GSD operational audit 2026-04-02
title: chore(policy): resolve work-queue vs GitHub-issues contradiction in AGENTS.md and active docs

## Context

## Tasks

   - Compare: CAPEX (anode cost vs rectifier + ground bed), OPEX (monitoring, power, replacement)

2. **Cost optimization**
   - `optimize_anode_selection(demand, catalog, constraints) -> OptimalDesign`
   - Select optimal anode size/type from a catalog to minimize cost or weight
   - Support constraints: max weight per anode, min spacing, max current density

   - Dimensions, mass, resistance, cost per kg

- [ ] Comparison module with Pydantic I/O models

body:
## Context

CP package has 198 tests across 17 test files, but each module is tested in isolation. Missing: cross-module integration tests and edge case hardening. Parent: #1676.

## Tasks

1. **Cross-module integration tests** (`tests/cathodic_protection/test_cp_integration.py`)
   - End-to-end: coating breakdown → current demand → anode sizing → depletion profile → inspection recommendation
   - Pipeline CP → potential criteria → compliance reporting
   - Marine CP → anode distribution → retrofit assessment
   - At least 5 integration scenarios

2. **Edge case hardening**
   - Zero-length anode, zero-area structure, extreme temperatures (-2°C to 40°C)
   - Very long design life (50+ years) with coating degradation beyond 100%
   - Negative/invalid inputs: proper ValueError or Pydantic validation
   - Parametric pytest (pytest.mark.parametrize) for resistance formulas across anode dimensions

3. **Property-based testing** (Hypothesis)
   - `calculate_current_demand` always non-negative
   - `calculate_anode_mass` monotonically increases with design life
   - `anode_resistance` always positive for valid inputs
   - Marine CP total demand >= sum of individual zone demands

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5+ integration test scenarios
- [ ] Edge cases covered with parametrize
- [ ] At least 3 Hypothesis property tests
title: cathodic_protection: cross-module integration tests and edge case hardening

## Objective

Build an end-to-end fatigue assessment pipeline connecting OrcaFlex output (stress time histories) to the fatigue package (rainflow counting, S-N evaluation, damage summation).

## Workflow
```
OrcaFlex .sim results
  → Extract stress time histories (multiple locations)
  → Rainflow cycle counting (existing rainflow.py)
  → S-N curve selection via sn_library_api (221 curves)
  → Thickness correction (existing damage.py)
  → Miner damage accumulation
  → Design life check
  → Automated fatigue report (fatigue_reporting.py)
```

  - `extract_stress_histories(sim_path, nodes) -> dict[str, np.ndarray]`

## Context
With retry logic (#1654) and dashboard (#1648) in place, the next gap is alerting. When retries are exhausted or the same job fails multiple times, no one is notified.

## Task
1. Add a `notify_on_failure(job_id, error, attempt_count)` function to retry_handler.py
2. Hook into the existing `scripts/notifications/notify.sh` pattern
3. Alert conditions:
   - All retries exhausted for a job (transient failure, gave up)
   - Permanent failure detected (license, invalid model)
   - Queue health reaches CRITICAL status
4. Notification payload: job_id, solver, error, retry count, queue health
5. Log notifications to `logs/notifications/solver-alerts.jsonl`

body:
## Context
During Terminal 4 overnight (2026-04-02), triage revealed that `pytest-asyncio==0.21.1` is incompatible with `hypothesis>=6.151`. The `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook in pytest-asyncio calls `function.hypothesis.inner_test` on functions that lack a `hypothesis` attribute, raising `AttributeError`.

## Impact
- Causes INTERNALERROR after test collection in digitalmodel/tests/
- Intermittent — depends on random seed and test ordering
- Does not prevent test execution but produces noisy traceback

## Fix
Update `digitalmodel/pyproject.toml` main dependencies:
```
# Change from:
"pytest-asyncio==0.21.1",
# To:
"pytest-asyncio>=0.23.0,<1.0.0",
```
Then run `uv sync` and verify no regressions.

## References
- Triage report: docs/dashboards/test-error-triage-2026-04-02.md
- Parent: #1647
title: Upgrade pytest-asyncio from 0.21.1 to >=0.23.0 — fix hypothesis INTERNALERROR (#1647 follow-up)

body:
## Objective

Prepare and execute a go-to-market (GTM) email outreach campaign targeting **vessel installation contractors** as the first client segment for ACE Engineer consulting and AI-powered engineering services.

## Context

- ACE Engineer (Vamsee Achanta) offers deep subsea/offshore engineering expertise — hydrodynamics, mooring/riser design, structural analysis, OrcaFlex/OrcaWave, fatigue assessment
- An engineering chatbot pitch exists at `docs/strategy/engineering-chatbot-oilgas-pitch.md` with a phased capability roadmap (Knowledge Base → Document Intelligence → Analysis Pre-Processor → Review Assistant → Digital Advisor)
- Target segment: vessel installation contractors (EPIC contractors, pipelay/heavy-lift vessel operators, subsea installation companies)
- Goal: generate warm leads for engineering consulting engagements and/or AI-assisted engineering tool pilots

## Target Companies (Vessel Installation Contractors)

Research and compile a contact list of key companies including but not limited to:

1. **Tier 1 — Major EPIC / Installation Contractors**
   - Subsea7, TechnipFMC, Saipem, McDermott, Allseas, Heerema
   - Boskalis (Boskalis Subsea Services)
   - Van Oord (offshore wind installation)
   - DEME Offshore

2. **Tier 2 — Specialist Installation Vessel Operators**
   - DOF Subsea, Solstad Offshore, Bourbon Offshore
   - Sapura Energy, EMAS (now Ezra Holdings)
   - Seaway7 (Subsea7 renewables arm)
   - Coastline Maritime (cable/pipe installation)

3. **Tier 3 — Regional / Niche Players**
   - Cal Dive (now Helix Energy Solutions), Superior Energy Services
   - Global Industries legacy contacts
   - Gulf Offshore, DeepOcean Group

## Deliverables

### Phase 1 — Strategy & Templates (this issue)
- [ ] Define value proposition per company tier
- [ ] Draft 3 email templates (cold intro, follow-up, meeting request)
- [ ] Create a prospect contact list with company, contact name, title, email (research-based)
- [ ] Define email sequence timing (Day 0, Day 3, Day 7)
- [ ] Prepare a 1-page capability summary / attachment (PDF or link)

### Phase 2 — Personalization & Execution
- [ ] Personalize emails per company (reference their fleet, recent projects, pain points)
- [ ] Set up email tracking (open/click if tooling available)
- [ ] Execute first batch of outreach
- [ ] Log responses and schedule follow-ups

### Phase 3 — Measure & Iterate
- [ ] Track response rates by tier
- [ ] Refine messaging based on feedback
- [ ] Expand to adjacent segments (drilling contractors, FPSO operators, floating wind developers)

## Value Proposition Angles

| Angle | Message |
|-------|---------|
| **Engineering Efficiency** | Reduce analysis turnaround by 40-60% with AI-assisted OrcaFlex/structural workflows |
| **Knowledge Continuity** | Stop losing institutional knowledge when engineers leave — encode it in AI |
| **QC Multiplier** | AI-powered review catches errors that fatigued reviewers miss |
| **Junior Engineer Ramp-up** | New hires become productive 3x faster with an AI engineering mentor |
| **Cost Arbitrage** | Senior-level engineering insight at a fraction of full-time consultant rates |

## Email Template Structure

### Email 1 — Cold Introduction
- Subject line: problem-aware, specific to their operations
- Open with a relevant industry pain point (e.g., engineer shortage, project delays, rising analysis costs)
- Brief intro of capability (1-2 sentences)
- Specific offer (free 30-min consultation or a sample analysis)
- Clear CTA

### Email 2 — Follow-up (Day 3)
- Reference Email 1
- Share a concrete example / case study
- Lower-friction CTA (reply with availability)

### Email 3 — Meeting Request (Day 7)
- Final touch — direct calendar link
- Mention will reach out via LinkedIn if no response

## Files to Create

- `docs/strategy/gtm/vessel-installation-contractors/README.md` — campaign overview
- `docs/strategy/gtm/vessel-installation-contractors/prospect-list.md` — target companies and contacts
- `docs/strategy/gtm/vessel-installation-contractors/email-templates.md` — 3 email templates
- `docs/strategy/gtm/vessel-installation-contractors/value-proposition.md` — per-tier messaging
- `docs/strategy/gtm/vessel-installation-contractors/capability-summary.md` — 1-pager for attachment

## References

- `docs/strategy/engineering-chatbot-oilgas-pitch.md` — existing AI chatbot pitch
- `docs/BUSINESS_BRAIN.md` — ecosystem context
- `client_projects/energy_engineering/ecs/ecs_clients.md` — existing client contacts

## Success Criteria

- 20+ personalized emails sent to decision-makers at vessel installation contractors
- 3+ positive responses or meeting requests within 2 weeks
- At least 1 pilot engagement initiated within 30 days
title: [WRK] GTM: Vessel Installation Contractor Email Outreach Campaign

### Top 10 worst offenders

| Symbol | Packages | Count |
|--------|----------|------:|
| `main` | data_systems, hydrodynamics, infrastructure, marine_ops, signal_processing, solvers, specialized, structural, subsea, visualization, workflows | 11 |
| `cli` | data_systems, hydrodynamics, marine_ops, signal_processing, solvers, structural, subsea, workflows | 8 |
| `ymlInput` | asset_integrity, data_systems, hydrodynamics, infrastructure, structural | 5 |
| `AnalysisType` | hydrodynamics, solvers, visualization, workflows | 4 |
| `BatchProcessor` | hydrodynamics, signal_processing, solvers, structural | 4 |
| `ConfigurationManager` | data_systems, signal_processing, solvers, structural | 4 |
| `EnvironmentalConditions` | hydrodynamics, marine_ops, subsea, visualization | 4 |
| `MaterialProperties` | asset_integrity, infrastructure, structural, subsea | 4 |
| `PipeSizing` | asset_integrity, specialized, structural, subsea | 4 |
| `ValidationError` | hydrodynamics, marine_ops, solvers, workflows | 4 |

### Proposed approach

1. **Namespace prefixing**: Rename symbols to include domain (e.g. `HydroAnalysisType`, `SolverAnalysisType`)
2. **Shared base classes**: Extract common abstractions (`ConfigurationManager`, `BatchProcessor`, `DataValidator`) to a `common` or `core` package
3. **Convention enforcement**: `main` and `cli` entry points should be prefixed with package name
4. **`__all__` audit**: Ensure only intentional exports appear in each package

### Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Top 20 collisions reduced to under 10
- [ ] No new collisions introduced
- [ ] Re-run `--detect-collisions` to verify

## Context
`scripts/solver/process-queue.py` moves failed jobs to `queue/failed/` permanently.
There is no retry mechanism for transient failures (license timeout, network glitch).

## Work needed
1. **Add retry_count field to job YAML schema** — track how many times a job has been retried
2. **Implement retry logic in process-queue.py** — on failure, if retry_count < max_retries (default 3), move job back to pending with incremented count and a backoff delay
3. **Exponential backoff** — 5min, 15min, 45min between retries
4. **Dead letter queue** — after max_retries exhausted, move to `queue/dead-letter/` (not just failed/)
5. **Tests** — add retry scenarios to `tests/solver/`

## Acceptance criteria
- Transient failures auto-retry up to 3 times
- Permanent failures go to dead-letter queue
- queue-health.sh reports dead-letter count
- All existing 44 solver tests still pass

## References
- Parent: #1586
- Sprint: #1628
title: Solver queue: retry logic for failed jobs + exponential backoff

body:
## Problem

Multiple test suites across digitalmodel fail at collection time due to missing Python dependencies. These are not test failures — the tests never run because imports fail.

## Affected Test Suites & Missing Modules

| Test Directory | Missing Module | Error |
|---------------|---------------|-------|
| tests/structural/pipe_capacity/ | `pint` | ModuleNotFoundError |
| tests/structural/analysis/wall_thickness_codes/ | `pint` (via digitalmodel.units) | ModuleNotFoundError |
| tests/structural/fatigue/ | numpy.trapz removed | AttributeError (numpy API change) |
| tests/subsea/ (all 7 test files) | `plotly` (via assetutilities.common.validation) | ModuleNotFoundError |
| tests/asset_integrity/ (all 6 test files) | `deepdiff` | ModuleNotFoundError |

## Root Cause

These packages are likely missing from the project's `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies, or the test environment was set up without the full optional dependency set.

## Proposed Fix

1. Add `pint`, `plotly`, and `deepdiff` to `[project.optional-dependencies]` or `[tool.uv.dev-dependencies]`
2. Fix `numpy.trapz` → `numpy.trapezoid` (renamed in NumPy 2.0)
3. Run `uv sync` and verify all test suites collect successfully

## Impact

~21 test files across 3 packages are completely blocked from running. This represents a significant gap in CI coverage.
title: Fix broken test dependencies — pint, plotly, deepdiff missing from dev environment

## Context
Overnight run 2026-04-01 (#1596) delivered a parametric spec.yml generator at `digitalmodel/src/digitalmodel/solvers/parametric_spec_generator.py` with frequency, heading, and hull parameter sweeps. Current limitations:
- Cross-product of sweeps produces N*M specs (can explode)
- No CLI interface for batch generation
- No spec naming convention based on sweep parameters

## What to do
1. Add a `max_specs` guard to prevent accidental generation of >1000 specs
2. Add a CLI entry point: `uv run python -m digitalmodel.solvers.parametric_spec_generator --base spec.yml --freq-sweep 0.1:2.0:5 --output-dir output/`
3. Add naming convention: `spec_{vessel}_{sweep_dim}_{value}.yml`
4. Add combined sweep mode (frequency x heading = cartesian product)
5. Tests for each new feature
6. Update docstrings

## Acceptance criteria
- CLI works: `uv run python -m digitalmodel.solvers.parametric_spec_generator --help`
- max_specs guard raises ValueError when exceeded
- Naming convention applied to output files
- All existing 14 tests + new tests pass

## Parent
Follows from #1596
title: Parametric spec generator: multi-dimensional sweep combinations and CLI interface

### wavespectra (High)

- Why: Wave spectra analysis — directly relevant to OrcaWave input preparation
- Action: pip install wavespectra

## Context

The architecture scanner (#1569) discovers packages, counts classes/functions, and detects entry points. The next evolution is to also discover the **API surface** (public functions/classes per package) and the **internal import graph** (which packages import which).

### Deliverables
1. Extend `repo_architecture_scanner.py` to extract:
   - Public API surface: classes/functions exported via `__all__` or not prefixed with `_`
   - Internal imports: `from digitalmodel.X import Y` patterns across all files
2. Generate a **Mermaid dependency graph** showing which packages depend on which
3. Identify **circular dependencies** (if any)
4. Identify **orphan packages** (imported by nobody)
5. Output: `docs/architecture/{repo}-dependency-graph.md`

### Why this matters
- Enables safe refactoring (know what breaks if you change a package)
- Identifies coupling hotspots (packages imported by many others)
- Feeds into the cross-repo dependency verification (#1574)

### Parent
- #1567 (Continuous Repo Architecture Intelligence)

## Deliverables

1. **Hull registry** — `data/hull_library/hull_registry.yaml` — single source of truth listing all hull forms with principal dimensions (L, B, T, Cb, mass), mesh file paths, and RAO database entries.

2. **5–10 standard hull profiles** as HullProfile YAML files in `data/hull_library/profiles/`:
   - Generic barge (L=100m, B=30m, T=6m)
   - Generic tanker/FPSO (L=250m, B=45m, T=16m)
   - Semi-submersible (column + pontoon geometry)
   - Spar (cylindrical hull, deep draft)
   - Supply vessel (~80-100m)
   - Optional: VLCC, container ship, jack-up

3. **Validation** — Each profile generates a valid PanelMesh via HullMeshGenerator, exports to GDF, and passes mesh quality checks.

## Why this matters

Without seed data, the entire parametric sweep pipeline (#1588) has nothing to sweep over. The hull_library code works — it just needs hull data to operate on.

### E) Memories policy
- `MEMORY.md` and `USER.md` are personal/accumulated — do NOT sync via repo
- They build naturally per machine as Hermes is used

body:
## Context
After OS update on ace-linux-2, the KVM display (NVIDIA T400 + EDID drop) and VNC access likely need re-validation and re-application of the fix.

## Root Cause (WRK-307)
NVIDIA T400 loses EDID signal when KVM switches away. Wayland makes it worse. X11 + forced EDID config is the workaround. OS updates can reset:
- `/etc/gdm3/custom.conf` (re-enable Wayland)
- Kernel DRM card numbering (`card2-DP-3` may change)
- NVIDIA driver DFP-N mappings
- Stale EDID in `/etc/X11/edid.bin`

## Existing Scripts
- `scripts/operations/system/fix-kvm-display-ace-linux-2.sh`
- `scripts/operations/connection/vnc-ace-linux-2.sh`

## Steps
1. SSH verify: `ssh vamsee@ace-linux-2 'echo ok'`
2. Check Wayland state: `grep WaylandEnable /etc/gdm3/custom.conf`
3. Check NVIDIA driver: `nvidia-smi`
4. Check DRM card numbering: `ls /sys/class/drm/ | grep DP`
5. Re-run display fix: `bash scripts/operations/system/fix-kvm-display-ace-linux-2.sh`
6. If card numbering changed, update the fix script + re-capture EDID
7. Verify KVM display works after switching away and back
8. Verify VNC: `bash scripts/operations/connection/vnc-ace-linux-2.sh`
9. Permanent fix: DP EDID emulator dongle (~$10)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] KVM display works (switch away and back without loss)
- [ ] Wayland confirmed disabled in GDM
- [ ] EDID captured and Xorg config applied
- [ ] DRM card mapping verified and script updated if changed
- [ ] VNC connection works from dev-primary
title: Post-update display/VNC recovery on ace-linux-2

body:
## Feature: Continuous Repo Architecture Intelligence

### Problem

The workspace-hub ecosystem has extensive architecture documentation (25 repos mapped, 110 domain directories, 1,594 source files, 830 test files across digitalmodel alone). However:

1. **No automated staleness detection** — docs like CAPABILITIES_SUMMARY.md (Oct 2025), SKILLS_INDEX.md (Dec 2025), and TIER2_REPOSITORY_INDEX.md (Jan 2026) drift silently
2. **No per-repo code architecture summaries** — capability maps track *what standards are implemented* but not *how the code is structured* (classes, modules, entry points, API surfaces)
3. **No consolidated module status view** — no single artifact showing: across all repos, N modules production-ready, M in development, P gaps
4. **No cross-repo test health dashboard** — Phase 1e data is 3 months stale (491 tests at Jan 2026)
5. **No automated cross-repo dependency verification** — dependency graphs are hand-maintained
6. **No domain-specific capability roadmaps** — e.g., OrcaWave/OrcaFlex capabilities vs gaps vs planned work
7. **No continuous learning loop** — architecture understanding degrades between audits

### What exists (leverage, do not rebuild)

| Asset | Path | Status |
|-------|------|--------|
| Repository overview (25 repos) | docs/WORKSPACE_HUB_REPOSITORY_OVERVIEW.md | Current (2026-03-31) |
| File structure taxonomy | docs/standards/FILE_STRUCTURE_TAXONOMY.md | Current |
| Control plane contract | docs/standards/CONTROL_PLANE_CONTRACT.md | Current |
| Engineering capability map | docs/research/engineering-capability-map.md | Moderate (2026-02-24) |
| Per-repo capability YAMLs | .planning/archive/capability-map/*.yaml | Moderate |
| Data intelligence map | docs/document-intelligence/data-intelligence-map.md | Current |
| Domain coverage report | docs/document-intelligence/domain-coverage.md | Current |
| Vision + autonomy tiers | docs/vision/VISION.md | Current |
| Agent vision blueprint | .planning/architecture/agent-vision.md | Moderate |
| CAPABILITIES_SUMMARY | docs/WORKSPACE_HUB_CAPABILITIES_SUMMARY.md | **STALE** (Oct 2025) |
| SKILLS_INDEX | docs/SKILLS_INDEX.md | **STALE** (Dec 2025) |
| TIER2 index | docs/modules/tiers/TIER2_REPOSITORY_INDEX.md | **STALE** (Jan 2026) |

### Prior art (completed issues to reference, not redo)

- #312 Capability map (CLOSED)
- #444 Standards-to-Module Capability Map (CLOSED)
- #1100 Skills knowledge graph (CLOSED)
- #1533 File structure taxonomy (CLOSED)
- #1125 Repo-structure skill (CLOSED)
- #1532 Control-plane contract (CLOSED)
- #1547 Skills ecosystem improvement (CLOSED)

### digitalmodel scale (why automation matters)

- 1,594 Python source files across 31 packages
- 830 test files (612 test_* files)
- 110 domain directories in docs/domains/
- 12+ integrated solvers (OrcaFlex, OrcaWave, AQWA, CalculiX, OpenFOAM, Gmsh, FreeCAD, Blender, Capytaine, BEMRosetta, ANSYS, WAMIT)
- Manual tracking cannot keep up — need automated discovery

### Sub-features (child issues)

1. Automated doc staleness scanner + dashboard
2. Per-repo code architecture discovery (module map, entry points, API surface)
3. Consolidated cross-repo module status view (implemented vs planned vs gap)
4. Cross-repo test health dashboard (current pass/fail, coverage trends)
5. Domain-specific capability roadmaps (OrcaWave/OrcaFlex, structural, pipeline, etc.)
6. Refresh stale docs (CAPABILITIES_SUMMARY, SKILLS_INDEX, TIER2_REPOSITORY_INDEX)
7. Automated cross-repo dependency verification

### Related open issues

- #1563 (Data Intelligence umbrella)
- #1545 (Agentic feature progression)
- #113 (3-6 month roadmap)
- #1426 (Correction-to-skill promotion pipeline)

body:
## Context

Workspace-hub already has a workflow framework: GSD. The goal is not to replace that framework, but to improve repo ecosystem productivity by adding stronger agentic capabilities around the existing control plane.

Prior decision points:
- #1467 evaluated Hermes and concluded: extract useful patterns rather than blindly adopting Hermes as a new framework.
- The current harness architecture in `docs/modules/ai/MINIMAL_HARNESS_ARCHITECTURE_2026-03.md` explicitly says to keep GSD as the workflow and avoid building a parallel framework.
- Hermes remains relevant as one possible sidecar/adapter for messaging, memory, provider coverage, routing, scheduling, and multi-machine execution patterns.

This issue is the umbrella progression for improving agentic repo-ecosystem productivity while keeping GSD as the control plane.

Related enabler:
- #1546 expands machine targeting in the work-item issue form so multi-machine and `ace-linux-*` work can be tracked correctly.

Out of scope for the first pass:
- Replacing GSD
- Creating a second unmanaged workflow framework
- Broad home-automation rollout before repo-ecosystem value is proven

## Objective

Use the existing GSD framework to enhance repo ecosystem productivity with agentic capabilities across planning, routing, research, maintenance, scheduling, and selective execution.

Hermes is one candidate path within that progression, but not the only acceptable implementation path.

## Problem

Today the repo ecosystem has useful but fragmented agent surfaces:
- Claude, Codex, and Gemini are documented and actively used
- GSD provides the workflow discipline
- machine targeting and multi-machine tracking are still incomplete in issue intake
- additional agentic capabilities such as messaging gateways, memory, scheduling, and broader provider utilization are not yet integrated coherently

That leaves productivity on the table across:
- cross-repo coordination
- multi-machine execution
- provider/account utilization
- unattended scheduled work
- remote/mobile interaction patterns
- routing tasks to the best tool/provider for the job

## Success Criteria

- GSD remains the explicit workflow/control plane
- Agentic enhancements improve productivity without weakening review gates or control-plane clarity
- The repo ecosystem gains a clear operating model for multi-provider and multi-machine work
- Hermes or equivalent sidecar capabilities are evaluated based on measured fit, not novelty
- Work is phaseable, testable, and reversible

## Decision Principles

- Keep GSD as the framework; add adapters/sidecars only where they increase leverage
- Prefer incremental integration over framework replacement
- Optimize first for repo-ecosystem productivity, not general-purpose home automation
- Preserve review routing, test discipline, and current control-plane contract
- Use explicit machine/work-queue metadata so future agentic work is trackable

## Phased Task Plan

### Phase 0: Governance and tracking hygiene
- [ ] Use #1546 to fix work-item machine targeting for `ace-linux-*` and multi-machine scope
- [ ] Align machine naming across issue templates, labels, and workstation policy/docs
- [ ] Define how agentic initiatives are represented in WRK/GSD planning artifacts

### Phase 1: Current-state inventory
- [ ] Inventory current provider surfaces, accounts, adapters, and workflow entry points across workspace-hub
- [ ] Inventory current workstation roles and where agentic execution can safely occur
- [ ] Identify the highest-friction repo-ecosystem workflows that would benefit from agentic augmentation

### Phase 2: Hermes fit assessment inside GSD
- [ ] Reproduce Hermes current local failure and determine the minimum path to a runnable state
- [ ] Evaluate Hermes capabilities relevant to repo work: provider coverage, messaging gateway, scheduling, memory, SSH/multi-machine support
- [ ] Compare Hermes against existing GSD-native or lightweight adapter alternatives
- [ ] Decide posture: extract patterns only, sidecar adapter, or limited first-class integration

### Phase 3: Operating model design
- [ ] Define a routing matrix: task type -> preferred provider/account/tool
- [ ] Define a multi-machine operating model for agentic work under GSD
- [ ] Define how remote/mobile interaction should work without bypassing repo controls
- [ ] Document security, secrets, auth, quota, and maintenance constraints

### Phase 4: Pilot integrations
- [ ] Select 1-2 narrow, high-value pilots for repo-ecosystem productivity
- [ ] Example pilot areas: scheduled research/reporting, cross-repo triage, remote routing/notification, provider coverage gap filling
- [ ] Add tests/docs/review evidence for each pilot before any broader rollout

### Phase 5: Architecture decision
- [ ] Produce an explicit go/no-go decision for Hermes in this ecosystem
- [ ] If go: define phased implementation with rollback path
- [ ] If no-go: capture the patterns worth porting into the current GSD stack

## Deliverables

- Agentic productivity progression plan for workspace-hub
- Machine/work-queue tracking alignment via #1546
- Provider and machine capability inventory
- Hermes fit assessment within the GSD control plane
- Routing and operating model for multi-provider, multi-machine work
- Pilot shortlist with acceptance criteria
- Final architecture recommendation

## Notes

- Follow-up to #1467, but broadened from Hermes-only follow-up into agentic feature progression under GSD
- #1546 is a prerequisite/enabler, not a duplicate
- Any future home-device or home-automation use should be treated as a later extension after repo-ecosystem value is demonstrated

title: Agentic feature progression for repo ecosystem productivity under GSD

## Summary

Follow-up to #1537. The cross-review gate scripts and Claude hook are deployed. Need to verify over 3-5 real sessions that adversarial review enforcement is actually firing and working correctly.

## Verification checklist

- [ ] Session 1: Attempt `gh pr create` — confirm gate fires, requires codex/gemini evidence
- [ ] Session 2: Attempt `gsd-execute-phase` — confirm plan review gate blocks without REVIEWS.md
- [ ] Session 3: Attempt `gsd-ship` — confirm verify-artifacts gate checks code + TDD + artifacts
- [ ] Session 4: Run full GSD cycle (plan → review → execute → ship) — confirm no false blocks
- [ ] Session 5: Confirm `--strict` mode escalates warnings to hard blocks

## What to watch for

- False positives: gates blocking when review evidence exists
- False negatives: gates passing without adversarial provider (self-review slipping through)
- Hook timeout: 15s may be tight if `.planning/` is large
- Edge cases: quick-mode tasks, non-GSD manual PRs, worktree sessions

## Resolution criteria

Close when 3+ sessions confirm all three gates fire correctly with no false positives/negatives.

## References

- Enforcement commit: e2215faa
- Gate scripts: `scripts/enforcement/require-{cross-review,plan-review,verify-artifacts}.sh`
- Hook: `.claude/hooks/cross-review-gate.sh`
- Policy: `docs/modules/ai/CROSS_REVIEW_POLICY.md`
- Parent: #1537
title: Verify cross-review adversarial enforcement across sessions

body:
## Objective
Migrate cron jobs from local crontab to Claude-powered scheduled execution for self-healing and higher success rates. Deploy on ace-linux-1 first, then roll out to other ecosystem machines.

## Current State
15 unique cron jobs on ace-linux-1 (plus duplicates to clean up). See full inventory below.

## Decision Needed: Execution Model
Need to evaluate cost/benefit before proceeding. Three options:

### Option A: Remote Triggers (Anthropic Cloud)
- Runs in Anthropic's cloud with fresh git checkout
- **Pro:** Fully managed, no local dependencies
- **Con:** No local machine access (can't rsync, delete local logs, sync repos across LAN)
- **Con:** Consumes API credits per run — cost scales with frequency
- **Suitable for:** 12 of 15 jobs (repo-based analysis, code quality, research)

### Option B: Local cron → Claude Code CLI wrapper
- Keep cron as scheduler, replace bash with `claude` CLI invocations
- **Pro:** Full local access, AI-powered error handling and self-healing
- **Con:** Requires Claude Code running locally, consumes API credits
- **Suitable for:** All 15 jobs including local-only ones

### Option C: Hybrid (A + B)
- Remote triggers for repo-level work, local CLI wrapper for machine-specific tasks
- **Pro:** Best of both worlds
- **Con:** Two systems to maintain, highest total cost

## Cost Analysis TODO
- [ ] Estimate API credit usage per job per month (each option)
- [ ] Compare against current zero-cost bash cron
- [ ] Determine which jobs actually benefit from AI self-healing vs. simple bash
- [ ] Evaluate if a subset (high-failure jobs only) makes more sense

## Cron Job Inventory (ace-linux-1)

### Migratable to remote triggers (12 jobs)
| Schedule | Job | Purpose |
|----------|-----|---------|
| 01:00 daily | dep-health.sh | Dependency health check |
| 01:30 daily | run-benchmarks.sh | Benchmarks |
| 01:35 daily | gsd-researcher-nightly.sh | GSD researcher |
| 02:00 daily | comprehensive-learning-nightly.sh | Learning reports |
| 02:30 daily | check_doc_drift.py | Doc drift detection |
| 02:30 daily | generate-agent-radar.py | Agent radar |
| 03:00 daily | session-analysis.sh | Session analysis |
| 03:15 Sun | ai-tools-status.sh | AI tools status (weekly) |
| 03:30 Sun | update-model-ids.sh | Model ID updates (weekly) |
| 03:30 Mon | harness-lean-out.sh | Harness lean-out (weekly) |
| 04:00 Mon | skills-curation.sh | Skills curation (weekly) |
| 06:00 daily | daily_today.sh | Daily tip generation |

### Local-only (3 jobs)
| Schedule | Job | Why local |
|----------|-----|-----------|
| every 4h | cron-repository-sync.sh | LAN repo sync |
| 04:30 daily | log cleanup (find -delete) | Local filesystem |
| 05:00 daily | rsync backup | SSH to ace-linux-2 |

### Duplicates to clean up
- Log cleanup (04:30) appears 3x in crontab
- rsync backup (05:00) appears 3x in crontab

## Prerequisites
- [ ] `/web-setup` for GitHub auth on remote triggers (if Option A or C)
- [ ] Cost estimate from Anthropic usage dashboard
- [ ] Claude Desktop installed on target machines (#1504)

## Target Date
May 2026 — revisit after cost analysis

## Related
- #1504 — Install Claude Desktop on all ecosystem machines
title: Migrate cron jobs to Claude Desktop scheduled agents for self-healing execution

## Type
research

## Priority
medium

## Complexity
simple

## Route
A

## Category
harness

## Subcategory
multi-ai-workflow

## Machine
dev-primary

## Description

We have 2x ChatGPT Plus ($20/mo each) accounts that are underutilized for Codex CLI workloads. With the cross-plan and cross-review infrastructure now shipped (#1501), Codex is a primary agent for 6/11 task types but we're not tracking or optimizing credit consumption.

### Areas to investigate

1. **Credit usage tracking** — Codex uses a 5-hour rolling credit window. A single Plus-tier prompt eats ~7% of weekly limits (per community reports). Measure actual usage per cross-plan/cross-review invocation to understand capacity.

2. **Dual-account strategy** — Second account could run on a different machine (e.g., `licensed-win-1` or `dev-secondary`) to avoid rate limit contention during parallel dispatch. Evaluate whether `cross-plan.sh` can route to account-2 when account-1 is throttled.

3. **Codex plugins evaluation** — 20+ plugins now available (Gmail, Slack, Google Drive, Figma, Notion, GitHub, Linear, Sentry, Hugging Face). Assess which would add value to existing workflows vs. noise. Low priority — current workflows use Codex for code reasoning, not external service access.

4. **Rate limit monitoring** — 2x rate limits are available as a current promo. Add monitoring to detect when limits are hit during cross-plan/cross-review and log throttle events for capacity planning.

5. **Model selection** — Codex supports GPT-5.1-Codex (Max default, Mini optional). Evaluate whether Mini is sufficient for cross-review (lower credit cost) while Max is reserved for cross-planning (needs higher reasoning).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Measured actual credit consumption per cross-plan and cross-review invocation
- [ ] Documented dual-account setup (which machine, how to configure)
- [ ] Decision on Codex plugins (install list or "none needed" with rationale)
- [ ] Rate limit detection added to cross-plan.sh and cross-review workflows
- [ ] Model tier recommendation (Max vs Mini per task type)

## Target Repos
workspace-hub
title: [WRK] Maximize OpenAI Codex account utilization — dual 0 Plus accounts strategy

## Context
sectionproperties #1452 evaluation covered homogeneous steel only. Offshore structures frequently use composite sections: concrete-filled tubulars (CFT), grouted connections, steel-concrete composite beams.

## Scope
- PoC: concrete-filled CHS (steel outer + concrete infill) using sectionproperties `Material` and `CompoundGeometry`
- PoC: grouted pile-sleeve connection (steel sleeve + grout annulus + steel pile)
- Validate effective properties (EA, EI) against hand calculations
- Assess API ergonomics for composite workflow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CFT section with steel + concrete materials analyzed
- [ ] Grouted connection cross-section analyzed
- [ ] Effective stiffness values validated against analytical formulas
- [ ] Stress distribution plot across materials

## References
- sectionproperties evaluation: #1452
- API note: composite sections require `get_eic()`, `get_ej()` instead of `get_ic()`, `get_j()`
title: Composite/multi-material section analysis PoC with sectionproperties

## Scope

body:
## Context
sectionproperties (#1452 evaluation) has no built-in AISC shapes database — users must supply dimensions manually via `i_section(d, b, t_f, t_w, r, n_r)`. This is error-prone and tedious for standard shapes.

## Scope
- Evaluate `steelpy` (has `aisc.W_shapes.W14X90` lookup) or create a YAML/JSON AISC shapes catalog
- Build a thin wrapper: `get_section("W14x90")` → returns sectionproperties `Geometry` with correct dimensions
- Cover W, S, HP, C, L, HSS, Pipe shapes from AISC Steel Construction Manual 16th Ed.
- Include metric equivalents (W360x134 → W14x90)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Name-to-geometry lookup for all AISC W-shapes
- [ ] Validated against AISC manual for 5+ representative sections
- [ ] Works with sectionproperties 3.10.x

## References
- sectionproperties evaluation: #1452
- steelpy: https://pypi.org/project/steelpy/
title: Add AISC shapes database lookup for sectionproperties

body:
### Mission

Install and configure a terminal multiplexer (tmux, or the winner from the Zellij evaluation in #88) consistently across all workstation machines so every machine has persistent sessions, pane/window management, and session recovery.

### What

- **Choose multiplexer:** tmux is the default unless #88 concludes Zellij is better — decision gates on #88 outcome
- **Install on all Linux machines** (dev-primary, dev-secondary) via apt/package manager
- **Install on macOS** (macbook-portable) via Homebrew
- **Evaluate Windows options** (home-win, licensed-win-1): tmux via WSL, or Windows Terminal native panes if multiplexer not viable on Git Bash
- **Create a shared config file** (e.g. `.tmux.conf`) versioned in workspace-hub or dotfiles, deployed via setup script
- **Key config:** sensible prefix key, mouse support, status bar with session/window info, copy-mode vi bindings, 256-color support
- **Add install steps** to new-machine-setup documentation (see #137)
- **Test Claude Code / AI agent session persistence** through the multiplexer

### Why

Long-running AI agent sessions, build processes, and multi-repo workflows benefit from persistent terminal sessions that survive SSH disconnects and accidental terminal closures. A shared config ensures muscle memory works identically on every machine.

### Related Issues

- **Depends on / gates on:** #88 (Zellij evaluation — if Zellij wins, adopt that instead of tmux)
- **Related:** #12 (consistent terminal experience — multiplexer is a key piece of terminal consistency)
- **Related:** #137 (new-machine-setup — multiplexer install should be added to onboarding steps)
- **Related:** #87 (cross-machine deployment patterns)

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Multiplexer chosen (tmux or Zellij per #88 outcome)
- [ ] Installed on dev-primary and dev-secondary
- [ ] Installed on macbook-portable
- [ ] Windows strategy documented (WSL tmux, or alternative)
- [ ] Shared config file committed to repo
- [ ] Config deployed on all Linux and macOS machines
- [ ] new-machine-setup docs updated with install steps
- [ ] AI agent session persistence verified (detach/reattach with session intact)
title: Install and configure tmux (or chosen multiplexer) across all workstation machines

body:
## Summary
Install and configure Capytaine — open-source Python BEM solver for wave-body interaction — as the first OSS integration from the engineering software catalog (#1397).

## Rationale
- **Replaces/validates AQWA**: Does the same job (added mass, radiation damping, wave excitation forces for floating structures) but is free and open-source
- **Python-native**: `pip install capytaine`, outputs xarray Datasets — fits directly into ACE data stack
- **Standard conventions**: Uses rad/s ascending (industry standard), unlike OrcFxAPI's Hz descending (RES-001)
- **Gmsh integration built-in**: Mesh generation → BEM solve in one Python pipeline
- **Ranked #1**: Doc-intelligence integration priority across all 57 cataloged libraries
- **Cross-validation**: Run same geometry through AQWA + Capytaine, compare RAOs — open-source validation of commercial results
- **NREL-funded since 2022**: Active development, published in JOSS, strong community

## Context
- **Source**: cloned to `/mnt/ace/capytaine` (4.2 MB)
- **Package**: `pip install capytaine` (PyPI, conda-forge)
- **Dependencies**: numpy, scipy, pandas, xarray, rich
- **License**: GPL-3.0
- **Key classes**: FloatingBody, BEMSolver, DiffractionProblem, RadiationProblem

## Tasks
- [ ] Create virtualenv or install into digitalmodel's environment
- [ ] `pip install capytaine` with optional deps (matplotlib, vtk, meshio, gmsh)
- [ ] Verify installation: run a minimal BEM solve (e.g., sphere in waves)
- [ ] Document environment setup in digitalmodel docs
- [ ] Create a thin adapter that converts Capytaine xarray output → digitalmodel RAO format
- [ ] Cross-validate: compare Capytaine vs AQWA results for a simple geometry

## Acceptance Criteria
- `import capytaine` succeeds in the target environment
- A simple diffraction problem runs end-to-end and produces added mass + RAO arrays
- Output format is compatible with existing digitalmodel hydrodynamic coefficient pipeline
- Cross-validation shows <5% difference vs AQWA for a reference geometry

## References
- Catalog: `specs/catalog/open-source-engineering-catalog.yaml`
- Doc-intelligence: `specs/catalog/doc-intelligence-report.yaml` (capytaine section)
- Capytaine docs: https://capytaine.github.io/
- Related knowledge: RES-001 (OrcFxAPI Hz convention), RES-002 (AQWA QPPL DIFF)
- Skills graph: `specs/skills/skills-knowledge-graph.yaml` (hydrodynamics domain)
title: Install Capytaine BEM solver into ACE ecosystem

body:
## Mission
whats-next.sh reads a pre-computed wrk-status-index.json that goes stale when files move between pending/working/done or stages advance. Users see incorrect status. Fix by refreshing only displayed items, not the full index.

## Design
1. **Targeted refresh** — identify top ~20 display candidates from stale index, refresh only those via update-wrk-index.sh before rendering
2. **Parallel refresh** — use subagents/team agents to update multiple WRK entries concurrently
3. **Stage exit hooks** — ensure exit_stage.py and file moves call update-wrk-index.sh
4. **Full rebuild as fallback** — rebuild-wrk-index.sh remains for manual use, not the hot path

## Also fix
- rebuild-wrk-index.sh is O(n) subprocess spawns — needs single-pass Python rewrite
- Current rebuild times out on 750+ items

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] whats-next.sh refreshes displayed items before rendering
- [ ] Refresh is parallel when running with agent capability
- [ ] exit_stage.py calls update-wrk-index.sh after stage transitions
- [ ] File move operations trigger index update
- [ ] Display latency stays under 5 seconds for 20 items
- [ ] Works on all machines (Linux + Windows WSL)
title: whats-next: targeted index refresh before display + parallel agent updates

body:
## Discrepancy

Lifecycle HTML was removed from the workflow, but references to it persist in:

1. **Stage prompt packages** — `stage-4-prompt.md` for workspace-hub-1291 lists `assets/WRK-NNN/WRK-NNN-lifecycle.html` as an exit artifact and includes instructions to "Record plan in lifecycle HTML Stage 4 section" and "Update lifecycle HTML Stage 4 chip to done"
2. **Stage micro-skill** (embedded in prompt) — the checklist mentions "Update lifecycle HTML" as a step
3. **Existing lifecycle HTML files** — e.g., `assets/workspace-hub-1291/WRK-1339-lifecycle.html` still exists with stage chips

## Impact

Agents following stage prompts will attempt to create/update lifecycle HTML files that should no longer exist, wasting tokens and creating confusion when the user intervenes.

## Observed

During workspace-hub-1291 Stage 4 execution, the agent updated the lifecycle HTML chip from "pending" to "done" before the user corrected: "lifecycle html? I thought we deleted the html from the workflow completely"

## Suggested Fix

1. Remove lifecycle HTML references from stage prompt generation (`stage-4-prompt.md` template)
2. Remove lifecycle HTML from stage micro-skill checklists
3. Clean up orphaned `WRK-*-lifecycle.html` files from assets directories
4. Ensure `exit_artifacts` in stage contracts don't reference HTML files

Labels: bug, workflow
title: Lifecycle HTML still referenced in stage prompts and micro-skills

## WRK-1341: slim down large repos — relocate PDFs, reduce .git bloat



**Status:** Stage 1: Capture (in_progress) | **Priority:** high | **Category:** infrastructure | **Subcategory:** operations

**Repo:** ['workspace-hub', 'digitalmodel', 'aceengineer-website'] | **Complexity:** complex | **Machine:** ace-linux-2



---



### Mission

Large repos are causing slow sync times. Three-pronged approach:
1. Remove PDFs from repos and relocate to mounted document-intelligence volume (related WRK exists)
2. Investigate saving large .git folders to a separate branch or using shallow clones (emergency measure)
3. Make all existing repos lean going forward — enforce size limits, add .gitattributes LFS rules or exclusions



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Final Plan</summary>

- [ ] Implement shallow clone or partial clone strategy for daily use

- [ ] Verify sync times improve on all machines

## WRK-1392: 3D interactive frame viewer (HTML/three.js)



**Status:** All stages done | **Priority:** medium | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** visualization

**Repo:** ['workspace-hub', 'digitalmodel'] | **Complexity:** medium | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Generate a browser-based 3D interactive viewer from `FrameGeometry3D` so the frame can be inspected without FreeCAD installed.

### What

1. Create a Python script that reads `FrameGeometry3D` and outputs a self-contained HTML file with three.js
2. Render tubes as cylinders, bends as arc-extruded profiles, connection markers as colored spheres
3. Color-code by assembly (rear trunk = blue, under-chassis = orange)
4. Mark BCs: fixed (red squares), bolted (dark red triangles), origin (gold diamond)
5. Add node labels, dimension annotations, and orbit/zoom controls
6. Include a toggle panel to show/hide assemblies, node labels, and connection markers

### Why

Currently the frame can only be viewed via:
- Static matplotlib PNG (no rotation)
- FreeCAD (requires installation on ace-linux-2)
- GitHub markdown stick figures

An interactive HTML viewer enables:
- Client review without software installation
- Quick geometry verification during iterative refinement
- Embedding in engineering reports (WRK-1368)



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>


**Tests:** Passed: 17, Failed: 0

</details>

<details><summary>Final Plan</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Acceptance Criteria</summary>

- [ ] Single self-contained HTML file (no external dependencies)
- [ ] All 15 nodes, 16 members rendered with correct geometry
- [ ] Bend members shown as curves, not straight lines
- [ ] Orbit, zoom, pan controls
- [ ] Node labels toggleable
- [ ] Assembly color coding matches matplotlib preview
- [ ] File opens in any modern browser

</details>

Not yet available

## WRK-5115: Custom GitHub CLI bash tools for session-cycle efficiency



**Status:** pending | **Priority:** medium | **Category:** engineering | **Subcategory:** tooling

**Repo:** - workspace-hub | **Complexity:** medium | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### What

Create a set of lightweight `gh` API wrapper scripts for quick GitHub posting and reading mid-session — avoiding context switches and verbose `gh` CLI boilerplate during work cycles.

### Why

During sessions we frequently need to check PR status, read/post review comments, create/update issues, and label PRs. Each time this requires composing `gh api` or `gh pr` commands from scratch. Purpose-built wrappers will let us cycle through GitHub interactions rapidly without leaving the session flow.



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Final Plan</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Acceptance Criteria</summary>

- [ ] `gh-pr-status.sh [PR#]` shows state, checks, reviewers in ≤2 lines
- [ ] `gh-pr-comments.sh read [PR#]` fetches latest review comments
- [ ] `gh-pr-comments.sh post [PR#] "message"` posts a comment
- [ ] `gh-issue-quick.sh create "title" --body "desc"` creates an issue
- [ ] `gh-issue-quick.sh read [ISSUE#]` shows issue details
- [ ] `gh-post.sh label [PR#] "label1,label2"` applies labels
- [ ] All scripts work with zero args (default to current repo/branch/PR)
- [ ] `--json` flag on read commands for machine-parseable output
- [ ] Scripts source a shared `scripts/github/_common.sh` for repo detection and auth check

### Mission

Generate a browser-based 3D interactive viewer from `FrameGeometry3D` so the frame can be inspected without FreeCAD installed.

1. Create a Python script that reads `FrameGeometry3D` and outputs a self-contained HTML file with three.js

## WRK-1382: Naval architect expert skill — engineering + legal consultant agent



**Status:** pending | **Priority:** high | **Category:** ai-orchestration | **Subcategory:** expert-agent

**Repo:** [workspace-hub, digitalmodel] | **Complexity:** complex | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Create the definitive naval architecture expert skill — an AI agent that represents the best naval architect who is simultaneously an engineering and legal consultant. This is the capstone of the naval architecture knowledge extraction pipeline. The agent embodies the combined expertise of ~30 textbooks, 110 ship plans, and regulatory standards.

### What

### Engineering Consultant Persona
1. **Design advisor**: hull form selection, powering estimates, stability assessment
2. **Calculation engine**: routes to digitalmodel functions for hydrostatics, resistance, propulsion, seakeeping, stability
3. **Source-traced answers**: every recommendation cites textbook + page + equation number
4. **Trade-off analysis**: articulates engineering trade-offs (e.g., stability vs speed, draft vs displacement)
5. **Historical reference**: 110 ship plans as precedent library — "vessels of similar type used X"

### Legal Consultant Persona
1. **Regulatory compliance**: automated IMO/SOLAS/ABS/DNV checks against vessel parameters
2. **Classification guidance**: which class rules apply, what scantlings are required
3. **Liability awareness**: flags when a design parameter is near regulatory limits
4. **Documentation**: generates survey-ready calculation reports with regulatory references
5. **Jurisdiction mapping**: knows which flag state rules apply to which vessel types

### Knowledge Foundation
- 7,127+ classified records (constants, equations, procedures, definitions, requirements)
- 33 EN400 verified worked examples + 100+ deep-extracted examples
- 3,724 regulatory requirements from SOLAS, ABS, DNV, MCA
- 1,826 engineering constants with units and sources
- 815 equations with derivation context
- Ship-specific hydrostatic tables (DDG-51, FFG-7, etc.)
- GZ curves and stability data

### Skill Architecture
```
.claude/skills/digitalmodel/naval-architect-expert/
├── SKILL.md              # Master skill with persona and routing
├── engineering.md         # Engineering consultant sub-skill
├── legal-regulatory.md    # Legal/regulatory consultant sub-skill
├── knowledge-query.md     # How to search extracted knowledge
└── calculation-routing.md # Which digitalmodel function for which question
```

## WRK-5124: INP writer: validate beam element/section compatibility and auto-reorder B32 connectivity



**Status:** Stage 1: Capture (in_progress) | **Priority:** medium | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** calculix-solver

**Repo:** ['digitalmodel'] | **Complexity:** medium | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Description

WRK-5119 revealed three INP writer gaps:

1. **Element/section compatibility validation** — CalculiX v2.21 rejects CIRC with B31 and PIPE with non-B32R. The INP writer silently produces invalid INP files. Add validation that raises early.

2. **B32/B32R connectivity reordering** — gmsh Line 3 is [start, end, mid] but CalculiX B32 expects [start, mid, end]. INP writer passes through unchanged, forcing callers to pre-reorder.

3. **CIRC dimension semantics** — docstring corrected (radius to diameter) but callers may still pass radius. Document prominently.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] INP writer raises ValueError for CIRC+B31 and PIPE+non-B32R combinations
- [ ] B32/B32R connectivity auto-reordered from gmsh to CalculiX convention
- [ ] Existing tests still pass (fem_bridge.py already uses CalculiX order)
- [ ] Unit tests for validation and reordering logic

## WRK-5095: CFD analysis plan — car + parachute aerodynamics, time-marching deployment



**Status:** pending | **Priority:** high | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** computational-fluid-dynamics

**Repo:** ['digitalmodel'] | **Complexity:** complex | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Plan and scope CFD analysis for the drag car parachute deployment scenario — vehicle aerodynamics, parachute flow interaction, time-marching transient at 250 MPH deployment, and impact load extraction.

### What

Develop a CFD analysis plan addressing four questions:

### Q1: Car Drag, Lift, and Tire Traction Forces

**Existing capability:** `chute_assessment.py` already calculates:
- Aerodynamic lift: `L = 0.5 * rho * V^2 * Cl * A_frontal` (R35 defaults: Cl=0.35, A=25 ft²)
- Tire traction: `N_eff = W - F_lift + F_downforce`, `F_friction = mu * N_eff` (mu=0.8)
- Load cases at 200 and 250 MPH with liftoff risk detection

**Gap:** These use simplified coefficients. CFD would provide spatially resolved pressure distribution, more accurate Cd/Cl at high speed, and ground effect modeling. Plan should define whether simplified coefficients are sufficient or CFD-derived values are needed.

### Q2: Parachute Aerodynamics (Existing Assets)

**Existing capability:** `parachute_drag.py` implements:
- `F = 0.5 * rho * V^2 * Cd * A * Cx` (22 test cases, opening shock Cx=1.2-1.8)
- Single and dual chute configurations
- Stroud sizing chart lookups

**Gap:** Current model is quasi-static (steady drag at a given speed). Does not model:
- Canopy inflation dynamics (squidding → partial → full inflation)
- Wake interaction between dual chutes
- Vehicle wake effect on chute Cd
- Porosity-dependent Cd variation during opening

### Q3: Time-Marching Transient Analysis at 250 MPH Deployment

**Existing capability:**
- OrcaFlex dynamic simulation framework (`execute_dynamic_analysis()` with configurable timestep/duration)
- OpenFOAM infrastructure (domain builder, mesh, post-processing)

**Plan scope:**
- Transient simulation from deployment (t=0, V=250 MPH) through full inflation
- Vehicle deceleration profile: `m * dV/dt = -F_drag_car - F_drag_chute(t)`
- Chute inflation curve: empirical Cx(t) or CFD-derived inflation profile
- Time history of all forces (drag, lift, traction, chute pull)
- Key output: peak force, time-to-peak, deceleration g-load profile

### Q4: Impact Load and Associated Forces

**Existing capability:**
- Opening shock factor Cx in parachute_drag.py (static multiplier)
- Frame solver handles static equivalent loads

**Plan scope:**
- Dynamic amplification factor (DAF) for frame response
- Impulse load calculation: `J = integral(F(t) dt)` over snatch interval
- Peak vs quasi-static force ratio
- Whether frame analysis needs dynamic FEM (transient CalculiX) or static equivalent with DAF suffices



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Final Plan</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Acceptance Criteria</summary>

- [ ] Decision matrix: which approach option(s) to pursue
- [ ] Force time history at 250 MPH deployment (analytical minimum)
- [ ] Peak impact load with dynamic amplification quantified
- [ ] Vehicle deceleration profile (g-load vs time)
- [ ] Assessment: are simplified Cd/Cl sufficient or does CFD add value for this geometry
- [ ] If CFD selected: OpenFOAM case definition (domain, mesh strategy, turbulence model, BCs)

</details>

<details><summary>TDD Results</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Evidence and Cost</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Stage Progress</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

## WRK-1367: Member and connection checks



**Status:** pending | **Priority:** high | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** structural-dynamics

**Repo:** ['workspace-hub'] | **Complexity:** medium | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Perform member capacity and connection design checks — unity ratios for all members, bolts, welds, and pins under governing load cases.

### What

Using forces from the governing analysis model (per WRK-1366 recommendation):

### Member Checks
1. **Axial capacity**: tension yield, compression buckling (Euler/Johnson) for each tube member
2. **Bending capacity**: plastic moment vs applied moment
3. **Combined**: interaction equation (axial + bending) per AISC 360 H1.1 or equivalent
4. **Unity ratios**: demand/capacity for each member, each load case

### Connection Checks
1. **C1 bolt+pin**: bolt shear, bearing on plate, pin bending/shear
2. **B1 bolted (6x)**: bolt group shear, prying action, bearing
3. **Coupler pin**: double-shear capacity, pin bending between strut eyes
4. **Welds (C0, C2, C3)**: fillet weld capacity per load direction (longitudinal/transverse shear)

### Material
- 4130 chromoly: Fy=460 MPa, Fu=560 MPa (normalized condition)
- Weld: assume E70xx filler (matching or overmatching)

### Why

Unity ratios are the deliverable that answers the client's question: is the frame adequate for 200/250 MPH deployment? Any ratio >1.0 identifies members or connections that need reinforcement.



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Final Plan</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Acceptance Criteria</summary>

- [ ] Unity ratios computed for all members under all load cases
- [ ] Unity ratios computed for all connections (C1, B1, coupler, C0, C2, C3)
- [ ] All ratios < 1.0 for assumed dimensions (or deficiencies flagged)
- [ ] Governing member and governing connection identified
- [ ] Checks use forces from both 2D and 3D analyses (extended with 3D FEM per decomposition)
- [ ] Results tabulated in YAML for report integration (WRK-1368)

## WRK-1366: 2D vs 3D comparison



**Status:** pending | **Priority:** high | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** structural-dynamics

**Repo:** ['workspace-hub'] | **Complexity:** medium | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Quantify the simplification error between 2D direct stiffness, 3D CalculiX FEM, and OrcaFlex frame analysis results.

### What

1. **Collect results** from WRK-1363 (2D), WRK-1364 (CalculiX), and WRK-1365 (OrcaFlex)
2. **Compare per member**: axial force, shear, moment — tabulate % difference
3. **Compare reactions** at each BC: magnitude and direction differences
4. **Compare deflections** at chute attachment point and critical locations
5. **Identify** which members show the largest discrepancy and explain why (out-of-plane effects, connection modeling differences, etc.)
6. **Recommend** which model governs for design checks

### Why

The 2D model is faster to iterate but makes simplifying assumptions (planar, rigid connections). Quantifying the error against 3D FEM and OrcaFlex determines whether the 2D model is adequate for design or if 3D results must govern.

**Status:** pending | **Priority:** high | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** structural-dynamics

### Mission

Build a static/quasi-static frame model in OrcaFlex with applied parachute drag force — independent verification of 2D and CalculiX results.

### What

1. **Model frame** as OrcaFlex line objects with correct geometry from WRK-1360
2. **Define section properties**: 4130 chromoly tube (OD/wall from CAD), EA/EI/GJ
3. **Apply boundary conditions**: fixed supports at vehicle mount points (C3, B1)
4. **Apply static drag force** from WRK-1362 at chute attachment point
5. **Run static analysis** for all load cases (200/250 MPH)
6. **Extract**: member forces, reactions, deflections at key points

Execution on **licensed-win-1** (OrcaFlex license required).

### Why

OrcaFlex provides an independent third-party verification of the frame analysis — different solver, different element formulation. Comparing with 2D direct stiffness and CalculiX builds confidence in the results.

## WRK-1363: 2D frame analysis



**Status:** pending | **Priority:** high | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** structural-dynamics

**Repo:** ['workspace-hub'] | **Complexity:** medium | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Extend `frame_solver.py` with the full frame geometry (rear trunk + under-hood) and solve for member forces, reactions, and deflections under parachute drag loading.

### What

The existing `frame_model.py`/`frame_solver.py` models only the **rear trunk frame** (Page 1: horizontal bar + V-struts, 6 nodes, 6 members). This child must:

1. **Add under-hood frame members** (Page 2 geometry): horizontal bar, curved member at 150°, B1 bolted connection (6 bolts), P1 pinned BC
2. **Connect both assemblies** through the vehicle chassis load path (C3 → chassis → B1)
3. **Apply drag force** from WRK-1362 at the chute attachment point (coupler pin)
4. **Solve** using direct stiffness method for all load cases:
   - Case 1: Single chute at 200 MPH
   - Case 2: Single chute at 250 MPH
   - Case 3: Dual chute at 250 MPH (if applicable per WRK-1362 findings)
5. **Extract**: member axial forces, shear, moments; reaction forces at all BCs; deflections

### Why

The current model overestimates fixity at C3 (treats trunk endpoints as fully fixed) because the under-hood frame flexibility is missing. Adding it gives realistic reaction distributions and deflections for the member/connection checks (WRK-1367).



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

- [ ] Node coordinates match WRK-1360 CAD geometry (within 0.25")
- [ ] Member forces and reactions computed for all load cases
- [ ] Equilibrium verified: sum of reactions = applied drag force (within 0.1%)
- [ ] Deflections reported at chute attachment point and all connections
- [ ] Results exported to YAML for comparison (WRK-1366) and checks (WRK-1367)

</details>

### Background

Four discrepancies found during WRK-5104 (#1252) and WRK-5106 (#1254) live sessions,
confirmed again during WRK-5100 (#1257). Deferred from WRK-5100 Section F.

### Scope

1. **Stage summaries missing**: `exit_stage.py` `_post_stage_comment()` posts bare
   "Stage N — DONE" with no content. Enhance to include evidence-based summary.

2. **Approval UX too verbose**: Agent re-asks confirmation after approval already given.
   Approval detection in `wait-for-approval.sh` requires approval *after* AWAITING marker.
   Should also accept approvals that predate the marker within a time window.

3. **No human gate in execute group**: Stages 8-10 run without pause after stage 7
   approval. Evaluate whether a mid-execution human checkpoint is needed.

4. **Confusing author aliases**: All GitHub issue comments appear under `vamseeachanta` —
   no way to distinguish human vs AI agent comments. Add agent attribution prefix to
   bot-posted comments in `update-github-issue.py`.

## WRK-5082: Parachute frame force calculation — drag car parachute deployment



**Status:** Stage 8: Claim (in_progress) | **Priority:** medium | **Category:** engineering-calculations | **Subcategory:** structural-dynamics

**Repo:** ['digitalmodel'] | **Complexity:** complex | **Machine:** dev-primary



---



### Mission

Calculate the force in the parachute frame from the launch/deployment of a parachute on a drag car.

### What

Determine the structural loads on the parachute mounting frame during deployment, using drag car parachute deployment dynamics.

### Why

Reference video (drag car parachute deployment): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IW4TXPrB8Ug&t=25s
Client video (frame walkthrough): https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=shared&v=Rl_mMhHAF0M



---



<details><summary>Implementation Summary</summary>

Not yet available

</details>

<details><summary>Final Plan</summary>

# WRK-5082 Feature Plan — Parachute Frame Force Calculation

## Context

GT1R R35 parachute mounting frame — calculate structural loads during deployment at 200 and 250 MPH. Full engineering deliverable: 3D CAD, 2D drawings, 2D and 3D structural analysis, comparison, and engineering report.

## Existing Implementation (2D analytical — complete)

| Module | Purpose | Tests |
|---|---|---|
| `parachute_drag.py` | F = 0.5*rho*V^2*Cd*A*Cx | 22 |
| `frame_model.py` | 2D stick model + 4130 chromoly | 15 |
| `frame_solver.py` | 2D direct stiffness, 3 DOF/node | 22 |
| `member_check.py` | Von Mises, unity ratios, bolt/weld/pin | 16 |

## Existing Infrastructure (available in digitalmodel)

| Tool | Path | Status |
|---|---|---|
| FreeCAD agent | `.claude/agents/freecad/` | Available |
| CalculiX INP writer | `solvers/calculix/inp_writer.py` | Available (3D solids) |
| CalculiX result parser | `solvers/calculix/result_parser.py` | Available |
| CalculiX FEM chain | `solvers/calculix/fem_chain.py` | Available (plate geometry) |
| OrcaFlex | External — needs integration | TBD |

## Decomposition

| Child key | Child Title | Scope | blocked_by | orchestrator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3D CAD geometry (FreeCAD) | Model frame from hand sketches and photos on dev-secondary | — | claude |
| 2 | 2D engineering drawing | Derive dimensioned 2D views from 3D CAD | 1 | claude |
| 3 | Chute drag force | Drag at 200 and 250 MPH (existing parachute_drag.py) | — | claude |
| 4 | 2D frame analysis | Extend frame_solver with under-hood frame (Page 2 geometry) | 1 | claude |
| 5 | 3D FEM analysis (CalculiX) | Beam FEM with stress distribution and deformation | 1 | claude |
| 6 | OrcaFlex frame analysis | Static frame model with applied drag force on licensed-win-1 | 1 | claude |
| 7 | 2D vs 3D comparison | Quantify simplification between direct stiffness and FEM | 4 | claude |
| 8 | Member and connection checks | Unity ratios and bolt weld pin checks with 3D results | 4 | claude |
| 9 | Pipeline and engineering report | End-to-end script and formatted calc report | 7 | claude |

## Dependency Graph

```
          1 (3D CAD)
         / | \
        2  5  6        3 (Drag)
        |  |  |         |
        |  |  |         4 (2D analysis)
        |  |  |         |
        |  7 ←+---------+
        |  |
        8 ←+
        |
        9 (Report — collects everything)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. 3D FreeCAD model matches hand sketches and photos
2. 2D drawings dimensioned and printable
3. Drag force calculated for 200 MPH and 250 MPH
4. 2D frame analysis: member forces, reactions, unity ratios
5. 3D FEM: stress contours, deformation, reaction forces
6. OrcaFlex: time-domain deployment load history
7. 2D vs 3D comparison: quantified error (%) per member
8. All unity ratios < 1.0 for assumed dimensions
9. Engineering report with all results, drawings, and conclusions

## Key Files

**Existing:** `digitalmodel/src/digitalmodel/structural/parachute/`
**Assets:** `.claude/work-queue/assets/WRK-5082/` (photos, sketches, PDF)
**FreeCAD agent:** `.claude/agents/freecad/`
**CalculiX:** `digitalmodel/src/digitalmodel/solvers/calculix/`

</details>

- [ ] All unity ratios < 1.0 for assumed dimensions
- [ ] Engineering report with all results, drawings, and conclusions

## WRK-300: workstations skill — evolve from registry to multi-machine work distribution

**Status:** archived | **Priority:** medium | **Category:** platform/ops
**Complexity:** unset

---

<details><summary>📋 Plan</summary>


## Goal

Evolve the `workstations` skill from a static registry into an active work-distribution
and task-routing skill that understands software availability per machine.

## Motivation

- ace-linux-2 works against ace-linux-1's workspace-hub (shared mount) — needs
  to know which WRK items are meant for it vs ace-linux-1
- acma-ansys05 is isolated (OrcaFlex/ANSYS license-locked, Windows) — needs a
  separate cron path and should not pull hub-level WRK items blindly
- WRK items already have `computer:` field but nothing enforces or routes based on it
- `comprehensive-learning` (WRK-299) needs machine-aware behavior from this skill

## Scope

### 1. Software capability map per machine

Extend the registry with a `programs:` list per machine:

```yaml
ace-linux-1:
  programs: [python, uv, git, claude-code, worldenergydata, digitalmodel]
  exclusive: []

ace-linux-2:
  programs: [python, uv, git, claude-code]
  exclusive: []
  shares_hub: ace-linux-1   # mounts ace-linux-1 workspace-hub

</details>
title: WRK-300: workstations skill — evolve from registry to multi-machine work distribution

These calculation files are the basis for writing tests in new pipeline modules.
Each project calc (e.g., propagation buckle check in Excel, wall thickness report
in PDF) provides expected inputs and outputs that unit tests can be validated against.
Without this mapping, gap WRK items (WRK-497..503) cannot reference test data.

# =============================================================================
# CALCULATION EXAMPLES — project workbooks and reports that demonstrate each
# standard in practice. These files are used as reference test data when
# implementing gap modules (see gaps list above).
# =============================================================================
calc_examples:
# --- Pipeline domain ---
- standard: DNV-OS-F101
  domain: pipeline
  description: >
    Wall thickness, pressure containment, and installation load checks per DNV OS F101.
    Thinwall FEA project (0116) has list-of-pipelines workbooks with SMYS/SMTS, nominal WT,
    and design pressure. Mecor project (611) has dedicated DNV WT sizing and pipe-check
    workbooks explicitly labelled DNV-OS-F101 covering burst, collapse, and propagation.
  files:
  - path: /mnt/ace/docs/disciplines/production/projects/0116_thinwall_pipe_fea/00_inbox/0116 Thinwall Pipe FEA/CAL/0116-CAL-0001-01 List of Pipelines Installed.xlsx
    description: Pipeline inventory with OD, WT, grade, MAOP — DNV OS F101 Sec 5 wall thickness basis
    type: xlsx
    host: dev-primary
  - path: /mnt/ace/docs/disciplines/production/projects/0116_thinwall_pipe_fea/00_inbox/0116 Thinwall Pipe FEA/CAL/0116-CAL-0001-05 List of Pipelines Installed.xlsx
    description: Updated pipeline list with revised wall thicknesses after FEA
    type: xlsx
    host: dev-primary
  - path: /mnt/ace/docs/disciplines/production/projects/611_mecor_pipeline_installation_analysis/00_inbox/611 Mecor Pipeline Installation Analysis/Analysis/Wall Thickness Sizing/0611-CAL-0003-01 Mecor Pipeline Installation DNV WT Sizing.xlsm
    description: DNV OS F101 wall thickness sizing — burst (SC-High) and collapse (SC-Low) checks
    type: xlsm
    host: dev-primary
  - path: /mnt/ace/docs/disciplines/production/projects/611_mecor_pipeline_installation_analysis/00_inbox/611 Mecor Pipeline Installation Analysis/Analysis/Wall Thickness Sizing/0611-CAL-0004-01 Mecor Pipeline Installation Pipe Checks (Dnv-OS-F101).xlsx
    description: Full DNV-OS-F101 pipe checks — combined loading, installation, operational cases

# OrcaFlex Model Library

# All paths on host: dev-secondary under /mnt/remote/dev-secondary/dde/Orcaflex/
# =============================================================================
orcaflex_models:

- project: drilling-riser-development
  path: /mnt/remote/dev-secondary/dde/Orcaflex/0000 Drilling Riser Development/Latest
  description: >
    Drilling riser model library — vessel offset sensitivity, current profiles,
    wave loading cases (Rev8: 2,282 load cases). Includes Atwood Condor vessel
    specific models and parametric load case sweeps. Key inputs for DNV-OS-F201
    drilling riser design checks (effective tension, bending, VIV screening).
  domain: pipeline
  standards: [DNV-OS-F201, ISO-13624-1]
  host: dev-secondary
  file_count: 3358
  portability: direct
  notes: >
    Rev8/ contains the bulk of load cases (2,282 files). Latest/ has the
    current canonical model (31 files). Vessel Offset* folders are dedicated
    sensitivity runs. Use Latest/ as the base model for porting.

- project: wellhead-bop-fatigue
  path: /mnt/remote/dev-secondary/dde/Orcaflex/31290 Shell WellHead Fatigue Assement
  description: >
    BOP-on-wellhead/tree/THS (Tubing Hanger System) fatigue assessment models.
    594 load cases across three BOP configurations. Provides fatigue damage
    accumulation inputs for API-RP-2A and DNV-OS-E301 wellhead fatigue checks.
    Includes VIV subfolder for vortex-induced vibration screening.
  domain: structural
  standards: [API-RP-2A-WSD-22nd, DNV-OS-E301, DNV-RP-F105]
  host: dev-secondary
  file_count: 594
  portability: direct
  notes: >
    Three BOP configuration folders: BOP on Wellhead (240), BOP on THS (172),
    BOP on Tree (174). For Verification/ has 6 spot-check models.

- project: riserless-completion-analysis
  path: /mnt/remote/dev-secondary/dde/Orcaflex/31242
  description: >
    Riserless completion analysis — inner string model for deepwater well
    completion operations without a marine riser. Reference model for
    DNV-OS-E301 mooring and completion equipment fatigue checks.
  domain: marine
  standards: [DNV-OS-E301]
  host: dev-secondary
  file_count: 1
  portability: direct
  notes: Single model file InnerString.dat — lightweight reference.

- project: orcaflex-api-stress-check
  path: /mnt/remote/dev-secondary/dde/Orcaflex/5 - OrcaFlex API Check
  description: >
    OrcaFlex API stress verification model. Single .dat file used to verify
    the OrcaFlex Python API stress result extraction. Useful as a minimal
    test fixture for the OrcaFlex Python interface.
  domain: marine
  standards: []
  host: dev-secondary
  file_count: 1
  portability: direct
  notes: OrcaFlexAPIStressCheck.dat — minimal model for API testing.
# ── API 579-1 / ASME FFS-1 (added 2026-02-25, WRK-606/607) ──────────────────
- id: API-579-PART4-GML
  title: API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 Part 4 — General Metal Loss (GML)
  org: API
  index_ref: ''
  status: implemented
  domain: structural
  summary: >
    Implemented in digitalmodel.asset_integrity — GML Level 1+2 assessment,
    RSF (Remaining Strength Factor), MAWP re-rating, B31G / modified B31G.
    Modules: asset_integrity.common.API579_components, assessment.level2_engine,
    rsf_calculations. Grid-based UT data ingestion + contour plots.
  keywords: [API 579, GML, general metal loss, RSF, MAWP, fitness-for-service, Part 4]
- id: API-579-PART5-LML
  title: API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 Part 5 — Local Metal Loss (LML)
  org: API
  index_ref: ''
  status: implemented
  domain: structural
  summary: >
    Implemented in digitalmodel.asset_integrity — LML Level 1+2 assessment
    with Folias factor. RSF for localised thin areas, MAWP re-rating.
    Modules: asset_integrity.common.API579_components (API579LML),
    assessment.level2_engine (_evaluate_lml).
  keywords: [API 579, LML, local metal loss, Folias factor, RSF, Part 5]
- id: API-579-PART6-PITTING
  title: API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 Part 6 — Pitting Corrosion
  org: API
  index_ref: ''
  status: gap
  domain: structural
  summary: >
    Not yet implemented. Part 6 covers assessment of pitting damage using pit
    coupling ratios and equivalent thickness methods. No Python module exists.
  keywords: [API 579, pitting corrosion, pit coupling, Part 6, FFS]
- id: API-579-PART7-HIC
  title: API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 Part 7 — HIC/SOHIC/SSC Damage
  org: API
  index_ref: ''
  status: gap
  domain: structural
  summary: >
    Not yet implemented. Part 7 covers hydrogen-induced cracking, step-oriented
    HIC, and stress-oriented SOHIC for wet H2S environments. Critical for
    sour service pipelines and pressure vessels.
  keywords: [API 579, HIC, SOHIC, SSC, hydrogen damage, sour service, Part 7]
- id: API-579-PART8-DENTS
  title: API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 Part 8 — Shell Distortions (Dents, Gouges, Arc Burns)
  org: API
  index_ref: ''
  status: gap
  domain: structural
  summary: >
    Not yet implemented. Part 8 covers assessment of dents, gouges, arc burns,
    and misalignment in pressure-containing components. Relevant for pipeline
    mechanical damage assessment.
  keywords: [API 579, dents, gouges, shell distortion, mechanical damage, Part 8]
